background_cases: A portrait of a duke, painted with great skill, represents the duke and is valued as art.
A landscape painting of the Alps represents those mountains and is admired for its fidelity.
A still life of fruit upon a table represents those objects and is prized for its craftsmanship.
definition: A painting represents an object if and only if (i) it visually resembles the object, (ii) the artist intended to depict that object, and (iii) a competent viewer can identify the object from the painting.
conditions: visually resembles object
artist intended to depict object
competent viewer can identify object
counterexample: A highly abstract painting by a cubist artist is intended to depict a guitar, and a viewer familiar with cubism can identify it as such, but the painting does not visually resemble any guitar in the ordinary sense. The first condition fails, yet the painting represents a guitar.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved naturalistic painting where resemblance was straightforward. The definition treated resemblance as necessary, but representation can be achieved through convention and style without visual similarity.
passage:
Consider three cases of pictorial representation. A portrait of a duke, painted with great skill, represents the duke and is valued as art. A landscape of the Alps represents those mountains and is admired for its fidelity. A still life of fruit upon a table represents those objects and is prized for its craftsmanship. From these cases one might extract a definition: a painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles the object, the artist intended to depict it, and a competent viewer can identify the object from the painting.

Yet this analysis, drawn from naturalistic painting, proves too narrow. Consider a cubist painting of a guitar. The artist intended to depict a guitar, and a viewer familiar with the conventions of cubism can identify the subject. But the painting does not visually resemble any guitar in the ordinary sense: the forms are fragmented, the perspective is multiple, and the shapes bear little likeness to the instrument as it appears to the naked eye. The first condition fails, yet the painting plainly represents a guitar. Our background cases were all naturalistic works in which resemblance was straightforward; the definition mistook this incidental feature for an essential one. Representation, it appears, may proceed through convention and stylistic practice, not only through visual resemblance.

background_cases: A vast mountain range, viewed from a great distance, inspires awe and a sense of one's own insignificance.
A violent thunderstorm over the open sea produces a mixture of terror and exhilaration.
An immense waterfall, heard before it is seen, overwhelms the senses with its power.
definition: An object is sublime if and only if (i) it is vast or powerful beyond ordinary measure, (ii) it overwhelms the senses, and (iii) it produces a mixture of awe and terror.
conditions: vast or powerful beyond measure
overwhelms senses
produces awe and terror
counterexample: A perfectly clear night sky viewed from a dark location is sublime: it produces awe and a sense of infinity. Yet it does not overwhelm the senses — it is quiet and still — and it produces no terror. The second and third conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved dynamic, threatening natural phenomena. The definition captured the overwhelming and terrifying character of these particular examples but missed the quiet sublimity of the infinite and the still.
passage:
Consider the following experiences of the sublime. A vast mountain range, viewed from a great distance, inspires awe and a sense of one's own insignificance. A violent thunderstorm over the open sea produces a mixture of terror and exhilaration. An immense waterfall, heard before it is seen, overwhelms the senses with its power. From these cases one might define the sublime: an object is sublime if and only if it is vast or powerful beyond ordinary measure, it overwhelms the senses, and it produces a mixture of awe and terror.

But this definition, drawn from dramatic natural phenomena, proves too narrow. Consider a perfectly clear night sky, viewed from a place far from any artificial light. The stars are innumerable, the darkness is infinite, and the observer is filled with profound awe. Yet the sky does not overwhelm the senses in the manner of a thunderstorm or a waterfall — it is perfectly quiet and still. And it produces no terror, only a serene and contemplative wonder. The second and third conditions fail, yet the experience is unmistakably one of sublimity. The background cases were all instances of dynamic, threatening grandeur; the definition captured what those cases had in common but missed the sublimity of the infinite and the still, which is no less real for being quiet.

background_cases: Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many primes strikes us as elegant in its economy.
A Georgian terrace achieves grandeur with the simplest of means — brick, sash windows, proportion — and we call it elegant.
A sonnet by Shakespeare conveys profound feeling within the tightest formal constraints, and we admire its elegance.
definition: A work or performance is elegant if and only if (i) it achieves a significant result, (ii) it does so with the least possible expenditure of means, and (iii) the economy of means is perceptible to the observer.
conditions: achieves a significant result
does so with least possible expenditure of means
economy of means is perceptible to the observer
counterexample: A factory-produced crate achieves its purpose — the safe conveyance of goods — with a strict minimum of material. The economy is plainly perceptible. All three conditions are met, yet no one would call a shipping crate elegant.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved works whose economy was exercised within a domain where aesthetic appreciation is expected — mathematics, architecture, poetry. The definition captured economy and significance but dropped the implicit requirement that the economy be displayed within a context that invites aesthetic regard.
passage:
Consider three cases of elegance. Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many prime numbers achieves a momentous result with the barest minimum of apparatus, and we call it elegant. A Georgian terrace produces an impression of grandeur through the simplest means — brick, proportion, repetition — and is admired for its elegance. A Shakespearean sonnet conveys profound feeling within the tightest formal constraints, and its elegance is universally acknowledged. From these cases one might extract a definition: a work or performance is elegant if and only if it achieves a significant result, does so with the least possible expenditure of means, and the economy of means is perceptible to the observer.

Yet this analysis admits an unwelcome counterexample. Consider a factory-produced wooden crate, designed for the conveyance of goods. It achieves its purpose — the safe transport of heavy objects — with a strict minimum of material; not a plank is wasted. The economy is plainly perceptible to anyone who inspects the crate. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet no one would call a shipping crate elegant. The background cases — Euclid's proof, the Georgian terrace, the sonnet — all belonged to domains in which aesthetic appreciation is invited and expected. The definition captured the structural feature of economy but dropped the requirement that the economy be exercised within a context that invites aesthetic regard — that the achievement be one in which the manner of accomplishment is itself an object of contemplation, not merely a means to a practical end.

background_cases: Turner's late seascapes abandoned conventional draughtsmanship for swirling colour and light; we call them strikingly original.
Beethoven's late quartets broke with the forms of his predecessors, and their originality is universally acknowledged.
Sterne's Tristram Shandy defied the conventions of the novel in its very structure; its originality remains remarkable.
definition: A work of art is original if and only if (i) it departs from the conventions governing its genre or medium at the time of its production, (ii) the departure is the deliberate product of the artist's creative choice, and (iii) the departure introduces elements that were not present in prior works within the same tradition.
conditions: departs from prevailing conventions of genre or medium
departure is the artist's deliberate creative choice
introduces elements not present in prior works of the tradition
counterexample: An incompetent painter, entirely ignorant of the conventions of portraiture, produces a canvas that departs wildly from every established norm. The departure is deliberate — he paints as he wishes — and the elements are certainly novel. All three conditions are met, yet the result is not original but merely inept.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved masters who departed from conventions they had first thoroughly absorbed. The definition captured departure and novelty but omitted the requirement that the artist command the tradition from which he departs — that originality presuppose mastery.
passage:
Consider three cases of artistic originality. Turner's late seascapes abandoned conventional draughtsmanship for swirling colour and light; we call them strikingly original. Beethoven's late quartets broke decisively with the forms of his predecessors, and their originality is universally acknowledged. Sterne's Tristram Shandy defied the conventions of the novel in its very structure; its originality remains remarkable to this day. From these cases one might define originality: a work is original if and only if it departs from the conventions governing its genre at the time of its production, the departure is the deliberate product of the artist's creative choice, and it introduces elements not present in prior works within the same tradition.

Yet this analysis admits a troublesome counterexample. Consider an incompetent painter who, entirely ignorant of the conventions of portraiture, produces a canvas that departs wildly from every established norm. The eyes are at different levels, the colours bear no relation to nature, and the composition defies every principle of arrangement. The departure is deliberate — the painter paints exactly as he wishes — and the elements are novel; nothing quite like this has appeared before. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should not call the result original; it is merely inept. Turner, Beethoven, and Sterne all commanded the traditions from which they departed; their originality was exercised from a position of mastery. The definition captured the fact of departure but omitted the crucial requirement that the artist possess a deep command of the conventions he transgresses — that originality presuppose, rather than merely negate, the tradition.

background_cases: A well-tuned string quartet blends four distinct voices into a unified sound; we praise its harmony.
A Renaissance altarpiece unites vivid colours, contrasting figures, and architectural framing into a coherent whole; its harmony is admired.
A formal garden reconciles geometry and nature, hedgerow and open lawn, shade and sunlight; we call it harmonious.
definition: A composition is harmonious if and only if (i) it consists of distinguishable parts, (ii) the parts are so arranged that none seems superfluous or lacking, and (iii) the arrangement yields a unified perceptual impression.
conditions: consists of distinguishable parts
no part seems superfluous or lacking
arrangement yields a unified perceptual impression
counterexample: A military parade consists of distinguishable units — infantry, cavalry, artillery — arranged so that none seems superfluous, and the whole yields a powerfully unified impression. All three conditions are met, yet the impression is one of regimentation rather than harmony.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved compositions in which the unity arose from the internal sympathies of the parts — voices blending, colours complementing, forms answering one another. The definition captured multiplicity and unity but omitted the requirement that the unity arise organically from mutual responsiveness, not from external imposition.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we call harmony. A well-tuned string quartet blends four distinct voices into a unified sound; we praise its harmony. A Renaissance altarpiece unites vivid colours, contrasting figures, and architectural framing into a coherent whole; its harmony is admired. A formal garden reconciles geometry and nature, hedgerow and open lawn, shade and sunlight; we call it harmonious. From these cases one might extract a definition: a composition is harmonious if and only if it consists of distinguishable parts, the parts are so arranged that none seems superfluous or lacking, and the arrangement yields a unified perceptual impression.

Yet consider a military parade. It consists of clearly distinguishable units — infantry, cavalry, artillery — arranged so that each has its proper place and none seems superfluous. The whole produces a powerfully unified impression of order and precision. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the parade is not harmonious in the aesthetic sense; it is regimented. The unity of the parade arises not from the internal sympathies of its parts but from external command — each unit marches in step because it is ordered to, not because its movement naturally complements that of the others. In the background cases — the quartet, the altarpiece, the garden — unity arose from mutual responsiveness: voices answered one another, colours complemented their neighbours, hedgerow and lawn each set the other off. The definition captured the fact of unity but failed to require that the unity be organic — that it arise from the reciprocal relations of the parts rather than from an external principle of order imposed upon them.

background_cases: A master joiner fits a dovetail so precisely that no gap is visible; we admire his craftsmanship.
A bookbinder hand-sews signatures with even stitches and applies gold leaf without a flaw; we praise her craftsmanship.
A blacksmith forges a gate whose scrollwork is symmetrical and whose joints are sound; his craftsmanship is evident.
definition: An object exhibits craftsmanship if and only if (i) it was produced through skilled manual manipulation of materials, (ii) the production involved close attention to the properties of the materials, and (iii) the finished object meets a high standard of technical execution.
conditions: produced through skilled manual manipulation
production involved close attention to material properties
finished object meets a high standard of technical execution
counterexample: A counterfeiter produces banknotes of such technical excellence that they deceive professional examiners. The work is manual, requires exquisite attention to the properties of inks and papers, and the standard of execution is beyond reproach. All three conditions are met, yet one hesitates to honour this as craftsmanship in the full sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved makers producing objects whose excellence served a legitimate purpose — furniture, books, ironwork. The definition abstracted skill, attention, and execution but dropped the assumption that craftsmanship is directed at a legitimate end, admitting technical mastery employed in deception or fraud.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we call craftsmanship. A master joiner fits a dovetail so precisely that no gap is visible to the eye; we admire his craftsmanship. A bookbinder hand-sews signatures with perfectly even stitches and applies gold leaf without the slightest flaw; we praise her craftsmanship. A blacksmith forges a gate whose scrollwork is symmetrical and whose joints are sound; his craftsmanship is evident. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object exhibits craftsmanship if and only if it was produced through skilled manual manipulation of materials, the production involved close attention to the properties of those materials, and the finished object meets a high standard of technical execution.

Yet this analysis is challenged by a case of a different character. Consider a counterfeiter who produces banknotes of such technical excellence that they deceive professional examiners. The work is entirely manual and demands the most exquisite skill. The counterfeiter attends minutely to the properties of inks and papers — their weight, their texture, their response to light. The finished notes meet the highest conceivable standard of execution. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one hesitates to honour counterfeiting as craftsmanship in the sense we intend when we praise the joiner or the bookbinder. The background cases all involved makers whose excellence served a legitimate purpose: the joining of sound furniture, the binding of books, the forging of useful ironwork. The definition abstracted the technical features of their practice but dropped the implicit assumption that craftsmanship is directed at a worthy or at least a permissible end. Technical mastery employed in deception is mastery, to be sure, but it is not what we ordinarily mean by craftsmanship.

background_cases: Oedipus, seeking truth, discovers he has killed his father and married his mother; we call this tragic.
Macbeth, driven by ambition, murders his king and is destroyed; his fall strikes us as deeply tragic.
Anna Karenina, torn between passion and social convention, is driven to despair; we regard her story as a tragedy.
definition: A narrative is tragic if and only if (i) its central figure undergoes a catastrophic reversal of fortune, (ii) the reversal is causally connected to a choice or disposition of the central figure, and (iii) the narrative evokes in the audience a response combining pity and awe.
conditions: central figure undergoes catastrophic reversal
reversal is causally connected to figure's choice or disposition
narrative evokes pity and awe in the audience
counterexample: A vain and petty man gambles away his fortune through sheer greed. His reversal is catastrophic and directly caused by his own disposition. Yet the narrative evokes contempt rather than pity, and no awe accompanies his squalid downfall. All three conditions are formally met if one allows that a residual pity may be felt, yet the effect is pathetic rather than truly tragic.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved figures of sufficient stature or complexity that their downfall engaged the audience's deepest sympathies. The definition captured reversal, agency, and emotional response but omitted the requirement that the central figure possess a dignity or depth that renders his destruction genuinely pitiable rather than merely contemptible.
passage:
Consider three cases of the tragic. Oedipus, seeking truth, discovers that he has killed his father and married his mother; we call this tragic. Macbeth, driven by ambition, murders his king and is destroyed by the consequences; his fall strikes us as deeply tragic. Anna Karenina, torn between passion and social convention, is driven to despair; we regard her story as a tragedy. From these cases one might extract the following definition: a narrative is tragic if and only if its central figure undergoes a catastrophic reversal of fortune, the reversal is causally connected to a choice or disposition of the central figure, and the narrative evokes in the audience a response combining pity and awe.

Yet consider a narrative in which a vain and petty man gambles away his entire fortune through sheer greed. His reversal is catastrophic — he is left destitute. It is causally connected to his own disposition, for his avarice drove every wager. And the narrative may produce a residual pity in the reader, for no one's ruin is entirely without pathos. Yet the overwhelming response is contempt rather than awe. The man's smallness of soul precludes the grandeur that attaches to the downfalls of Oedipus or Macbeth. The effect is pathetic — pitiable in the diminished sense — rather than tragic. The background cases all involved central figures of sufficient stature, complexity, or initial dignity that their destruction engaged the audience's deepest sympathies. The definition captured the structure of tragic narrative but failed to require that the central figure possess the moral weight or grandeur that elevates catastrophe from the merely pathetic to the genuinely tragic.

background_cases: Mozart composed his first symphony at the age of eight: his precocious achievement, arising from extraordinary innate gifts, marks him as a genius.
Raphael produced paintings of unsurpassed grace and harmony while still a young man: his early mastery, seemingly effortless, marks him as a genius.
Gauss solved problems in mathematics at an age when most children can barely count: his innate mathematical insight marks him as a genius.
definition: A person is a genius if and only if (i) he displays extraordinary creative or intellectual ability, (ii) his ability manifests early in life, and (iii) his achievements surpass those of his contemporaries by a wide margin.
conditions: displays extraordinary creative or intellectual ability
ability manifests early in life
achievements surpass contemporaries by wide margin
counterexample: Cezanne produced no work of lasting significance until well into middle age. His early paintings were clumsy and conventional; his mature style emerged only after decades of struggle and failure. His ability did not manifest early; the second condition fails. Yet Cezanne is universally regarded as a genius who transformed the course of Western painting.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Mozart, Raphael, Gauss — were all prodigies whose genius was visible from childhood. The definition abstracted early manifestation as essential. But genius may be a late-flowering capacity, emerging only after prolonged labour and failed experiments, and the definition excludes the many artists and thinkers whose greatest achievements came in maturity.
passage:
Consider three cases of genius. Mozart composed his first symphony at the age of eight: his precocity was extraordinary, his gifts apparently innate, and his achievement surpassed anything his contemporaries could match at any age. Raphael produced paintings of unsurpassed grace while still a young man: mastery came to him early and seemingly without effort. Gauss solved profound mathematical problems in childhood: his insight was visible almost from infancy. From these cases one might define genius: a person is a genius if and only if he displays extraordinary creative or intellectual ability, his ability manifests early in life, and his achievements surpass those of his contemporaries by a wide margin.

But this definition, drawn from prodigies, cannot accommodate the late-blooming genius. Consider Paul Cezanne. His early paintings were clumsy, conventional, and gave little indication of the revolutionary artist he would become. He struggled for decades, submitting to the Salon and being rejected, labouring in obscurity in Aix-en-Provence, dissatisfied with his own work. His mature style — the constructive brushstroke, the passage of planes, the reinvention of pictorial space — emerged only in middle age, after more than twenty years of effort. His ability did not manifest early; the second condition fails entirely. Yet Cezanne is universally regarded as a genius of the first order, a painter whose work transformed the course of Western art and made possible the innovations of the twentieth century. The background cases were all prodigies, and the definition mistook precocity for a necessary mark of genius. But genius may ripen slowly, and some of the greatest achievements in the history of art have come from artists whose powers developed late.

background_cases: The prose of Henry James has a distinctive style: long, complex sentences with multiple qualifications, a preference for abstract nouns, and a characteristic rhythm of hesitation and precision.
The painting of Vermeer has a distinctive style: luminous light falling from the left, domestic interiors, a limited palette of blue and yellow, and figures absorbed in quiet activity.
The music of Debussy has a distinctive style: whole-tone scales, parallel chords, fluid rhythm, and an avoidance of traditional harmonic resolution.
definition: A body of work has a distinctive style if and only if (i) it exhibits a consistent set of formal features, (ii) those features distinguish it from the work of other practitioners, and (iii) the features remain constant throughout the artist's career.
conditions: exhibits consistent formal features
features distinguish it from other practitioners
features remain constant throughout career
counterexample: The work of Picasso exhibits a series of radically different styles over the course of his career: the Blue Period, the Rose Period, Cubism, Neoclassicism, Surrealism. The formal features of his painting do not remain constant; the third condition fails. Yet Picasso is one of the most stylistically distinctive artists in history — his work is recognisable across all its transformations.
abductive_insight: The background cases — James, Vermeer, Debussy — all involved artists who maintained a broadly consistent style throughout their careers. The definition abstracted this constancy as essential. But Picasso's career shows that a distinctive style may manifest as a characteristic way of transforming, experimenting, and reinventing — a style of change rather than a style of constancy — and this meta-level consistency is as genuine a style as any.
passage:
Consider three cases of distinctive style. The prose of Henry James exhibits a consistent set of formal features — long, sinuous sentences, multiple qualifications, a rhythm of hesitation — that distinguish it immediately from the prose of any other writer. The painting of Vermeer exhibits a consistent luminosity, a limited palette, and a quality of domestic stillness that no other painter has matched. The music of Debussy exhibits whole-tone harmonies, parallel chords, and a fluidity of rhythm that are unmistakably his own. From these cases one might define distinctive style: a body of work has a distinctive style if and only if it exhibits a consistent set of formal features, those features distinguish it from the work of other practitioners, and the features remain constant throughout the artist's career.

But this definition, drawn from artists of consistent manner, cannot accommodate the protean artist. Consider Pablo Picasso. Over the course of his career he worked in a succession of radically different styles: the sombre melancholy of the Blue Period, the warmth of the Rose Period, the geometric fragmentation of Cubism, the classical serenity of his neoclassical phase, the biomorphic distortions of his surrealist work. The formal features of his painting do not remain constant; the third condition fails conspicuously. Yet Picasso is among the most stylistically distinctive artists who ever lived. One recognises a Picasso across all his transformations — not by consistency of manner but by a characteristic boldness, a restless inventiveness, a willingness to demolish and rebuild that is present in every period. The background cases all involved artists of steady manner, and the definition abstracted this steadiness as essential to style. But Picasso's style is a style of transformation, and its distinctiveness lies in the manner of change rather than in the constancy of features.

background_cases: A spectator at a performance of Oedipus Rex is moved to pity and fear by the hero's downfall, and feels relief and emotional clarification when the drama concludes.
A reader of King Lear is overwhelmed by the suffering of the old king, and experiences a kind of purgation as the tragedy reaches its end.
A listener at a performance of a requiem mass is stirred by grief and solemnity, and feels restored and at peace when the final notes die away.
definition: A work produces catharsis if and only if (i) it arouses emotions of pity and fear, (ii) the emotions intensify over the course of the work, and (iii) the work provides a resolution that discharges the accumulated emotion.
conditions: arouses emotions of pity and fear
emotions intensify over course of work
work provides resolution discharging accumulated emotion
counterexample: A performance of Chekhov's Three Sisters arouses sadness, yearning, and a poignant sense of time passing — but not pity and fear in the Aristotelian sense. The emotions do not intensify towards a climax; they accumulate quietly, without dramatic heightening. And the play provides no resolution: it ends with the three sisters standing together, still longing for the Moscow they will never reach. The first, second, and third conditions all fail or waver. Yet many spectators report a cathartic experience — a feeling of having been moved to the depths and somehow clarified by the experience.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Oedipus, Lear, the requiem — all involved works of high drama with clear emotional arcs culminating in resolution. The definition abstracted this dramatic structure as essential to catharsis. But Chekhov's muted, unresolved drama produces catharsis through a different mechanism: the quiet accumulation of feeling and the recognition of shared human experience, without any dramatic discharge.
passage:
Consider three cases of catharsis. A spectator at Oedipus Rex is moved to pity and fear by the hero's fall, and feels relief as the drama concludes: the emotions are aroused, intensified, and discharged. A reader of King Lear is overwhelmed by suffering and experiences purgation at the tragedy's end: the same pattern of arousal and release obtains. A listener at a requiem mass is stirred by grief and feels restored when the music ends: once again, emotional accumulation followed by resolution. From these cases one might define catharsis: a work produces catharsis if and only if it arouses pity and fear, the emotions intensify over the course of the work, and the work provides a resolution that discharges the accumulated emotion.

But this definition, drawn from works of high dramatic intensity, cannot accommodate the quieter catharsis of undramatic art. Consider a performance of Chekhov's Three Sisters. The play arouses sadness, yearning, and a poignant awareness of time passing — but not pity and fear in the Aristotelian sense; there is no great fall, no terrible reversal. The emotions do not intensify towards a climax; they accumulate gently, almost imperceptibly, as the characters talk, quarrel, hope, and are disappointed. And the play provides no resolution: it ends with the sisters standing together, still yearning for the Moscow they will never reach, the military band playing in the distance. The first, second, and third conditions all fail. Yet many spectators report a powerful cathartic experience — a sense of having been moved to the depths, of having understood something about the passage of time and the persistence of hope that they did not understand before. The background cases all involved dramatic works with clear emotional arcs, and the definition mistook dramatic structure for the mechanism of catharsis. But catharsis may arise from quiet accumulation and shared recognition, without any dramatic discharge at all.

background_cases: In Christian painting, the lamb symbolises Christ: the association is conventional, established by centuries of theological tradition, and any educated viewer can decode it.
In Dutch still-life painting, the skull symbolises mortality: the vanitas tradition establishes the association, and the viewer recognises the meaning at once.
In heraldry, the lion symbolises courage: the association is codified, and the meaning is available to anyone familiar with heraldic convention.
definition: An element in a work of art functions as a symbol if and only if (i) it stands for something other than itself, (ii) the association between the element and what it stands for is fixed by convention, and (iii) the meaning is recoverable by any viewer who knows the convention.
conditions: stands for something other than itself
association fixed by convention
meaning recoverable by viewer who knows convention
counterexample: In Kafka's The Trial, the court — with its labyrinthine corridors, its inaccessible judges, its incomprehensible procedures — functions as a symbol. It stands for something beyond itself: the opacity of authority, the helplessness of the individual, the absurdity of bureaucratic power. But the association is not fixed by convention; there is no tradition in which a court symbolises these things. Nor is the meaning fully recoverable by any single interpretive act; the symbol resists exhaustive decoding. The second and third conditions fail, yet the court is one of the most powerful symbols in modern literature.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the lamb, the skull, the lion — were all conventional symbols with fixed, decodable meanings. The definition abstracted conventionality and decodability as essential. But the most potent symbols in literary art are created by the artist, not inherited from tradition, and their power resides precisely in their resistance to fixed interpretation.
passage:
Consider three cases of symbolism. In Christian painting, the lamb symbolises Christ: the convention is ancient and fixed, and any educated viewer decodes the meaning at once. In Dutch still-life painting, the skull symbolises mortality: the vanitas tradition establishes the meaning, and the viewer grasps it immediately. In heraldry, the lion symbolises courage: the association is codified and universally recognised. From these cases one might define symbolic function: an element in a work of art functions as a symbol if and only if it stands for something other than itself, the association is fixed by convention, and the meaning is recoverable by any viewer who knows the convention.

But this definition, drawn from conventional symbols, cannot accommodate the created symbol of literary art. Consider the court in Kafka's The Trial. It stands for something beyond itself — of that the reader is certain. The labyrinthine corridors, the inaccessible judges, the proceedings that begin without explanation and end without resolution — all point towards something larger: the opacity of authority, the individual's helplessness before power, the absurdity of systems that demand compliance but offer no comprehension. But the association between the court and these meanings is not fixed by any convention. There is no tradition of court-symbolism on which Kafka draws; the symbol is his own creation. Nor is the meaning fully recoverable. Every reading of The Trial yields further significances; the symbol is inexhaustible. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases were all conventional symbols whose meanings were fixed and finite, and the definition abstracted these qualities as essential. But the most powerful symbols in literature are precisely those that resist definitive decoding.

background_cases: The facade of a Renaissance palazzo is well-proportioned: its windows, columns, and storeys stand in ratios derived from simple whole-number relationships.
The human figure as depicted by Leonardo is well-proportioned: the ratios of limbs to torso conform to the classical canon of Vitruvius.
A Greek amphora is well-proportioned: the ratio of its height to its width, and of its body to its neck, conforms to established ceramic traditions.
definition: An object is well-proportioned if and only if (i) its parts stand in ratios that are reducible to simple whole-number relationships, (ii) the ratios conform to an established canon or tradition, and (iii) the object produces an impression of visual harmony.
conditions: parts stand in simple whole-number ratios
ratios conform to established canon
produces impression of visual harmony
counterexample: The nave of a Gothic cathedral — Chartres, say — produces an overwhelming impression of visual harmony: the soaring verticals, the pointed arches, the play of light through stained glass combine to create a space of extraordinary beauty. The ratios of the nave are not, however, reducible to simple whole-number relationships in many cases; Gothic builders worked by geometrical construction (using compasses and straightedges) rather than by arithmetic ratios. Nor do the ratios conform to the classical canon; Gothic proportion is a different system entirely. The first and second conditions fail, yet the cathedral is magnificently proportioned.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the palazzo, the Vitruvian figure, the amphora — were all drawn from the classical tradition, in which proportion is governed by arithmetic ratios and codified canons. The definition abstracted these classical features as essential. But the Gothic tradition achieves proportion through geometrical construction rather than arithmetic ratios, and its results are as harmonious as anything the classical tradition produced.
passage:
Consider three cases of good proportion. A Renaissance palazzo has a facade whose windows, columns, and storeys stand in ratios derived from simple whole-number relationships: the classical tradition of proportion governs the design, and the result is a building of serene visual harmony. Leonardo's depiction of the human figure conforms to the Vitruvian canon: the navel divides the body in the golden ratio, the arm span equals the height, and the parts relate to the whole by simple numerical proportions. A Greek amphora exhibits ratios of height to width, body to neck, that are traditional and harmonious. From these cases one might define good proportion: an object is well-proportioned if and only if its parts stand in ratios reducible to simple whole-number relationships, the ratios conform to an established canon, and the object produces an impression of visual harmony.

But this definition, drawn from the classical tradition, cannot accommodate the achievements of the Gothic builders. Consider the nave of Chartres Cathedral. It produces an impression of visual harmony that is, by common testimony, among the most powerful in all of architecture: the soaring verticals, the pointed arches, the rhythm of bays, the light filtering through stained glass. Yet the proportions of the nave are not reducible to simple whole-number ratios. The Gothic builders did not work with arithmetic proportions; they worked with compass and straightedge, generating their forms through geometrical construction — the rotation of squares, the division of circles, the intersection of arcs. Nor did they conform to the classical canon; Gothic proportion is an entirely different system. The first and second conditions fail. The background cases were all classical, and the definition abstracted classical arithmetic proportion as the standard. But the Gothic tradition achieves harmony through geometry rather than arithmetic, and its proportions are no less beautiful for being differently generated.

background_cases: In Swift's 'A Modest Proposal,' the narrator recommends eating Irish children; the surface meaning is ironic, for Swift intends the opposite of what is said, and the reader recognises the discrepancy.

In Voltaire's Candide, the character Dr. Pangloss insists that this is the best of all possible worlds while surrounded by catastrophe; the reader recognises the ironic distance between assertion and reality.

In Jane Austen's opening sentence of Pride and Prejudice — 'It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife' — the surface assertion is ironically undermined by the narrative that follows.
definition: A literary work employs irony if and only if (i) the author says one thing and means another, (ii) the discrepancy between said and meant is signalled to the reader, and (iii) the reader's recognition of the discrepancy produces a specific effect — amusement, indignation, or moral insight.
conditions: author says one thing and means another
discrepancy signalled to reader
recognition produces specific effect — amusement indignation or moral insight
counterexample: In the novels of Thomas Hardy, situational irony pervades the narrative: characters make plans that are systematically thwarted by coincidence, fate, and the indifference of the universe. The irony does not consist in Hardy saying one thing and meaning another — he means precisely what he says. Nor is the discrepancy between said and meant, for there is no such discrepancy. The irony lies in the discrepancy between what the characters expect and what the universe delivers. The first condition fails, yet Hardy's novels are among the most deeply ironic in the English language.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Swift, Voltaire, Austen — all involved verbal irony, where the author's words diverge from his meaning. The definition abstracted the said-meant discrepancy as essential. But Hardy's situational irony operates through the divergence between human expectation and cosmic indifference, and involves no discrepancy between what the author says and what he means. The definition captured one variety of irony and missed the structural variety entirely.
passage:
Consider three cases of literary irony. In Swift's 'A Modest Proposal,' the narrator recommends eating children: Swift says one thing and means the opposite, and the reader, recognising the discrepancy, is moved to indignation. In Voltaire's Candide, Pangloss declares this the best of all possible worlds amid catastrophe: the discrepancy between assertion and reality produces mordant amusement. In Austen's opening of Pride and Prejudice, a 'universal truth' is ironically presented: the reader sees through the surface and smiles. From these cases one might define irony: a literary work employs irony if and only if the author says one thing and means another, the discrepancy is signalled to the reader, and recognition of the discrepancy produces a specific effect.

But this definition, drawn from cases of verbal irony, cannot accommodate the situational irony that pervades the novels of Thomas Hardy. In Tess of the d'Urbervilles and The Mayor of Casterbridge, characters make plans, form hopes, and strive towards goals that are systematically thwarted by coincidence, fate, and the indifference of the universe. The irony is powerful and pervasive. But it does not consist in Hardy saying one thing and meaning another. Hardy means exactly what he says; his prose is earnest, often anguished. The first condition fails. The discrepancy is not between said and meant but between what the characters expect and what the world delivers — between human aspiration and cosmic indifference. The background cases were all verbal ironies, and the definition abstracted the said-meant discrepancy as the essence of irony. But Hardy's irony is structural and situational, operating through the architecture of events rather than through the doubleness of speech, and it is no less ironic for being of a different kind.

background_cases: A Greek marble statue of Apollo, with symmetrical proportions and smooth, polished surfaces, is judged beautiful by virtually all observers.
A Renaissance painting of the Madonna, balanced in composition and harmonious in colour, is regarded as beautiful by critics and public alike.
A formal English garden, with geometrical beds, trimmed hedges, and mirror-image planting, is widely admired for its beauty.
definition: An object x is beautiful if and only if (i) x possesses geometrical regularity, (ii) x has smooth, polished, or refined surfaces, and (iii) x is the product of deliberate human design.
conditions: x possesses geometrical regularity
x has smooth, polished, or refined surfaces
x is the product of deliberate human design
counterexample: A rugged, weathered mountain peak — jagged in outline, rough in surface, wholly undesigned — is nonetheless beautiful. Its beauty has been affirmed by travellers, painters, and poets for centuries. It possesses no geometrical regularity, its surfaces are anything but smooth, and it is the product of geological forces rather than human design. All three conditions fail, yet the mountain is beautiful.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all artefacts — a statue, a painting, a garden — characterised by regularity, smoothness, and design. The definition abstracted these features as essential to beauty because they were present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the domain of human-made objects, and the definition mistook features of designed beauty for features of beauty as such. The mountain reveals that beauty may be wild, irregular, rough, and wholly natural, and that the concept of beauty is far broader than any definition drawn from artefacts alone can capture.
passage:
Consider three objects commonly judged beautiful. A Greek marble statue of Apollo, symmetrical in proportion and smooth in surface. A Renaissance painting of the Madonna, balanced in composition and harmonious in colour. A formal English garden, geometrically regular and meticulously maintained. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses geometrical regularity, it has smooth or polished surfaces, and it is the product of deliberate human design. The three background cases all satisfy these conditions, and the definition appears to identify what they share.

But one must consider the mountain. A rugged Alpine peak, jagged against the sky, its faces scarred by glaciers and seamed with crevasses, its surfaces rough granite and loose scree — such a peak is beautiful, and its beauty has been affirmed by travellers, painters, and poets since the eighteenth century at least. Ruskin described the beauty of mountain forms at length; Turner painted them; Wordsworth found in them a beauty that surpassed anything the hand of man could produce. Yet the mountain possesses no geometrical regularity: its outline is irregular, its forms chaotic, its proportions governed by no canon. Its surfaces are not smooth or polished but rough, broken, and weathered. And it is not the product of human design; it is the product of tectonic uplift, glacial erosion, and the slow action of wind and rain over millions of years. All three conditions fail. The background cases were all artefacts, and the definition mistook the features of designed, human-made beauty — regularity, refinement, intentional arrangement — for necessary conditions of beauty as such. What the mountain reveals is that beauty may be wild, irregular, rough-surfaced, and wholly independent of human intention, and that any adequate account of beauty must accommodate the natural sublime no less than the classically proportioned.

background_cases: Michelangelo's David required years of skilled carving in marble and is universally acknowledged as a work of art.
A Stradivarius violin, crafted over months with extraordinary skill in the selection and shaping of wood, is regarded as a masterpiece of the luthier's art.
An illuminated manuscript from a medieval scriptorium, requiring years of painstaking calligraphy and painting, is treasured as a work of art.
definition: An object x is a work of art if and only if (i) x required extended labour over a substantial period, (ii) x demanded a high degree of manual skill, and (iii) x is made from natural materials shaped by hand.
conditions: x required extended labour over a substantial period
x demanded a high degree of manual skill
x is made from natural materials shaped by hand
counterexample: A musical improvisation — a jazz solo performed in the moment, lasting only minutes, employing no natural materials shaped by hand but only the transient medium of sound produced through a manufactured instrument — may be a work of art. The great improvisations of the finest performers are recognised as artistic achievements of the highest order. Yet the performance required no extended labour; it was created in the moment. The first condition fails, the third fails, and the skill involved is performative rather than manual in the relevant sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all durable physical objects created through prolonged manual labour with natural materials — marble, wood, vellum. The definition abstracted duration, manual skill, and natural materials as essential to art because these features were shared by every case considered. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the domain of enduring craft objects. A jazz improvisation reveals that a work of art may be ephemeral, created in an instant, and realised in a medium — sound — that leaves no material trace. The definition confused the features of one historically prominent category of art with the essence of art itself.
passage:
Consider three objects universally acknowledged as works of art. Michelangelo's David, carved from marble over a period of years with consummate manual skill. A Stradivarius violin, crafted over months from carefully selected wood. An illuminated manuscript, requiring years of painstaking calligraphy and miniature painting upon vellum. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a work of art if and only if it required extended labour over a substantial period, it demanded a high degree of manual skill, and it is made from natural materials shaped by hand. The three cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition appears to identify their common essence.

But one must consider the musical improvisation. A jazz musician — one thinks of the great soloists of the tradition — steps to the front of the ensemble and, over the course of a few minutes, creates a solo of extraordinary inventiveness, emotional depth, and formal coherence. The solo is recognised by listeners and fellow musicians as a work of art; it is discussed, analysed, and remembered with the same seriousness accorded to composed music. Yet it was not the product of extended labour; it was created in the moment, its entire duration measured in minutes. It demanded skill, certainly, but the skill is performative and improvisatory, not manual in the sense of shaping material by hand. And the medium is sound — ephemeral, intangible, leaving no material residue. The first and third conditions fail. The background cases were all durable physical objects requiring prolonged manual craftsmanship, and the definition mistook the features of this particular category of art for the features of art as such. What the improvisation reveals is that art may be ephemeral, instantaneous, and immaterial, and that the essence of art lies not in the duration of labour or the shaping of physical materials but in something altogether more elusive.

background_cases: Standing at the rim of a vast canyon and looking down into its depths produces a feeling of vertigo, awe, and the overwhelming sense of one's own smallness.
Watching a violent thunderstorm approach across an open plain, with towering cumulonimbus clouds and jagged lightning, occasions a mixed feeling of terror and exhilaration.
Standing at the base of a great waterfall, drenched in spray, deafened by the roar of falling water, one feels simultaneously overwhelmed and elevated.
definition: An experience is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) it is occasioned by a spatially vast or physically powerful natural phenomenon, (ii) it involves the engagement of multiple senses simultaneously, and (iii) the subject feels physically small or endangered in the presence of the phenomenon.
conditions: it is occasioned by a spatially vast or physically powerful natural phenomenon
it involves the engagement of multiple senses simultaneously
the subject feels physically small or endangered in the presence of the phenomenon
counterexample: A mathematician contemplating Cantor's proof that the real numbers are uncountable may experience the sublime. The proof reveals an infinity of infinities, a structure of magnitude so far exceeding ordinary comprehension that the mind feels a kind of vertigo analogous to that of the canyon's edge. Yet no spatially vast or physically powerful phenomenon is involved; the experience is purely intellectual. No multiple senses are engaged; the mathematician reads symbols on a page. And the mathematician does not feel physically small or endangered. All three conditions fail, yet the experience is sublime.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all encounters with spatially vast or physically powerful natural phenomena engaging multiple senses — canyons, storms, waterfalls. The definition abstracted spatial vastness, sensory multiplicity, and felt physical vulnerability as essential to the sublime. But these were features of one species of the sublime — the natural or dynamical sublime. The mathematical sublime, occasioned by the contemplation of infinite magnitudes through pure intellect, involves none of these features. The definition mistook the sensory and spatial character of natural sublimity for the essence of sublimity itself, when what is essential is the experience of confronting something that exceeds the mind's ordinary capacity for comprehension.
passage:
Consider three experiences commonly described as sublime. Standing at the rim of a vast canyon and gazing into its depths, one feels vertigo, awe, and an overwhelming sense of one's own smallness. Watching a violent thunderstorm approach across an open plain, one is struck by the scale and power of the phenomenon and experiences a mingling of terror and exhilaration. Standing at the base of a great waterfall, drenched in spray and deafened by the roar, one feels simultaneously overwhelmed and strangely elevated. From these cases one might extract a definition: an experience is sublime if and only if it is occasioned by a spatially vast or physically powerful natural phenomenon, it engages multiple senses simultaneously, and the subject feels physically small or endangered. The background cases all satisfy these conditions.

But one must consider the mathematician who contemplates Cantor's diagonal proof — the demonstration that the real numbers cannot be placed in one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers, that there exist, in a precise sense, more real numbers than natural numbers, and indeed that there is an ascending hierarchy of infinities, each larger than the last. The mathematician who grasps this result for the first time may experience something that can only be described as sublime: a kind of intellectual vertigo, a sense that the mind has been brought to the edge of what it can comprehend and shown a structure of magnitude that exceeds all ordinary reckoning. Writers on mathematics have described this experience in terms strikingly reminiscent of the accounts of natural sublimity given by Burke and others. Yet no spatially vast phenomenon is involved; the proof exists as a sequence of symbols on a page. No multiple senses are engaged; the experience is purely intellectual. The mathematician does not feel physically small or endangered; the feeling is one of cognitive overwhelm, not bodily peril. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were drawn exclusively from encounters with natural phenomena, and the definition mistook the sensory and spatial character of these encounters for the essence of the sublime, when in truth the sublime is a response to whatever exceeds the mind's capacity for ordinary comprehension — whether physical or purely intellectual.

background_cases: A music critic who has listened to thousands of symphonies reliably identifies the finest performances and can articulate the grounds of superiority in technical terms.
A wine connoisseur who has tasted extensively over decades reliably distinguishes excellent wines from mediocre ones and can describe the relevant qualities with precision.
A literary scholar who has read widely in several languages reliably judges the merit of novels and can explain, with reference to structure and style, why one surpasses another.
definition: A person S has good taste if and only if (i) S has extensive experience of the relevant domain accumulated over many years, (ii) S possesses a specialised technical vocabulary for describing the objects of judgement, and (iii) S can rank objects within the domain with consistency and can explain the ranking.
conditions: S has extensive experience of the relevant domain accumulated over many years
S possesses a specialised technical vocabulary for describing the objects of judgement
S can rank objects within the domain with consistency and can explain the ranking
counterexample: A young child of eight, taken to a concert for the first time, hears a performance of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony. The child has no extensive experience of music, possesses no technical vocabulary — cannot speak of sonata form, of motivic development, of orchestration — and cannot rank performances or explain a ranking. Yet the child is deeply moved, attends with rapt concentration, and, when later offered a choice between a recording of the Beethoven and a recording of trivial background music, unhesitatingly prefers the Beethoven. The child's taste, though inarticulate, is good. All three conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all expert judges — a music critic, a wine connoisseur, a literary scholar — who had accumulated years of experience and developed technical vocabularies. The definition abstracted experience, vocabulary, and the ability to rank and explain as essential to good taste. But these are features of expert taste, not of taste as such. The child's case reveals that good taste may manifest as an inarticulate but reliable sensitivity to genuine quality, prior to all experience, vocabulary, and explicit reasoning. The definition confused the marks of expert cultivation with the necessary conditions of taste itself.
passage:
Consider three persons acknowledged to have good taste. A music critic who has listened to thousands of symphonies and can articulate in technical terms why one performance excels another. A wine connoisseur of decades' experience who can describe with precision the qualities that distinguish an excellent vintage. A literary scholar who reads in several languages and can explain, with reference to structure and style, why one novel surpasses another. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person has good taste if and only if the person has extensive experience in the relevant domain, possesses a specialised technical vocabulary, and can rank objects with consistency and explain the ranking. The background cases all satisfy these conditions, and the definition appears to capture what good taste requires.

But one must consider the child. A child of eight, taken to a concert for the first time, hears Beethoven's Fifth Symphony performed by a competent orchestra. The child has no extensive experience of symphonic music; this is the first concert the child has attended. The child possesses no technical vocabulary whatsoever — cannot speak of sonata form, of motivic development, of tonal relations, of orchestral colour. And the child cannot rank performances or explain a ranking; the child has heard only one. Yet the child is deeply moved. The child attends with an intensity and concentration that the parent has never before observed; the child is silent, wide-eyed, wholly absorbed. And when, upon returning home, the child is offered a choice between a recording of the Beethoven and a recording of trivial, saccharine background music, the child unhesitatingly and with evident feeling chooses the Beethoven. The child's taste is good — it is responsive to genuine musical quality — yet it satisfies none of the three conditions. The definition drew its conditions from the features of expert, cultivated taste, and mistook the apparatus of expertise for the essence of taste itself. What the child reveals is that good taste may exist as an inarticulate sensitivity, prior to experience, prior to vocabulary, prior to the capacity for explanation.

background_cases: A visitor to the Louvre stands before the Mona Lisa and contemplates the painting for several minutes, attending to its composition, sfumato technique, and enigmatic expression.
A concert-goer listens to a string quartet by Haydn, attending to the interplay of the four voices, the elegance of the phrasing, and the wit of the compositional structure.
A reader sits in an armchair and reads a sonnet by Shakespeare, attending to the metre, the imagery, and the unfolding argument of the poem.
definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed at a recognised work of art, (ii) E involves sustained, focused attention over a period of at least several minutes, and (iii) E takes place in a state of physical stillness and repose.
conditions: E is directed at a recognised work of art
E involves sustained, focused attention over a period of at least several minutes
E takes place in a state of physical stillness and repose
counterexample: A person dancing to music at a village festival — moving vigorously, turning and leaping, wholly caught up in the rhythm and melody — may have a genuine aesthetic experience. The person attends to the music, is absorbed in its formal qualities, and experiences a heightened awareness of sound, movement, and bodily sensation. Yet the experience is not directed at a recognised work of art (the music may be a traditional tune of no fixed authorship); the attention, though intense, is not sustained in focused stillness but is distributed across the moving body and the social scene; and the person is emphatically not in a state of physical repose. All three conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of quiet, solitary contemplation of canonical works of art — a painting, a string quartet, a sonnet. The definition abstracted these features: direction at a recognised work, sustained focused attention, and physical stillness. But the cases were drawn from a narrow range of contemplative aesthetic situations. Dancing to music reveals that aesthetic experience may be active, bodily, socially embedded, and directed at objects that are not canonical artworks. The definition confused the conditions of one culturally specific mode of aesthetic engagement — the museum visit, the concert, the private reading — with the necessary conditions of aesthetic experience as such.
passage:
Consider three experiences that are paradigmatically aesthetic. A visitor contemplates the Mona Lisa at the Louvre, standing still before the painting for several minutes. A concert-goer sits in attentive silence, listening to a Haydn quartet. A reader reclines in an armchair with a Shakespeare sonnet. From these cases one might extract a definition: an experience is aesthetic if and only if it is directed at a recognised work of art, it involves sustained focused attention over several minutes, and it takes place in physical stillness and repose. The background cases all satisfy these conditions, and the definition seems to identify the essential features of aesthetic experience.

But one must consider the dancer. At a village festival, a person dances to traditional music — a fiddle tune, perhaps, of no fixed authorship, handed down through generations of anonymous musicians. The dancer is wholly absorbed in the music: in its rhythms, its melodic contours, the way the phrases rise and fall and return. The dancer's body moves in response to these formal qualities, and the experience is one of heightened awareness — of sound, of movement, of the relation between bodily gesture and musical form. This is an aesthetic experience; it is characterised by the same absorbed, appreciative attention to formal qualities that marks the museum visitor's contemplation of a painting. Yet it satisfies none of the three conditions. The music is not a recognised work of art in the canonical sense; the attention is not sustained in focused stillness but is dynamic, distributed, and embodied; and the dancer is emphatically not in repose but in vigorous motion. The background cases were drawn from a narrow range of contemplative settings — the museum, the concert hall, the study — and the definition mistook the features of these settings for the features of aesthetic experience itself. What the dancer reveals is that aesthetic experience may be active, bodily, and communal, and that it is not the exclusive possession of quiet contemplation.

background_cases: A Dutch still-life painting of a bowl of fruit represents the fruit by closely resembling the actual appearance of apples, grapes, and peaches arranged on a table.
A portrait by Ingres represents the sitter by closely reproducing the sitter's facial features, colouring, and costume in meticulous detail.
A topographical watercolour of an English village represents the village by depicting its church, cottages, and surrounding landscape with careful fidelity to their visible appearance.
definition: A picture P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P reproduces the visible appearance of S with a high degree of fidelity, (ii) P and S share a large number of visual properties in common, and (iii) a viewer unfamiliar with P could identify S on the basis of P alone.
conditions: P reproduces the visible appearance of S with a high degree of fidelity
P and S share a large number of visual properties in common
a viewer unfamiliar with P could identify S on the basis of P alone
counterexample: A political caricature — a cartoon drawing of a well-known statesman — represents its subject by grossly exaggerating certain facial features: an enormous nose, a jutting chin, a wild tuft of hair. The caricature does not reproduce the visible appearance of the subject with fidelity; it deliberately distorts it. The caricature and the subject share relatively few visual properties in common; the proportions are wrong, the features are exaggerated, the rendering is schematic. Yet viewers identify the subject instantly and with confidence — indeed, the caricature may be more immediately recognisable than a faithful portrait. All three conditions fail or are only partially satisfied, yet the caricature represents its subject.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all works in the tradition of illusionistic realism — a still life, a portrait, a topographical view — characterised by faithful reproduction of visible appearances. The definition abstracted fidelity, shared visual properties, and viewer identification as essential to representation. But these features were characteristic of one mode of representation, and the definition mistook this mode for representation itself. The caricature represents by exaggeration and distortion, not by fidelity, and the fact that it is immediately recognisable despite its departures from visual accuracy shows that representation does not require resemblance in the sense the definition assumes. What is at work is a system of pictorial conventions in which selective emphasis and distortion can be as representationally effective as faithful copying.
passage:
Consider three pictures that unquestionably represent their subjects. A Dutch still life reproduces the appearance of fruit on a table with meticulous fidelity. A portrait by Ingres captures the sitter's features with extraordinary precision. A topographical watercolour depicts an English village with careful attention to the visible details of its buildings and landscape. From these cases one might extract a definition: a picture represents a subject if and only if it reproduces the visible appearance of the subject with high fidelity, it shares a large number of visual properties with the subject, and a viewer unfamiliar with the picture could identify the subject on the basis of the picture alone. The background cases all satisfy these conditions admirably.

But one must consider the political caricature. A caricaturist draws a well-known statesman, grossly exaggerating certain facial features — the nose is rendered enormous, the chin juts forward absurdly, the brow is a single dark slash. The caricature does not reproduce the statesman's appearance with fidelity; it deliberately distorts it. The drawing and the actual face share relatively few visual properties in common: the proportions are wrong, the features are exaggerated beyond anything found in nature, and the rendering is schematic and simplified. Yet the caricature represents the statesman, and viewers identify the subject instantly — indeed, the caricature may be more immediately recognisable than a faithful photographic portrait, for it isolates and amplifies precisely those features by which the individual is distinguished from other persons. The definition, drawn from cases of illusionistic realism, assumed that representation requires faithful reproduction of appearances. The caricature shows that this is not so: representation may proceed by selective exaggeration and deliberate distortion, and what makes the caricature representationally effective is not the number of visual properties it shares with its subject but the skill with which it emphasises the distinguishing ones.

background_cases: Chopin's Funeral March, composed after a period of personal grief, expresses sorrow through its slow tempo, minor key, and heavy, weighted rhythmic figures.
A painting by El Greco depicting the agony of Christ in Gethsemane, created with evident emotional intensity, expresses anguish through its distorted forms and lurid colours.
Keats's 'Ode to a Nightingale,' written during a period of illness and the poet's awareness of mortality, expresses melancholy longing through its imagery and rhythmic languor.
definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) E is a negative or painful emotion such as sorrow, anguish, or longing, (ii) the artist experienced E and channelled it into the creation of W, and (iii) the formal properties of W — tempo, colour, rhythm, imagery — are sombre, dark, or heavy.
conditions: E is a negative or painful emotion such as sorrow, anguish, or longing
the artist experienced E and channelled it into the creation of W
the formal properties of W are sombre, dark, or heavy
counterexample: The finale of Beethoven's Seventh Symphony expresses a wild, ecstatic joy. Its tempo is fast, its rhythms are driving and energetic, its key is major, and its formal properties — far from being sombre, dark, or heavy — are bright, vigorous, and exuberant. The emotion expressed is not negative or painful; it is joyous in the extreme. Wagner called the movement 'the apotheosis of the dance.' All three conditions fail, yet the movement is deeply expressive.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all works expressing negative emotions — grief, anguish, longing — through sombre formal properties. The definition abstracted negativity of emotion, biographical channelling, and sombre formal qualities as essential to artistic expression. But the cases were drawn exclusively from works of sorrowful or painful character, and the definition mistook the features of sorrowful expression for the features of expression as such. The Beethoven finale reveals that a work may express positive, joyous emotion with equal or greater intensity, and that the formal properties appropriate to such expression — brightness, speed, vigour — are the opposite of those the definition requires. Expression in art encompasses the full range of human emotion, not merely its painful register.
passage:
Consider three works of art that are acknowledged to be deeply expressive. Chopin's Funeral March expresses sorrow through its slow tempo, minor key, and heavy rhythmic tread. An El Greco painting of Christ's agony expresses anguish through distorted forms and lurid, unsettling colour. Keats's 'Ode to a Nightingale' expresses melancholy longing through languorous rhythm and images of fading and dissolution. From these cases one might extract a definition: a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if the emotion is negative or painful, the artist experienced it and channelled it into the work, and the formal properties of the work are sombre, dark, or heavy. The background cases all satisfy these conditions, and the definition appears to identify what artistic expression requires.

But one must consider the finale of Beethoven's Seventh Symphony. This movement is among the most expressive in the entire orchestral repertoire. Wagner called it the apotheosis of the dance, and listeners since its first performance have been struck by the overwhelming power of the emotion it conveys. But the emotion is not sorrow, anguish, or longing. It is joy — a wild, ecstatic, almost frenzied joy that builds in intensity from the first bar to the last. The tempo is fast, the rhythms are driving and energetic, the key is major, and the formal properties of the movement are bright, vigorous, and exuberant — the very opposite of sombre, dark, or heavy. The definition, drawn from cases of sorrowful expression, assumed that all artistic expression involves negative emotion conveyed through sombre means. The Beethoven finale shows that expression encompasses the full range of human feeling, and that joyous expression may be no less powerful than sorrowful. What the definition failed to recognise is that the concept of expression is neutral with respect to the valence of the emotion expressed.

background_cases: A rose in full bloom, with its layered petals, rich colour, and sweet fragrance, is judged beautiful by persons of every culture and epoch.
A peacock's tail, with its iridescent eye-spots and symmetrical fan of feathers, is universally cited as an instance of natural beauty.
A clear night sky filled with stars, vast and luminous, is regarded as beautiful by travellers, poets, and philosophers alike.
definition: An object x is beautiful if and only if (i) x is a natural object untouched by human alteration, (ii) x possesses vivid sensory qualities — bright colour, luminosity, or fragrance, and (iii) x evokes a feeling of wonder or admiration in the observer.
conditions: x is a natural object untouched by human alteration
x possesses vivid sensory qualities — bright colour, luminosity, or fragrance
x evokes a feeling of wonder or admiration in the observer
counterexample: A sonata by Mozart — the Piano Sonata in A major, K. 331, with its graceful theme and variations — is beautiful. It evokes wonder and admiration in the listener, and its sensory qualities, though auditory rather than visual or olfactory, are vivid and affecting. Yet it is not a natural object; it is a human artefact, composed deliberately and notated on paper. The first condition fails, yet the sonata is beautiful.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all natural objects — a rose, a peacock's tail, a starry sky — and the definition abstracted naturalness as essential to beauty. But the cases were drawn exclusively from nature, and the definition mistook a feature of the sample for a feature of the concept. The Mozart sonata reveals that beauty extends to human artefacts, and that the properties essential to beauty — formal coherence, vivid sensory or affective quality, the power to evoke admiration — are not confined to objects untouched by human hands. The definition was an over-generalisation from a biased sample.
passage:
Consider three objects widely regarded as beautiful. A rose in full bloom, with its layered petals, its rich red or white colour, and its sweet fragrance. A peacock's tail, spread in a shimmering fan of iridescent greens and blues, each feather bearing an eye-spot of remarkable intricacy. A clear night sky, vast and filled with stars, luminous against the darkness. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it is a natural object untouched by human alteration, it possesses vivid sensory qualities such as bright colour, luminosity, or fragrance, and it evokes a feeling of wonder or admiration in the observer. The background cases satisfy all three conditions, and the definition seems to identify what is common to them.

But one must consider the sonata. Mozart's Piano Sonata in A major, with its graceful theme and elegant variations, is beautiful — the judgement is as secure as any in aesthetics. The sonata evokes wonder and admiration in the listener; its sensory qualities, though auditory rather than visual, are vivid, affecting, and exquisitely proportioned. But the sonata is not a natural object. It is a human artefact, composed by a particular individual at a particular time, notated on paper, and realised in performance through deliberate human action. The first condition fails entirely. The background cases were all natural objects, and the definition abstracted naturalness as a necessary condition of beauty because it was present in every case examined. But the sample was biased: it included only nature and excluded the entire domain of artistic beauty. The Mozart sonata — and with it every beautiful painting, every beautiful building, every beautiful poem — reveals that beauty is not the exclusive property of the natural world. The concept of beauty spans both nature and art, and any definition drawn from one domain alone will inevitably exclude the other.

background_cases: A solitary viewer stands before a Turner seascape in a quiet gallery, absorbed in the painting's luminous depiction of light and water, and reports an experience of profound aesthetic satisfaction.
A listener sits alone in a darkened concert hall, hearing a Bruckner symphony unfold over the course of an hour, and describes the experience as one of deep aesthetic absorption.
A reader, alone in a library, reads a passage of Proust and is so absorbed in the prose that the surrounding world recedes from awareness entirely.
definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E involves the absorption of a single individual in a perceptual or imaginative object, (ii) E is solitary — the subject is alone or effectively isolated from others during E, and (iii) E is characterised by a withdrawal from the social and practical world.
conditions: E involves the absorption of a single individual in a perceptual or imaginative object
E is solitary — the subject is alone or effectively isolated from others during E
E is characterised by a withdrawal from the social and practical world
counterexample: Consider the experience of the audience at a performance of a Greek tragedy in an ancient amphitheatre — thousands of spectators, gathered together, watching the chorus and actors, sharing in the unfolding of the dramatic action. The experience involves absorption in a perceptual and imaginative object — the performance. But it is emphatically not solitary; the spectator is surrounded by thousands of others, and the shared character of the experience — the collective gasp, the communal silence, the shared catharsis — is an essential part of its aesthetic quality.
