The second condition fails. Yet the experience of tragic drama in communal performance is among the oldest and most powerful forms of aesthetic experience known.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of solitary aesthetic engagement — a lone viewer in a gallery, a single listener in a concert hall, a reader alone in a library. The definition abstracted solitude and withdrawal from the social world as essential to aesthetic experience. But the cases were drawn from a modern, individualistic conception of aesthetic life, and the definition mistook the features of this particular cultural formation for universal features of aesthetic experience. The Greek theatre reveals that aesthetic experience may be profoundly communal — that the shared, collective character of the experience may be not an impediment to but a constitutive element of its aesthetic quality. The definition confused a culturally specific mode of aesthetic engagement with the essence of the concept.
passage:
Consider three experiences that are paradigmatically aesthetic. A solitary viewer stands before a Turner seascape in a quiet gallery, absorbed in the painting's luminous rendering of light and water. A single listener sits in a darkened concert hall, hearing a Bruckner symphony unfold in slow, majestic waves. A reader, alone in a library, reads a passage of Proust with such concentration that the surrounding world ceases to exist. From these cases one might extract a definition: an experience is aesthetic if and only if it involves the absorption of a single individual in a perceptual or imaginative object, it is solitary, and it is characterised by a withdrawal from the social and practical world. The background cases all satisfy these conditions, and the definition appears to identify the essential structure of aesthetic experience.

But one must consider the theatre — and not merely the modern theatre of hushed auditoriums and darkened seats, but the ancient Greek theatre, where tragedies were performed before audiences of ten or fifteen thousand spectators in the open air. The spectator at a performance of Sophocles is absorbed in the dramatic action — in the unfolding of Oedipus's fate, in the pronouncements of the chorus, in the spectacle of masked actors moving through the orchestra. The experience is genuinely aesthetic: it involves the perception of formal properties — the structure of the plot, the rhythm of the verse, the movement of the chorus — and it is characterised by the heightened, contemplative attention that marks all aesthetic engagement. But it is not solitary. The spectator is surrounded by thousands of fellow citizens, and the communal character of the experience is essential to its quality. The collective silence at the moment of recognition, the shared intake of breath, the catharsis that passes through the assembled audience like a wave — these are not accidental features of the experience but constitutive elements of it. The background cases were drawn from a modern, individualistic culture of aesthetic life, and the definition mistook the features of this culture — solitude, privacy, withdrawal — for necessary conditions of aesthetic experience as such. The Greek theatre reveals that aesthetic experience may be profoundly communal, and that its communal character, far from diminishing its aesthetic quality, may deepen and enrich it.

background_cases: A Greek marble statue of a young athlete, whose proportions follow the canon of Polyclitus, whose surface is smooth and luminous, and whose form embodies an ideal of physical perfection.
A Renaissance portrait by Raphael, in which the sitter's features are harmonised and idealised, the composition is balanced, and the colours are rich and pleasing.

A formal garden at a great English country house, whose parterres are laid out in geometrical patterns, whose hedges are clipped to mathematical precision, and whose fountains and statuary are arranged with perfect symmetry.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O embodies an ideal form that transcends the imperfections of its particular material, (ii) O exhibits a regularity and symmetry of structure that is immediately perceptible, and (iii) O produces in the observer a serene and elevating pleasure free of all disturbance or unease.
conditions: O embodies an ideal form that transcends the imperfections of its particular material
O exhibits a regularity and symmetry of structure that is immediately perceptible
O produces in the observer a serene and elevating pleasure free of all disturbance or unease
counterexample: Consider a gnarled and ancient oak tree standing alone in a winter field. Its trunk is twisted, its branches are asymmetrical, and its bark is rough and deeply furrowed. It embodies no ideal form; its shape is the product of decades of wind, disease, and the accidents of growth. It exhibits no regularity or symmetry; every limb reaches in a different direction. And the pleasure it produces is not serene; it is a pleasure mixed with something like awe, a sense of endurance and struggle that is closer to disquiet than to tranquillity. Yet the tree is beautiful.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of idealised, regular, and serene beauty — a classical statue, a Renaissance portrait, a formal garden — in which beauty was associated with the transcendence of material imperfection, symmetrical structure, and calm pleasure. The definition abstracted these features as essential to beauty because they were shared by every case considered. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the domain of art and design that aims at ideal form, and the definition mistook the properties of classical beauty for the properties of beauty as such. The gnarled oak reveals that beauty may reside in irregularity rather than symmetry, in the marks of age and struggle rather than in the transcendence of material imperfection, and may produce a pleasure tinged with awe and disquiet rather than serene elevation.
passage:
Consider three objects that are widely acknowledged to be beautiful. A Greek marble statue of a young athlete, carved according to the canon of Polyclitus, in which every limb stands in precise mathematical proportion to every other, in which the surface of the marble has been polished to a luminous smoothness, and in which the human form is presented not as it is but as it ideally ought to be — purified of blemish, irregularity, and the accidents of individual existence. A portrait by Raphael, in which the sitter's features have been gently harmonised and idealised, the composition balanced with an effortless grace, and the colours modulated to produce a rich and satisfying whole. A formal garden at a great English country house — Blenheim, perhaps, or Chatsworth — whose parterres are laid out in geometrical patterns, whose hedges are clipped to mathematical precision, and whose fountains and statuary are disposed with a symmetry that pleases the eye at every turn. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it embodies an ideal form that transcends the imperfections of its particular material, exhibits a regularity and symmetry of structure that is immediately perceptible, and produces in the observer a serene and elevating pleasure free of all disturbance or unease.

But one must consider the gnarled and ancient oak. In a winter field in the English Midlands, there stands an oak tree that is perhaps four hundred years old. Its trunk is massively thick and deeply furrowed; the bark is rough, cracked, and blackened with age. Its branches are asymmetrical — one great limb extends far to the east, another has broken off and left a jagged scar, and the remaining boughs twist and writhe in directions that no geometrician could predict. It embodies no ideal form; its shape is the accumulated record of centuries of wind, frost, lightning, disease, and the sheer stubborn persistence of life. It exhibits no regularity or symmetry; it is irregular in every particular. And the pleasure it produces in the observer is not serene. It is a pleasure mixed with something graver — a sense of endurance, of struggle against the elements, of time made visible in wood and bark. There is awe in the response, and something close to disquiet, as though the tree's beauty were inseparable from the harshness of the conditions that have shaped it. Yet the tree is beautiful, and its beauty is of a kind that no formal garden can rival. The definition, drawn from cases of classical, idealised, and symmetrical beauty, mistook one species of the beautiful for the genus, and failed to accommodate the beauty that resides in irregularity, in the marks of age and endurance, and in pleasures that are not serene but shot through with awe and the intimation of mortality.

background_cases: A Dutch still-life painting of the seventeenth century, depicting a table laid with fruit, flowers, and a half-filled glass of wine, each object rendered with meticulous fidelity to visual appearance.
A portrait bust by Houdon, in which the marble captures the texture of skin, the folds of a cravat, and the particular set of the sitter's jaw with astonishing verisimilitude.
A landscape painting by Canaletto, depicting the Grand Canal of Venice with such precision that each building, each gondola, and each ripple of light on the water can be identified and verified.
definition: A work of art W represents a subject S if and only if (i) W reproduces the visual appearance of S with a high degree of fidelity, (ii) the resemblance between W and S is sufficient to enable a viewer to identify S solely by inspecting W, and (iii) the skill of the representation is measured by the closeness of the correspondence between the appearance of W and the appearance of S.
conditions: W reproduces the visual appearance of S with a high degree of fidelity
the resemblance between W and S is sufficient to enable a viewer to identify S solely by inspecting W
the skill of the representation is measured by the closeness of the correspondence between the appearance of W and the appearance of S
counterexample: Consider a political caricature by Daumier. The drawing distorts the subject's features — enlarging the nose, shrinking the forehead, exaggerating the jowls — to the point where the visual appearance of the drawing bears little faithful resemblance to the subject's actual face. Yet the caricature represents its subject; indeed, it captures something about the subject — his pomposity, his venality, his self-satisfaction — that a faithful portrait might fail to convey.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of high-fidelity visual reproduction — a still life, a portrait bust, a topographical landscape — in which representation was achieved by reproducing the appearance of the subject as accurately as possible. The definition abstracted fidelity of visual appearance as the essence of representation. But the cases were drawn from the tradition of mimetic realism, and the definition mistook the standards of that tradition for the standards of representation as such. The caricature by Daumier reveals that representation may operate through distortion rather than fidelity, that it may capture aspects of its subject — character, moral quality, social type — that faithful reproduction would miss, and that the skill of the representation may be measured not by the closeness of visual correspondence but by the acuity of the interpretive insight that the distortion embodies.
passage:
Consider three works of art that plainly represent their subjects. A Dutch still-life painting of the seventeenth century, in which a table is laid with fruit, flowers, a silver dish, and a half-filled glass of wine, each object rendered with a fidelity so minute that one can count the seeds of the pomegranate and trace the condensation on the glass. A portrait bust by Houdon, in which the marble captures the texture of the sitter's skin, the precise folds of his cravat, and the particular set of his jaw with an accuracy that seems almost to animate the stone. A landscape by Canaletto, depicting the Grand Canal of Venice with such topographical precision that each palazzo, each gondola, each ripple of light on the water can be identified and verified against the actual scene. From these cases one might extract a definition: a work of art represents a subject if and only if it reproduces the visual appearance of the subject with a high degree of fidelity, the resemblance is sufficient to enable a viewer to identify the subject by inspecting the work, and the skill of the representation is measured by the closeness of the correspondence between the appearance of the work and the appearance of the subject.

But one must consider the political caricature. Honoré Daumier produced, in the Paris of Louis-Philippe, a series of lithographic caricatures of the politicians, lawyers, and bourgeois worthies of his day. In these drawings, the subject's features are deliberately and extravagantly distorted. The nose is enlarged to grotesque proportions; the forehead is compressed; the jowls are exaggerated into pendulous folds of flesh. The visual appearance of the drawing bears little faithful resemblance to the subject's actual face; a viewer who had never seen the subject and who compared the caricature with a photograph would find the correspondence remote. Yet the caricature represents its subject. It represents him not by reproducing his appearance but by interpreting it — by seizing upon those features that reveal character, by exaggerating the physical signs of pomposity, venality, or complacent self-regard. The caricature captures something about its subject that a faithful portrait might entirely miss: the inner man, the moral quality, the social type. The skill of the representation is measured not by the closeness of visual correspondence but by the acuity and penetration of the artist's interpretive insight. The definition, drawn from cases of mimetic realism, mistook faithful reproduction of appearance for the essence of representation, and thereby excluded an entire tradition of representational art in which distortion, exaggeration, and interpretive freedom are the very means by which the subject is most powerfully represented.

background_cases: The experience of standing before Botticelli's Primavera in the Uffizi, absorbed in the painting's beauty for a sustained period of contemplative attention.
The experience of listening to a complete performance of Bach's St Matthew Passion in a concert hall, carried along by the unfolding musical narrative over the course of several hours.
The experience of reading Keats's Ode to a Nightingale slowly and attentively, savouring each image and each modulation of rhythm and sound.
definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is an experience of concentrated, focused attention directed towards a single work of art, (ii) E unfolds over a sustained temporal duration sufficient for the appreciation of the work's formal and expressive qualities, and (iii) E is characterised by a sense of removal from the concerns and distractions of everyday life.
conditions: E is an experience of concentrated, focused attention directed towards a single work of art
E unfolds over a sustained temporal duration sufficient for the appreciation of the work's formal and expressive qualities
E is characterised by a sense of removal from the concerns and distractions of everyday life
counterexample: Consider the experience of a man walking through a busy market who hears, from an open window above, a few bars of a melody played on a violin — perhaps eight or ten seconds of music — before the sound is lost in the noise of the crowd. The experience is not sustained; it lasts a mere instant. His attention is not concentrated and focused; he was occupied with the business of the market, and the music caught him unawares. He is not removed from everyday concerns; he is standing in the midst of them. Yet the experience is aesthetic — those few bars of music struck him with a vividness and a beauty that he will remember for years.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all paradigmatic encounters with major works of art in dedicated settings — a painting in a gallery, a performance in a concert hall, a poem read in solitude — characterised by sustained, concentrated, and sequestered attention. The definition abstracted these features as essential to aesthetic experience. But the cases were drawn from the domain of deliberate, extended engagement with art in institutional or private settings, and the definition mistook the conditions of that domain for the conditions of aesthetic experience as such. The fleeting experience of hearing a few bars of music in a market reveals that aesthetic experience may be instantaneous rather than sustained, involuntary rather than concentrated, and embedded in ordinary life rather than removed from it. The definition confused the optimal conditions for aesthetic appreciation with the necessary conditions of aesthetic experience.
passage:
Consider three experiences that are readily acknowledged as aesthetic. The experience of standing before Botticelli's Primavera in the Uffizi Gallery in Florence, giving the painting one's full and undivided attention for twenty minutes or more, tracing the movement of the figures, attending to the delicacy of the draughtsmanship and the subtlety of the colour, and feeling oneself transported into the world of the painting. The experience of listening to a complete performance of Bach's St Matthew Passion in a concert hall, carried along by the unfolding musical narrative over the course of three hours, oblivious to everything but the music. The experience of reading Keats's Ode to a Nightingale slowly and attentively in a quiet room, savouring each image, each modulation of rhythm and sound, and feeling the poem's melancholy beauty permeate one's consciousness. From these cases one might extract a definition: an experience is an aesthetic experience if and only if it is an experience of concentrated, focused attention directed towards a single work of art, unfolds over a sustained temporal duration, and is characterised by a sense of removal from the concerns of everyday life.

But one must consider the fleeting musical moment. A man is walking through a busy market on a Saturday morning — a market crowded with stalls, with the cries of vendors, with the press of shoppers and the smell of fish and flowers. His mind is occupied with his errands. As he passes beneath an open window on the second storey of a building, he hears — for no more than eight or ten seconds — a few bars of a melody played on a violin. The sound is faint, half-drowned by the noise of the market, and it ceases as suddenly as it began. Yet in that instant something happens. The melody arrests him; it penetrates the noise and the bustle and strikes him with a beauty so vivid and so unexpected that for a moment the market and his errands and the whole apparatus of his daily concerns seem to recede. The experience lasts only seconds. His attention was not focused; he was not seeking an aesthetic experience, and the music caught him entirely off guard. He was not removed from everyday life; he was standing in the thick of it, surrounded by the clamour of commerce and the jostling of the crowd. Yet the experience is aesthetic. Those few bars of music, heard by accident in a noisy street, struck him with a vividness that he will carry with him for years, and the memory of them will return to him at unexpected moments with undiminished force. The definition, drawn from cases of sustained, concentrated, and sequestered engagement with art, mistook the conditions of one important form of aesthetic experience for the conditions of aesthetic experience as such, and thereby excluded the fleeting, involuntary, and wholly unsought aesthetic moments that are among the most powerful and memorable experiences of beauty that ordinary life affords.

background_cases: A self-portrait by Rembrandt in old age, in which the painter's weariness, resignation, and hard-won self-knowledge are legible in every brushstroke and every shadow on the aged face.
A nocturne by Chopin, in which the composer's melancholy and longing are expressed through the slow, singing melody and the rich, dark harmonies of the piano.
A sonnet by Shakespeare — 'That time of year thou mayst in me behold' — in which the poet's awareness of ageing and mortality is expressed through the imagery of autumn, twilight, and dying embers.
definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W is the product of the artist's personal experience of E, (ii) W conveys E through imagery, metaphor, or sensory qualities that are intuitively associated with E, and (iii) the emotion expressed in W is a single, identifiable affective state that can be named with a single word — grief, joy, longing, and so forth.
conditions: W is the product of the artist's personal experience of E
W conveys E through imagery, metaphor, or sensory qualities that are intuitively associated with E
the emotion expressed in W is a single, identifiable affective state that can be named with a single word
counterexample: Consider a passage in a late novel by Henry James — a passage describing a character's dawning awareness that she has been deceived by the two people she trusted most. The passage expresses something, and what it expresses is unmistakably an emotion, but the emotion cannot be named with a single word. It is a compound of recognition, betrayal, humiliation, a strange admiration for the skill of the deception, and a quiet, almost impersonal sorrow for the fragility of trust. It is not grief, not anger, not disillusionment, but something that partakes of all three without being reducible to any.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all works in which a single, nameable emotion — weariness, melancholy, awareness of mortality — was expressed through intuitively associated imagery and sensory qualities, and in which the artist's personal experience was plausibly the source of the expressed emotion. The definition abstracted personal experience, intuitive association, and single nameable affect as essential to artistic expression. But the cases were drawn from works of relatively transparent emotional content, and the definition mistook the features of emotionally simple and direct expression for the features of expression as such. The passage from James reveals that art may express emotional states of such complexity and nuance that no single word can name them — states that are compounds of recognition, betrayal, admiration, and sorrow, intertwined in ways that resist decomposition into discrete affects. The definition confused the properties of emotionally lucid, single-affect works with the necessary conditions of artistic expression.
passage:
Consider three works of art that plainly express emotion. A self-portrait by Rembrandt painted in the last years of his life, in which the aged painter looks out at the viewer with an expression of weariness, resignation, and hard-won self-knowledge — an expression that is legible in the heaviness of the eyelids, the slackness of the flesh, and the unflinching honesty of the gaze. A nocturne by Chopin, in which a slow, singing melody unfolds over dark and richly voiced harmonies, conveying a melancholy and a longing that seem to belong not merely to the composer but to the human condition itself. A sonnet by Shakespeare — 'That time of year thou mayst in me behold' — in which the imagery of bare ruined choirs, twilight, and the glowing embers of a dying fire expresses the poet's awareness of ageing and the approach of death. From these cases one might extract a definition: a work of art expresses an emotion if and only if it is the product of the artist's personal experience of that emotion, conveys it through imagery, metaphor, or sensory qualities intuitively associated with it, and the emotion expressed is a single, identifiable affective state that can be named with a single word.

But one must consider the late fiction of Henry James. In The Wings of the Dove, there is a passage — one might point to the scene in which Milly Theale begins to apprehend the truth of her situation — that expresses something of extraordinary emotional complexity. What the passage conveys is unmistakably an emotion, but it is not an emotion that can be named with a single word. It is a compound: there is recognition, the slow dawning of an understanding that has been resisted; there is the particular pain of betrayal by those one has loved and trusted; there is a thread of humiliation, but also, strangely, a kind of admiration for the intricacy and patience of the deception practised upon her; and beneath it all, a quiet, almost impersonal sorrow for the fragility of human trust and the ease with which it may be exploited. This compound emotion is not grief, not anger, not disillusionment, though it partakes of all three; it is something for which the English language has no single name, and its very resistance to naming is part of what makes the passage so powerful. Whether James himself experienced precisely this emotion is unknowable and, one suspects, irrelevant; the emotion is a creation of the art rather than a transcription of the life. The definition, drawn from works of relatively transparent and nameable emotional content, mistook the features of emotionally simple expression for the features of expression as such, and failed to accommodate the complex, compound, and unnameable emotions that the greatest works of literary art are uniquely capable of expressing.

background_cases: A connoisseur of painting who, upon entering a gallery, immediately distinguishes the genuine Vermeer from the skilful forgery, perceiving subtle differences in the handling of light that escape the untrained eye.
A wine taster who can identify the vineyard, the vintage, and the grape variety of a wine from a single sip, distinguishing flavours and aromas that others cannot detect.

A literary critic who, upon reading an anonymous manuscript, correctly attributes it to its author on the basis of subtle features of style — sentence rhythm, habitual metaphor, the characteristic movement of thought — that are imperceptible to the ordinary reader.
definition: A person P possesses good taste if and only if (i) P possesses an unusual acuity of sensory discrimination — the ability to perceive fine differences that escape ordinary perception, (ii) P exercises this acuity consistently and reliably across a wide range of objects within the relevant domain, and (iii) the judgements that P forms on the basis of this acuity are convergent with those of other persons of acknowledged taste.
conditions: P possesses an unusual acuity of sensory discrimination
P exercises this acuity consistently and reliably across a wide range of objects
the judgements that P forms on the basis of this acuity are convergent with those of other persons of acknowledged taste
counterexample: Consider a person who possesses an unusually acute visual perception — she notices the finest differences of colour, line, and brushwork — and who exercises this acuity consistently and reliably. Yet her judgements of taste consistently diverge from those of other acknowledged connoisseurs. She finds Caravaggio's chiaroscuro vulgar and Rembrandt's late portraits sentimental, whilst she regards certain minor Flemish painters as superior to both. She may be wrong; or she may be perceiving something that the prevailing consensus has not yet learnt to see. Her acuity is not in question, and her judgements are sincere and consistent. She possesses taste — a discriminating, perceptive, and independent taste — yet her judgements do not converge with those of other persons of acknowledged standing.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances in which sensory acuity, consistency, and convergence with expert opinion coincided — the connoisseur, the wine taster, the literary critic who perceives what others miss and whose judgements align with the best critical opinion. The definition abstracted these features as jointly necessary for good taste. But the cases were drawn from domains in which consensus among experts happens to be strong, and the definition mistook this convergence for a necessary condition. The case of the independent connoisseur reveals that taste may be exercised with full acuity and consistency yet diverge from the prevailing consensus — and that such divergence may represent not a failure of taste but its most rigorous and independent exercise. The definition confused the social validation of taste with the thing itself.
passage:
Consider three persons who are readily acknowledged to possess good taste. A connoisseur of painting who, upon entering a gallery of Dutch masters, immediately perceives the difference between a genuine Vermeer and a skilful modern forgery — a difference that lies in the handling of light, in the weight and transparency of the pigment, in subtle features of composition that escape the untrained eye. A wine taster who can identify, from a single sip, the vineyard, the vintage, and the grape variety, distinguishing flavours and aromas of such delicacy that they are wholly imperceptible to the ordinary palate. A literary critic who, upon reading an anonymous manuscript, correctly attributes it to its author on the basis of features of style so fine — the characteristic rhythm of the sentences, the habitual choice of metaphor, the movement of thought from one idea to the next — that they are invisible to the general reader. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person possesses good taste if and only if he possesses an unusual acuity of sensory discrimination, exercises this acuity consistently and reliably across a wide range of objects, and his judgements converge with those of other persons of acknowledged taste.

But one must consider the independent connoisseur. There is a woman — let us suppose her a collector of some experience and a frequent visitor to the galleries of Europe — whose visual acuity is as fine as that of any acknowledged expert. She perceives differences of brushwork, of tonal modulation, of compositional balance, with a precision that is beyond dispute. She exercises this acuity consistently; her perceptions are not erratic but stable and reliable. Yet her judgements of taste diverge, persistently and fundamentally, from those of the prevailing critical consensus. She finds the chiaroscuro of Caravaggio, so admired by the majority of connoisseurs, to be crude and theatrical. She regards Rembrandt's late self-portraits, universally praised for their profundity, as exercises in pictorial sentimentality. She values certain minor Flemish painters — artists whom the consensus has relegated to obscurity — for qualities of restraint, precision, and luminous quietness that she believes the consensus has failed to appreciate. She may be wrong; the consensus may be right and her judgement eccentric. But she may also be perceiving something that the consensus has not yet learnt to see — as connoisseurs of an earlier generation perceived qualities in El Greco that their contemporaries dismissed as incompetence. Her acuity is genuine, her consistency is beyond question, and her judgements are the product of long and attentive looking. She possesses taste — a discriminating, perceptive, and independent taste — even though her judgements do not converge with those of other persons of acknowledged standing. The definition, drawn from cases in which acuity and consensus happened to coincide, mistook that coincidence for a necessary condition, and thereby confused the social endorsement of taste with taste itself.

background_cases: A Greek marble statue of a young athlete, with its balanced proportions, smooth surfaces, and idealised form, is judged beautiful by viewers across many centuries and cultures.
A formal English garden, with its symmetrical hedgerows, ordered flower beds, and geometrical paths, is judged beautiful by those who walk through it.
A Renaissance painting of the Madonna and Child, with its harmonious composition, soft gradations of light, and balanced arrangement of figures, is judged beautiful by visitors to the gallery.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits formal order — that is, symmetry, proportion, and regularity of structure, (ii) O is unified — every part contributes to a coherent whole with nothing superfluous, and (iii) O occasions in the observer a serene, contemplative pleasure free from agitation or disturbance.
conditions: O exhibits formal order — symmetry, proportion, and regularity of structure
O is unified — every part contributes to a coherent whole
O occasions a serene, contemplative pleasure free from agitation
counterexample: Consider a rugged, storm-battered coastline — the cliffs of the west of Ireland, let us say, where the sea has carved the rock into jagged, irregular forms, where the waves crash against the shore in violent disorder, and where the wind drives the spray across headlands that jut out at random angles into the Atlantic. This coastline is beautiful; travellers have testified to its beauty for centuries, and no one who has stood upon those cliffs in a gale would deny it. Yet it exhibits no formal order. There is no symmetry in the arrangement of the rocks, no proportion in the relation of cliff to sea, no regularity of structure; the coastline is as disordered and irregular as anything in nature. It is not unified in the sense that every part contributes to a coherent whole; the scene is chaotic, fragmentary, and resistant to aesthetic comprehension as a single composition. And the pleasure it occasions is not serene or contemplative; it is agitated, exhilarating, tinged with awe and even fear. All three conditions of the definition fail, yet the coastline is beautiful.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Greek statue, the formal garden, the Renaissance painting — were all instances of what might be called classical beauty: objects characterised by order, symmetry, proportion, and unity, which occasion a calm, contemplative pleasure. The definition abstracted these features as essential to beauty. But the background cases were drawn from a single tradition of aesthetic appreciation — the classical tradition, which prizes formal order above all — and the definition mistook the features of that tradition for the features of beauty as such. The storm-battered coastline reveals that beauty may reside in disorder, irregularity, and violence — in what the eighteenth century termed the sublime or the picturesque — and that the pleasure of beauty may be agitated rather than serene. The definition confused one species of beauty with the genus.
passage:
Consider three objects that are widely acknowledged to be beautiful. A Greek marble statue of a young athlete, carved in the fifth century before Christ, displays balanced proportions, smooth surfaces, and an idealised form that has been admired across many centuries and many cultures; the statue is symmetrical, unified, and occasions in the viewer a quiet, contemplative pleasure. A formal English garden, laid out with symmetrical hedgerows, ordered flower beds, and geometrical paths, satisfies the eye by its regularity and coherence; every element contributes to the design, and nothing is superfluous. A Renaissance painting of the Madonna and Child, with its harmonious composition and soft gradations of light, draws the viewer into a state of serene contemplation. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits formal order — symmetry, proportion, and regularity — it is unified, and it occasions in the observer a serene, contemplative pleasure.

But one must consider a rugged, storm-battered coastline — the cliffs of the west of Ireland, where the Atlantic has carved the rock into jagged, irregular forms over millions of years. The cliffs rise sheer from the water at wild angles; the sea crashes against their base with tremendous force, sending columns of spray into the air; the wind howls across headlands that project in no discernible pattern into the grey expanse of ocean. No visitor to this coastline doubts its beauty. Travellers have testified to it for centuries, and the experience of standing upon those cliffs in a winter gale is one of the most powerful aesthetic experiences that nature affords. Yet the coastline exhibits no formal order. There is no symmetry in the arrangement of the rocks, no proportion in the relation of cliff to wave, no regularity in the fractures and fissures that time and weather have inflicted upon the stone. The scene is not unified in the sense that every part contributes to a coherent whole; it is chaotic, fragmentary, vast beyond the capacity of the eye to take in at a single glance. And the pleasure it occasions is not serene or contemplative. It is agitated, exhilarating, shot through with awe and even a species of terror — the terror of standing at the edge of something immense and ungovernable. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were drawn from the classical tradition of formal beauty — statues, gardens, paintings — and the definition abstracted the features of that tradition as essential. The coastline reveals that beauty encompasses the wild and the disordered as well as the symmetrical and the composed, and that the pleasure of beauty may be as turbulent as it is tranquil.

background_cases: A listener sits in a concert hall, attending closely to a string quartet, and is absorbed in the unfolding of the musical argument; she is wholly engaged by the sensuous qualities of the sound and the formal structure of the composition.
A visitor to a gallery stands before a painting, attends to its colours, its composition, and the interplay of light and shadow, and is absorbed in the contemplation of its visual qualities.

A reader settles into an armchair and reads a sonnet with close attention to its imagery, its rhythm, and the development of its argument; he is absorbed in the sensuous and formal properties of the poem.
definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is directed towards the sensuous and formal properties of an object — its colours, sounds, shapes, rhythms, or structures, (ii) E involves sustained, focused attention upon these properties to the exclusion of practical concerns, and (iii) E is occasioned by a work of art — a painting, a musical composition, a poem, or the like.
conditions: E is directed towards sensuous and formal properties of an object
E involves sustained, focused attention to the exclusion of practical concerns
E is occasioned by a work of art
counterexample: Consider the experience of a naturalist who, walking through a wood in early spring, comes upon a glade carpeted with bluebells. He stops, arrested by the sight. The colour — a vivid, luminous blue, deepening to violet in the shadows — fills his visual field. The arrangement of the flowers, neither regular nor random but following the contours of the ground in sweeps and drifts, absorbs his attention. He attends to the play of light through the canopy of young leaves, to the contrast of blue and green, to the delicate forms of the individual blossoms. For several minutes he stands absorbed, his practical concerns — the walk he is taking, the specimens he intended to collect — forgotten. This is, by any ordinary account, an aesthetic experience. Yet it is not occasioned by a work of art. The glade of bluebells is a natural scene, the product of no human intention. The third condition fails, yet the experience is aesthetic.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all experiences occasioned by works of art — a string quartet, a painting, a sonnet — and the definition abstracted the connection to art as essential to aesthetic experience. But the cases were drawn from the narrow domain of the fine arts, and the definition mistook a contingent feature of the examples for a necessary condition of the concept. The glade of bluebells reveals that aesthetic experience may be directed towards natural objects with the same intensity, the same absorption in sensuous and formal properties, and the same exclusion of practical concerns that characterise the experience of art. The definition confused the most culturally prominent occasions of aesthetic experience with the necessary conditions of aesthetic experience itself.
passage:
Consider three experiences that are paradigmatic instances of the aesthetic. A listener sits in a concert hall, attending closely to a performance of a string quartet. She follows the interweaving of the four voices, attends to the quality of the tone, the shaping of phrases, the tension and release of harmonic progression, and is wholly absorbed in the experience. A visitor to a gallery stands before a painting, attends to its colours, its composition, and the interplay of light and shadow, and for a time the world outside the frame ceases to exist for him. A reader settles into a chair and reads a sonnet with close attention to its imagery, its rhythm, and the development of its argument, and is absorbed in the sensuous and formal properties of the language. From these cases one might extract a definition: an experience is aesthetic if and only if it is directed towards sensuous and formal properties, involves sustained attention to the exclusion of practical concerns, and is occasioned by a work of art.

But one must consider the experience of a naturalist who, walking through a wood in early spring, comes upon a glade carpeted with bluebells. He stops, arrested by the sight. The colour is extraordinary — a vivid, luminous blue, shading to violet where the flowers stand in the shadow of the trees — and it fills his visual field with an intensity that he has never encountered in any painting. The arrangement of the flowers is neither regular, as in a garden, nor random, as in a scatter of fallen leaves, but follows the contours of the ground in broad sweeps and drifts that the eye traces with delight. The light filtering through the canopy of young beech leaves falls in patches upon the flowers, creating a complex pattern of illumination and shade. The naturalist attends to these qualities with the same absorption and the same exclusion of practical concerns that the concert-goer brings to the string quartet. For several minutes he stands motionless, his intended walk forgotten, his collecting bag unopened, his practical purposes suspended. His experience has every mark of the aesthetic: it is directed towards sensuous and formal properties, it involves sustained and focused attention, and it excludes practical concerns. Yet it is not occasioned by a work of art. The glade of bluebells is a natural scene, produced by the interaction of soil, climate, and the biology of the bluebell, and no human hand has arranged it. The background cases were drawn from the domain of the fine arts, and the definition abstracted the connection to art as a necessary condition. The bluebells reveal that aesthetic experience is not tethered to art but may be occasioned by any object whose sensuous and formal properties are sufficiently arresting to command sustained, disinterested attention.

background_cases: A classical Greek temple, with its columns in strict Doric proportion, its pediment symmetrically balanced, and its surfaces finished in pale marble, is universally regarded as beautiful.
A formal French garden at Versailles, with its geometrical parterres, clipped hedges, and radiating avenues, is regarded as a paradigm of beauty in landscape design.

A Renaissance portrait by Raphael, with its harmonious arrangement of figure and background, its balanced composition, and its gentle gradations of tone, is held up as an exemplar of pictorial beauty.
A Mozart string quartet, with its symmetrical phrase-structures, its balanced movements, and its transparent textures, is celebrated as a work of supreme musical beauty.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits formal symmetry — its parts are arranged in a balanced, proportional, and regular manner, (ii) O possesses clarity of structure — its organisation is readily apprehensible by the perceiver, and (iii) O produces in the observer a calm, measured pleasure that is free from agitation or excess.
conditions: O exhibits formal symmetry — balanced, proportional, regular arrangement of parts
O possesses clarity of structure — its organisation is readily apprehensible
O produces a calm, measured pleasure free from agitation or excess
counterexample: Consider a late Beethoven string quartet — the Grosse Fuge, let us say. Its structure is dense, turbulent, and resistant to easy comprehension. It is not symmetrical in any obvious sense; its rhythms are jagged, its harmonies harsh, its textures knotted and conflicting. The pleasure it produces, if pleasure is the right word, is not calm or measured but fierce and disturbing. It violates all three conditions. Yet it is beautiful — many musicians regard it as among the most profoundly beautiful works in the repertoire. Its beauty is of a kind that the background cases, with their emphasis on balance and clarity, did not prepare us to recognise.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of classical or formal beauty — works in which balance, symmetry, and clarity of structure are prominent features. The definition extracted from them captured the properties these particular works share, but it mistook the properties of one tradition of beauty for the properties of beauty as such, thereby excluding the turbulent, obscure, and asymmetrical beauty that is equally real.
passage:
Consider four objects that are widely held to be beautiful: a classical Greek temple in Doric proportion, a formal French garden at Versailles, a Renaissance portrait by Raphael, and a Mozart string quartet. What do these objects have in common? Each exhibits formal symmetry — a balanced, proportional, regular arrangement of parts. Each possesses clarity of structure — its organisation is readily apprehensible, even on first encounter. And each produces in the observer a calm, measured pleasure, free from agitation or excess. One might therefore propose that beauty consists in these three properties: formal symmetry, clarity of structure, and the production of calm pleasure.

But the proposal, however well it fits the background cases, proves insufficient when tested against a wider range of instances. Consider a late Beethoven string quartet — the Grosse Fuge, opus 133. Its structure is dense, turbulent, and resistant to easy comprehension even after repeated hearings. It is not symmetrical; its rhythms are jagged, its harmonies harsh, its textures knotted and violently conflicting. The pleasure it produces — if pleasure is the right word — is not calm or measured but fierce, disturbing, and shot through with something approaching anguish. All three conditions are violated. Yet the Grosse Fuge is beautiful; many musicians of the most refined sensibility regard it as among the most profoundly beautiful works in the entire repertoire. What this case reveals is that the background cases from which the definition was drawn were all instances of a particular tradition — the classical tradition of formal beauty — and the definition mistook the properties of that tradition for the properties of beauty as such. An adequate account must recognise that beauty may be turbulent as well as serene, obscure as well as lucid, asymmetrical as well as balanced.

background_cases: The view from the summit of Mont Blanc, with the vast expanse of Alps spreading beneath and the sky pressing close above, produces in the observer a feeling of awe mingled with insignificance that is characteristically described as sublime.

A violent thunderstorm witnessed from the safety of a solid building — the crack of lightning, the rolling thunder, the sheets of rain — produces a mixture of fear and exaltation that is paradigmatically sublime.

Niagara Falls, with its immense volume of water crashing into the gorge below, its roar audible from a great distance, and its mist rising like smoke, is universally cited as an instance of the natural sublime.
definition: An experience E is sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great physical magnitude or force, (ii) E involves the perceiver's awareness of her own physical insignificance relative to the phenomenon, and (iii) E produces a compound feeling in which fear or awe is accompanied by a pleasurable sense of being overwhelmed.
conditions: E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great physical magnitude or force
E involves the perceiver's awareness of her own physical insignificance
E produces a compound feeling of fear or awe accompanied by pleasurable overwhelm
counterexample: Consider the experience of contemplating the infinite series of natural numbers. One apprehends that the sequence never terminates, that beyond every number there is another, and that the totality is without bound. This experience can produce a feeling of awe and a sense of being overwhelmed that closely resembles the sublime. Yet it is not occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great physical magnitude; it is occasioned by an abstract mathematical object. The perceiver's sense of insignificance is not physical but intellectual — it is the mind's recognition of its own finitude in the face of the infinite. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail as stated. Yet the experience is, one might insist, genuinely sublime.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all encounters with large-scale natural phenomena — mountains, storms, waterfalls — and the definition accordingly restricted the sublime to the physically vast. But what the cases genuinely shared was the confrontation with something that exceeds the perceiver's capacity to comprehend or encompass, and this confrontation can occur in the intellectual and mathematical domain as readily as in the natural one.
passage:
Consider three experiences commonly cited as instances of the sublime. First, the view from the summit of Mont Blanc — the vast expanse of peaks, the sky pressing close, the sense of standing at the roof of Europe. Second, a violent thunderstorm witnessed from safety — the crack of lightning, the rolling thunder, the sheets of rain. Third, Niagara Falls — the immense volume of water, the deafening roar, the mist rising like smoke. What these experiences share, one might propose, is that each is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great physical magnitude or force, each involves an awareness of one's own physical insignificance, and each produces a compound feeling in which fear or awe is accompanied by a pleasurable sense of being overwhelmed.

But the definition, however faithful to these particular cases, proves too narrow. Consider the experience of contemplating the infinite series of natural numbers. One apprehends that the sequence never terminates — that beyond every number there is another, and another beyond that, without end. This contemplation can produce a feeling of awe and overwhelm that closely resembles what one feels before Mont Blanc or Niagara. Yet it is not occasioned by any natural phenomenon of physical magnitude. It is occasioned by an abstract mathematical object — the series of natural numbers, which has no physical existence at all. The perceiver's sense of insignificance is not physical but intellectual: it is the mind's recognition of its own finitude in the face of a totality it can never complete or survey. The definition fails because it was extracted from cases that happened to be physical, and it mistook the physical character of those cases for an essential feature of the sublime. What the cases genuinely shared was a confrontation with something that exceeds the perceiver's capacity to comprehend or encompass, and this confrontation may be intellectual as readily as perceptual.

background_cases: Van Gogh's 'The Starry Night,' painted during a period of intense emotional turmoil, is said to express anguish and longing through its swirling, agitated brushwork and vivid colour contrasts.

Schubert's 'Winterreise,' composed during a period of illness and melancholy, expresses despair and loneliness through its spare textures, minor keys, and vocal lines that seem to trail off into silence.
Sylvia Plath's 'Lady Lazarus,' written during a period of profound psychological distress, expresses rage and defiance through its harsh imagery, clipped rhythms, and bitter irony.
definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the creator of W was experiencing E at or near the time of creation, (ii) E is the causal origin of the distinctive formal features of W — the emotion shaped the work's character, and (iii) the formal features of W are such that a sensitive audience can identify E from the work itself, without biographical information of the creator.
conditions: The creator was experiencing E at or near the time of creation
E is the causal origin of the distinctive formal features of W
A sensitive audience can identify E from the work without biographical information
counterexample: Consider the case of Ravel's 'Pavane pour une infante défunte.' The piece expresses a tender, elegiac melancholy — a gentle sorrow suffused with grace. A sensitive listener identifies this emotion without difficulty. Yet Ravel composed the piece not in a state of sorrow but as a technical exercise in antique dance forms. He was, by his own account, interested in the shape of the sarabande and the archaic flavour of the title, not in the expression of personal grief. Condition (i) fails: the composer was not experiencing the emotion the work expresses. Condition (ii) fails: the melancholy of the piece was not caused by any episode of sadness but by Ravel's deliberate deployment of harmonic and melodic conventions associated with elegiac music.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Van Gogh, Schubert, Plath — were all instances in which biographical suffering happened to coincide with expressive works, and the definition treated the biographical fact as essential. But what the works genuinely share is a set of formal properties that are conventionally or naturally associated with particular emotions, and these properties can be produced by craft as readily as by suffering.
passage:
Consider three works of art that are paradigmatically expressive. Van Gogh's 'The Starry Night,' painted during intense emotional turmoil, expresses anguish through swirling brushwork and vivid colour. Schubert's 'Winterreise,' composed during illness and melancholy, expresses despair through spare textures and trailing vocal lines. Plath's 'Lady Lazarus,' written during profound psychological distress, expresses rage through harsh imagery and bitter irony. In each case, the creator was experiencing the emotion the work expresses, that emotion shaped the formal character of the work, and a sensitive audience can identify the emotion without biographical information. One might therefore propose that artistic expression requires this biographical-causal link: the emotion must be felt by the creator and must causally produce the work's distinctive features.

But the proposal proves insufficient when tested against other cases. Consider Ravel's 'Pavane pour une infante défunte.' The piece expresses a tender, elegiac melancholy — a gentle sorrow suffused with grace. Any sensitive listener identifies this character without hesitation. Yet Ravel composed the piece not in a state of sorrow but as a technical exercise in antique dance forms. He was interested, by his own testimony, in the shape of the sarabande and the archaic quality of the title, not in the expression of personal grief. He was in perfectly good spirits. The melancholy of the work was produced not by any episode of sadness but by Ravel's masterful deployment of harmonic progressions, melodic contours, and rhythmic patterns that are conventionally and naturally associated with elegiac feeling. The background cases happened to be instances in which biographical suffering coincided with expressive art, and the definition mistook the coincidence for a necessary connection. What the works genuinely share is a set of formal properties associated with particular emotions, and these properties may be produced by deliberate craft as readily as by lived experience.

background_cases: A Dutch still-life painting of the seventeenth century — a table laden with fruit, a half-peeled lemon, a glass of wine — represents these objects by closely imitating their visual appearance in paint.
A detailed architectural drawing of a cathedral, rendered in precise line and wash, represents the building by reproducing its proportions, elevations, and structural details on paper.
A wax figure in Madame Tussauds represents a famous person by replicating their physical appearance — skin tone, hair, posture, clothing — with painstaking fidelity.
definition: An artefact A represents an object X if and only if (i) A visually resembles X — there is a point-by-point correspondence between the visual properties of A and those of X, (ii) A was produced with the intention of creating a visual likeness of X, and (iii) a competent viewer can identify X by inspecting A alone, without supplementary information.
conditions: A visually resembles X — point-by-point correspondence of visual properties
A was produced with the intention of creating a visual likeness of X
A competent viewer can identify X by inspecting A alone
counterexample: Consider a political cartoon in which a particular statesman is depicted as a bulldog. The cartoon represents the statesman — every reader of the newspaper recognises him — but there is no point-by-point correspondence between the visual properties of the cartoon and those of the man. The cartoonist has exaggerated certain features (the jaw, the frown, the stocky build) and replaced others with canine attributes. Condition (i) fails: the cartoon does not visually resemble the statesman in any precise sense. Yet it represents him, and does so effectively. Representation here works through caricature, exaggeration, and convention rather than through faithful visual likeness.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Dutch still life, the architectural drawing, the wax figure — were all instances of mimetic representation, where the artefact closely copies the appearance of its subject. The definition generalised from this mimetic character, but what the cases genuinely shared was a systematic mapping between artefact and subject that the viewer can decode, and this mapping need not take the form of visual resemblance.
passage:
Consider three artefacts that represent their subjects with evident success. A Dutch still-life painting represents fruit and glassware by closely imitating their visual appearance. A detailed architectural drawing represents a cathedral by reproducing its proportions and structural details. A wax figure at Madame Tussauds represents a famous person by replicating their physical appearance with painstaking fidelity. In each case, representation is achieved through visual resemblance: there is a point-by-point correspondence between the properties of the artefact and those of the subject. One might therefore propose that representation requires such resemblance, together with the intention to create a likeness and the capacity of a competent viewer to identify the subject from the artefact alone.

But the proposal encounters a difficulty when confronted with modes of representation that do not rely on faithful visual copying. Consider a political cartoon in which a particular statesman is depicted as a bulldog. The cartoon represents the statesman — every reader of the newspaper recognises him without hesitation. But there is no point-by-point correspondence between the visual properties of the drawing and those of the man. The cartoonist has exaggerated certain features — the heavy jaw, the stubborn frown, the stocky build — and has replaced the rest with canine attributes: floppy ears, a short tail, four legs. The result does not resemble the man in any precise sense; it resembles a dog. Yet it represents the man, and does so with a vividness that a faithful portrait might not achieve. What the background cases shared was not visual resemblance per se but a systematic mapping between artefact and subject — a mapping that the viewer can decode using the appropriate conventions. In the mimetic cases, the convention is simple: likeness. In the cartoon, the convention is more complex: exaggeration, substitution, and cultural association. An adequate account of representation must accommodate both.

background_cases: A sunset over the sea is widely regarded as beautiful: its colours blend in continuous gradation, its forms are soft and expansive, and it occasions a feeling of quiet contemplation in the observer.
A rose in full bloom is regarded as beautiful: its petals are arranged in a symmetrical spiral, its colours are rich and delicate, and it invites the observer to linger in absorbed attention.

A Greek temple — the Parthenon, say — is regarded as beautiful: its proportions are governed by mathematical ratios, its lines are refined by subtle curvatures, and it produces in the observer a sense of harmony and repose.

A passage of Mozart is regarded as beautiful: its melodic lines are balanced, its harmonies resolve with an air of inevitability, and it occasions in the listener a pleasure that seems inseparable from the perception of formal perfection.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits formal harmony — a proportionate arrangement of its parts, (ii) O occasions in a normally constituted observer a pleasurable feeling of contemplation, and (iii) the pleasure O occasions is calm and serene in character — free from agitation, excitement, or disturbance.
conditions: O exhibits formal harmony and proportionate arrangement
O occasions a pleasurable feeling of contemplation
The pleasure is calm and serene, free from agitation
counterexample: Consider the cathedral of Chartres. Its facade is asymmetrical — the two towers are of different heights and different styles, one Romanesque and one Gothic, built centuries apart. Its stained glass floods the interior with intense, saturated colour that is more overwhelming than serene. A visitor may feel not calm repose but a kind of exalted agitation — a mixture of awe, wonder, and something approaching vertigo. The formal harmony is at best imperfect, and the pleasure, if pleasure it be, is far from calm. Yet Chartres is widely regarded as one of the most beautiful buildings in Europe.
abductive_insight: The background cases — sunset, rose, temple, Mozart — all happened to exhibit a calm and balanced beauty, but the feature that made each beautiful was not the calmness of the pleasure but the capacity of the object to command absorbed attention and to reward that attention with an experience felt to be significant. The definition has abstracted the serenity that characterised the particular examples while dropping the deeper principle — that beauty is the power of an object to engage and reward contemplation, whether the resulting experience is calm or turbulent.
passage:
If one surveys the objects most commonly cited as beautiful — a sunset, a rose, a Greek temple, a passage of Mozart — one finds that each exhibits a certain formal harmony, occasions contemplative pleasure, and produces a feeling that is calm, balanced, and serene. The sunset blends its colours in gentle gradation; the rose unfurls its petals in symmetrical order; the temple disposes its columns in mathematical proportion; the music resolves its harmonies with an air of quiet inevitability. From these cases one might extract the definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits formal harmony, occasions contemplative pleasure, and produces a calm and serene feeling.

Yet this definition, shaped by the particular cases from which it was drawn, proves too narrow. Consider the cathedral of Chartres. Its facade is conspicuously asymmetrical: the two towers differ in height, in style, and in period of construction — one Romanesque, severe and solid; the other Gothic, soaring and ornate. The asymmetry is not a blemish but a feature, giving the facade a dynamic tension that a perfectly symmetrical design would lack. Inside, the stained glass does not produce calm serenity; it floods the interior with colour of an intensity that is closer to overwhelming than to soothing. The visitor may feel not repose but a kind of exalted agitation — an experience that partakes of awe, wonder, and something approaching the vertiginous. The formal harmony, if present at all, is of a kind quite different from the balanced proportions of the Parthenon. And the pleasure, though unmistakable, is far from calm. Yet few would deny that Chartres is beautiful — indeed, magnificently so. The background cases shared the feature of serenity, but what made each beautiful was not the particular character of the pleasure it produced but its capacity to command absorbed attention and to reward that attention with an experience felt to be significant and valuable. The definition has mistaken a feature of the particular examples — their calmness — for a feature of the concept. What is needed is a definition that captures the power of beauty to engage contemplation, without prescribing the emotional tenor of the resulting experience.

background_cases: Michelangelo's David is regarded as a great work of art because it exhibits consummate technical skill in the rendering of the human form, transforms a block of marble into a figure of extraordinary expressive power, and has been admired across centuries as an achievement of the highest order.

Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring is regarded as a great work of art because of its mastery of light and colour, its capacity to convey the inner life of the sitter through the subtlest formal means, and its enduring hold upon the imagination of viewers.

Bach's Mass in B minor is regarded as a great work of art because of its contrapuntal mastery, the depth and range of emotion it expresses through purely musical means, and its capacity to reward repeated listening with ever deeper understanding.
definition: A work of art W is great if and only if (i) W exhibits a high degree of technical mastery — the artist has employed the resources of the medium with exceptional skill, (ii) W possesses expressive depth — it conveys or embodies emotions, ideas, or experiences of significance, and (iii) W has endured — it continues to be admired and valued long after the period of its creation.
conditions: W exhibits a high degree of technical mastery
W possesses expressive depth
W has endured across time
counterexample: Consider a work created very recently — within the last few years — by a young and unknown artist. The work exhibits extraordinary technical mastery; it possesses a depth of expression that strikes every viewer who encounters it; and critics of discernment have pronounced it a work of the first rank. But it has not yet endured across time, for there has been no time for it to endure. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet to deny it the status of greatness on the ground that it is too new would be to make greatness a function of chronology rather than of quality. A work does not become great by ageing; if it is great, it is great from the moment of its creation, and endurance is a consequence of greatness rather than a condition of it.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Michelangelo, Vermeer, Bach — were all works that had endured for centuries, and their endurance was taken as evidence of their greatness. But the endurance was a consequence of the qualities that made them great, not a constituent of those qualities. The definition has elevated a reliable indicator of greatness — survival across time — to the status of a necessary condition, thereby confusing a sign of the thing with the thing itself.
passage:
When one considers the works most universally acknowledged as great — Michelangelo's David, Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring, Bach's Mass in B minor — certain features recur. Each exhibits technical mastery of the highest order: the sculptor's command of marble, the painter's mastery of light, the composer's contrapuntal skill. Each possesses expressive depth: a capacity to convey, through purely formal means, emotions, ideas, and experiences of the most profound significance. And each has endured — surviving changes of taste, the rise and fall of schools and movements, to command admiration centuries after its creation. From these cases one might extract the definition: a work of art is great if and only if it exhibits technical mastery, possesses expressive depth, and has endured across time.

Yet this definition, plausible as it seems, confuses a consequence of greatness with a condition of it. Consider a work created in the present year by an unknown artist — a painting, let us say, that exhibits a technique of extraordinary assurance, that conveys through its imagery and formal organisation an emotional depth that every sensitive viewer finds compelling, and that has been recognised by critics of discernment as a work of remarkable power. The work satisfies the first two conditions amply. But it has not endured across time, for the simple reason that it has only just been made. The third condition fails. Yet to deny the work the status of greatness on this ground alone would be to make a puzzling claim: that a work is not yet great but may become great in a century or two, as though greatness were something that accumulates with age rather than something that inheres in the work from the moment of its creation. The background cases had all endured, but their endurance was not what made them great — it was a consequence of the qualities that made them great. The definition has taken a reliable indicator of greatness and elevated it to the status of a necessary condition, confusing the sign with the thing signified. What is needed is a definition that identifies the intrinsic qualities of greatness without requiring the passage of time to confirm them.

background_cases: The sight of an Alpine peak, its summit lost in cloud, its flanks scarred by avalanche, occasions in the observer a feeling of insignificance before the vastness of nature, accompanied by a peculiar exaltation — as though the mind, confronted by what exceeds it, discovers in itself a capacity to comprehend what it cannot contain.

A violent storm at sea, observed from the safety of the shore, occasions a mixture of terror and fascination — the waves are destructive, the wind is deafening, yet the observer feels not simply afraid but elevated, as though the spectacle has enlarged the boundaries of experience.

The night sky in a place free from artificial light, with its innumerable stars and the faint luminous band of the Milky Way, occasions a feeling of awe that is at once humbling and expansive — the observer is diminished by the vastness of the cosmos and yet, in the very act of apprehending that vastness, feels the reach of the mind extended.
definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great magnitude — a mountain, a storm, a starry sky, or the like, (ii) E involves a tension between feelings of insignificance and feelings of exaltation, and (iii) E is undergone in conditions of safety — the observer is not in actual danger.
conditions: E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great magnitude
E involves tension between insignificance and exaltation
E is undergone in conditions of safety
counterexample: Consider a soldier in the midst of battle who, in a moment of strange clarity, perceives the vastness and terror of the scene around him — the thunder of artillery, the sweep of destruction across a wide landscape — and experiences, alongside his fear, a feeling of exaltation and an acute awareness of the insignificance of his individual existence against the scale of events. The experience involves a natural (or at least non-artificial) phenomenon of great magnitude; it involves the characteristic tension between insignificance and exaltation. But the soldier is not safe. He is in mortal danger. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the experience, as described, has the phenomenological character of the sublime — the distinctive mixture of terror and elevation that the background cases exhibit. The definition excludes it merely because the observer happens to be in danger.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Alpine peak, the storm at sea, the night sky — all involved observers in positions of safety, and the safety was taken as a condition of the sublime. But the safety was a feature of the circumstances in which those particular observers happened to experience the sublime, not a feature of the sublime itself. What the background cases had in common was not the observer's physical situation but the phenomenological character of the experience — the distinctive tension between terror and exaltation. The definition has confused a circumstantial feature of the paradigm cases with a necessary condition of the concept.
passage:
The experiences most commonly cited as instances of the sublime share certain features. The Alpine peak, with its cloud-wrapped summit, occasions a feeling of human insignificance alongside a strange exaltation. The violent storm at sea, observed from the shore, mingles terror with fascination. The night sky, in its immensity, humbles and enlarges the observer in the same moment. In each case, the object is a natural phenomenon of great magnitude; in each case, the experience involves a tension between feelings of smallness and feelings of elevation; and in each case, the observer is physically safe — watching the storm from the shore, contemplating the mountain from a valley, gazing at the sky from solid ground. From these cases one might extract the definition: an experience is sublime if and only if it is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great magnitude, involves the tension between insignificance and exaltation, and is undergone in conditions of safety.

Yet this definition, shaped by the comfortable circumstances of the paradigmatic observers, proves too restrictive. Consider a soldier who, in the midst of a bombardment, experiences a moment of extraordinary lucidity. The thunder of artillery shakes the earth; destruction stretches across a landscape of terrible grandeur; and the soldier, aware of his own smallness against the scale of events, feels not merely terror but something that partakes of exaltation — a heightened awareness, an expansion of perception, a sense that the boundaries of ordinary experience have been burst. The phenomenological character of this experience matches the background cases precisely: the same tension between insignificance and elevation, the same mixture of terror and a strange enlargement of the spirit. But the soldier is not safe. He is in mortal danger. The definition excludes his experience on the sole ground that he lacks the physical security that the background observers happened to enjoy. Yet what those observers shared with the soldier was not safety but the character of the experience itself — the distinctive phenomenology of the sublime. The definition has mistaken a circumstantial feature of the paradigm cases for a necessary condition. What is needed is a definition that identifies the sublime by its phenomenological signature rather than by the physical situation of the observer.

background_cases: A Dutch still life of the seventeenth century — a table laden with fruit, a half-peeled lemon, a glass of wine — represents its subject by closely imitating the visual appearance of the objects depicted: the viewer recognises the fruit, the lemon, the glass, because the painting looks like them.
A portrait by Gainsborough represents its sitter by reproducing, with painterly skill, the sitter's features, posture, and dress, so that a viewer who knows the sitter would recognise the likeness.

A landscape by Constable represents the English countryside by faithfully rendering the forms of clouds, trees, and water in a manner that enables the viewer to identify the scene as a depiction of a particular place.
definition: A painting P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P visually resembles S — a viewer looking at P would recognise a likeness to S, (ii) the artist intended P to depict S, and (iii) the resemblance between P and S is the result of the artist's deliberate exercise of representational skill.
conditions: P visually resembles S
The artist intended P to depict S
The resemblance is the result of deliberate representational skill
counterexample: Consider Picasso's Guernica. The painting was intended to depict the bombing of the town of Guernica during the Spanish Civil War. The artist's intention is not in doubt. And there are recognisable figures in the painting — a horse, a bull, a screaming woman. But the painting does not visually resemble the bombing of a town. The forms are fragmented, distorted, and rearranged according to principles that have nothing to do with visual likeness. A viewer who did not know the title might not identify the painting as a depiction of a bombing at all. Condition (i) is at best marginally satisfied, and condition (iii) — that the resemblance result from representational skill — fails, because the painting's power lies precisely in its departure from resemblance. Yet Guernica is one of the most powerful representations of war in the history of painting.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Dutch still life, Gainsborough, Constable — all achieved representation through visual resemblance, and the resemblance was the product of the artist's skill in imitating appearances. But what made these paintings successful representations was not the resemblance per se but the capacity of each painting to make the viewer see and understand something about its subject. The definition has elevated one method of achieving this — visual imitation — to the status of a necessary condition, when in fact representation may be achieved through distortion, abstraction, and expressive transformation of visual form.
passage:
The tradition of Western painting from the Renaissance to the nineteenth century furnishes many examples of representation through visual resemblance. A Dutch still life imitates the look of fruit and glassware with astonishing fidelity. A Gainsborough portrait reproduces the sitter's features so that recognition is immediate. A Constable landscape renders the English countryside with a faithfulness that enables the viewer to identify the very field and sky. From these cases one might extract the definition: a painting represents its subject if and only if it visually resembles the subject, the artist intended the depiction, and the resemblance results from deliberate representational skill.
