Yet this definition, shaped by the mimetic tradition, proves too narrow when applied to the full range of pictorial representation. Consider Picasso's Guernica. The painting was intended to depict the bombing of the town of Guernica — the artist's intention is explicit and unambiguous. But the painting does not visually resemble the bombing of a town. Its forms are violently distorted: the horse screams with a tongue like a dagger; the bull stares with dislocated eyes; the figures are fragmented and reassembled according to principles of expressive intensity rather than visual accuracy. A viewer unfamiliar with the title might struggle to identify the subject. The resemblance condition fails, and the painting's representational power lies not in imitation but in distortion — in the capacity of fragmented and re-ordered forms to convey the horror and chaos of aerial bombardment more forcefully than any faithful rendering could. The background cases achieved representation through likeness, but what made them successful was not the likeness itself — it was the capacity of the painting to make the viewer see and understand something about its subject. The definition has confused one means of representation with representation itself. A more adequate account would recognise that a painting may represent its subject through expressive distortion, symbolic abbreviation, or formal analogy, as well as through visual resemblance.

background_cases: A sunset over the sea is widely regarded as beautiful: it possesses a rich harmony of colours, its contemplation is valued for its own sake, and the pleasure it affords arises from the interplay of light and form rather than from any practical interest.

A well-proportioned Greek temple is considered beautiful: the ratios of its columns and pediment exhibit a formal harmony, the pleasure of contemplation is disinterested, and the observer's delight is grounded in the perceptible structure of the building.

A passage of Mozart is heard as beautiful: the melodic and harmonic relations are intricate yet balanced, the listener's pleasure is disinterested, and the beauty is located in the audible qualities of the music rather than in any extraneous association.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a unity of formal elements that can be apprehended by the senses, (ii) the contemplation of O yields a disinterested pleasure, and (iii) the pleasure is grounded in the object's perceptible properties rather than in associations or practical uses.
conditions: O exhibits a unity of formal elements that can be apprehended by the senses
The contemplation of O yields a disinterested pleasure
The pleasure is grounded in the object's perceptible properties rather than in associations or practical uses
counterexample: Consider a wallpaper pattern of perfect geometrical regularity. It exhibits a clear unity of formal elements apprehensible by the senses. Its contemplation yields a certain disinterested pleasure — the viewer has no practical stake in the pattern. And the pleasure is grounded in the pattern's perceptible properties. Yet the wallpaper does not strike us as beautiful; it is merely neat or orderly.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the wallpaper: in each, the formal unity is of a kind that rewards sustained attention and unfolds further relations upon continued contemplation. The wallpaper's unity is exhausted at a glance. Beauty, it appears, requires not merely formal unity but a unity of sufficient depth to engage the contemplative faculties over time.
passage:
The cases of the sunset, the temple, and the Mozart passage invite us to suppose that beauty consists in a perceptible formal unity that yields disinterested pleasure grounded in the object's sensory properties. Each case satisfies these three conditions, and in each case the attribution of beauty seems well warranted. One might therefore feel confident that the definition captures what it is for an object to be beautiful.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is readily available. A wallpaper pattern of perfect geometrical regularity — a repeating tessellation, let us say, in harmonious colours — exhibits a unity of formal elements, yields a pleasure that is disinterested, and grounds this pleasure in its perceptible properties. All three conditions are met. But we should hesitate to call the wallpaper beautiful. What distinguishes the sunset and the temple and the Mozart from the wallpaper, one might observe, is the depth of the formal unity in question. The sunset's beauty unfolds over time as the light changes; the temple's proportions reward prolonged study; the Mozart passage reveals new harmonic subtleties upon each hearing. The wallpaper's regularity, by contrast, is exhausted at a glance. The definition, as stated, treats all formal unities alike, but beauty appears to demand a unity that sustains and deepens the contemplative engagement rather than one that is immediately transparent and thereafter inert.

background_cases: Standing at the rim of a vast canyon, the observer is initially overwhelmed by the immensity of the scene and feels her own insignificance; this feeling is then succeeded by an exhilarating sense of the mind's power to encompass what physically overpowers it.

Witnessing a violent thunderstorm from a place of safety, a person first feels the terror of nature's destructive force, then experiences an elevation that arises from the awareness that her rational capacities are not themselves destroyed by what terrifies her senses.

Contemplating the starry sky on a cloudless night, a person is first oppressed by the incomprehensible vastness of the universe, then feels an exaltation grounded in the recognition that she can conceive of this vastness even though she cannot physically encompass it.
definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that exceeds the subject's capacity for sensory comprehension, (ii) E involves an initial affective response of awe, terror, or helplessness, and (iii) E gives rise to a subsequent feeling of elevation or exaltation.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon that exceeds the subject's capacity for sensory comprehension
E involves an initial affective response of awe, terror, or helplessness
E gives rise to a subsequent feeling of elevation or exaltation
counterexample: Consider a soldier who, caught in a bombardment, is initially overwhelmed by the magnitude and destructive power of the explosions — a phenomenon that plainly exceeds his capacity for sensory comprehension. He experiences terror and helplessness. Then, as the bombardment ceases, he feels a rush of exaltation at having survived. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to describe his experience as an experience of the sublime; it is more accurately characterised as relief.
abductive_insight: In the background cases, the subsequent exaltation arises from a reflective awareness of the mind's own capacities in relation to what overwhelms the senses — a turning inward that is contemplative rather than practical. The soldier's exaltation, by contrast, arises from the practical fact of his survival. The sublime, it appears, requires that the exaltation be reflective and contemplative in origin, not merely the relief of having escaped danger.
passage:
The canyon, the thunderstorm, and the starry sky each occasion an experience with a distinctive two-phase structure: an initial feeling of being overwhelmed, followed by a subsequent exaltation. In each case, the object exceeds the subject's sensory capacities, the initial response is one of awe or helplessness, and the subsequent feeling is one of elevation. One might therefore define the sublime as any experience possessing this structure, requiring only that the occasion exceed sensory comprehension, that the initial affect be one of awe or terror, and that an exaltation follow.

Yet consider a soldier caught in a bombardment. The magnitude of the explosions exceeds his capacity for sensory comprehension; he feels terror and helplessness; and when the bombardment ceases, he experiences a powerful exaltation. All three conditions are satisfied. But one would be reluctant to describe his experience as sublime. The exaltation he feels is the relief of survival — a reaction to the removal of danger rather than a contemplative response to the apprehension of magnitude. In the background cases, by contrast, the exaltation arises from a reflective awareness: the observer recognises that her rational capacities are adequate to what physically overpowers her, and this recognition is itself the source of elevation. The definition, as formulated, cannot distinguish between the soldier's relief and the stargazer's reflective exaltation, for it specifies the phenomenological arc without specifying the character of the exaltation that completes it. It appears that a further condition is needed: the exaltation must be contemplative in origin, arising from the subject's awareness of her own cognitive relation to the overwhelming object rather than from any practical deliverance from danger.

background_cases: Michelangelo's David is a work of art: it was produced by a skilled human agent with the intention of creating an object for contemplation, it is embedded in a tradition of sculptural practice, and it has been received by audiences as meriting sustained aesthetic attention.

Beethoven's Fifth Symphony is a work of art: it was composed with consummate skill and artistic intention, it stands within a tradition of symphonic composition, and audiences have valued it as an object of aesthetic engagement for over two centuries.

Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring is a work of art: it was painted with extraordinary skill and artistic intent, it belongs to a tradition of Dutch portraiture, and it has been received as a work of profound aesthetic merit.
definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is produced by a human agent exercising skill in a recognised medium, (ii) A is produced with the intention that it be an object of aesthetic contemplation, and (iii) A is received by a community of appreciators as meriting sustained aesthetic attention.
conditions: A is produced by a human agent exercising skill in a recognised medium
A is produced with the intention that it be an object of aesthetic contemplation
A is received by a community of appreciators as meriting sustained aesthetic attention
counterexample: Consider a highly skilled forger who produces a painting indistinguishable from a lost Vermeer. He exercises consummate skill in the recognised medium of oil painting. He intends the painting to be an object of aesthetic contemplation — he wants it to be admired and valued. And it is received by the art world as meriting sustained aesthetic attention, at least until the forgery is discovered. All three conditions are met. Yet upon discovery of the deception, we should hesitate to call the forgery a work of art in its own right, for its aesthetic identity is parasitic upon the original it imitates.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the forgery: each work makes an original contribution to the tradition in which it stands, extending or transforming the possibilities of its medium. The forgery, however skilfully executed, merely replicates an existing achievement. It appears that being a work of art requires not only skill, intention, and reception, but a measure of originality — a contribution to the ongoing practice of art that is genuinely the artist's own.
passage:
Michelangelo's David, Beethoven's Fifth Symphony, and Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring are paradigmatic works of art, and they share certain salient features: each was produced by a skilled human agent working in a recognised medium, each was created with the intention of being an object of aesthetic contemplation, and each has been received by a community of appreciators as meriting sustained aesthetic attention. A definition constructed from these common features appears to capture the concept of a work of art with considerable precision.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this tripartite account can be constructed. Imagine a forger of extraordinary skill who produces a painting indistinguishable from a lost Vermeer. He works in oil on canvas — a recognised medium — and exercises a degree of technical skill that equals Vermeer's own. He intends the painting to be admired and contemplated. And so long as the forgery remains undetected, it is received by the art world with all the sustained attention that a genuine Vermeer commands. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But when the deception is uncovered, our inclination is to deny that the forgery is a work of art in its own right. What it lacks, one might observe, is any original contribution to the tradition of painting. The background cases each extend or transform the possibilities of their respective media; the forgery merely replicates an existing achievement. The definition must, it seems, be supplemented with a condition requiring that the artefact make some original contribution to the practice of art.

background_cases: A portrait of Queen Victoria represents her by bearing a visual resemblance to her appearance: the shapes and colours on the canvas correspond to the shapes and colours of her face and figure, and a viewer acquainted with Victoria would recognise the likeness.

A landscape painting of the Lake District represents its subject by reproducing the spatial arrangement of hills, water, and sky as they would appear from a particular vantage point; the resemblance enables a viewer to identify the depicted scene.

A still-life painting of fruit in a bowl represents its subject by depicting the characteristic shapes, textures, and colours of the fruit and the bowl, enabling a viewer to recognise what is portrayed.
definition: A picture P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P bears a perceptible resemblance to S, (ii) P was made with the intention of depicting S, and (iii) a suitably informed viewer can identify S by inspecting P.
conditions: P bears a perceptible resemblance to S
P was made with the intention of depicting S
A suitably informed viewer can identify S by inspecting P
counterexample: Consider a caricature of a well-known politician in which the nose is exaggerated to three times its actual size, the chin is sharpened to a point, and the ears are drawn as enormous flaps. The caricature does not bear a perceptible resemblance to the politician in any straightforward sense — it systematically distorts rather than reproduces his features. Yet the caricature was made with the intention of depicting the politician, and any viewer acquainted with him would identify him immediately. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied while condition (i) fails, yet the caricature plainly represents the politician.
abductive_insight: The background cases rely upon resemblance because they are instances of naturalistic depiction. But the caricature represents its subject not by resembling him but by selectively exaggerating features that are already salient in his appearance. What the background cases and the caricature share is not resemblance per se but the exploitation of recognisable visual cues — a broader category that includes both faithful reproduction and systematic distortion. The definition must be revised to accommodate representational strategies that go beyond resemblance.
passage:
The portrait, the landscape, and the still life suggest a straightforward account of pictorial representation: a picture represents its subject by bearing a perceptible resemblance to it, being made with the intention of depicting it, and enabling a suitably informed viewer to identify it. In each background case, the resemblance between picture and subject is central to the representational success: the portrait looks like Victoria, the landscape reproduces the spatial arrangement of the Lake District, and the still life replicates the appearance of fruit in a bowl.

Yet consider a political caricature in which the subject's nose is exaggerated to three times its actual size, the chin is sharpened to a needle point, and the ears are drawn as enormous flaps. The caricature does not resemble the politician in any straightforward way — it systematically distorts his features rather than reproducing them. Condition (i) of the definition is not satisfied. Yet the caricature unambiguously represents the politician: it was made with the intention of depicting him, and any viewer acquainted with him would identify him at a glance. The difficulty is that the definition ties representation to resemblance, whereas the caricature represents its subject by exploiting recognisable visual cues through exaggeration rather than imitation. What the background cases and the caricature share is not resemblance in the narrow sense but the use of visual features that enable recognition. The definition must be broadened to accommodate representational strategies — including distortion, exaggeration, and schematisation — that achieve recognition without relying upon perceptible resemblance.

background_cases: The beauty of a Greek temple is typically attributed to its proportional harmony — the precise ratios of column height to diameter, of entablature to colonnade — such that every part stands in a measured relation to every other and to the whole.

The beauty of a Renaissance painting by Raphael is attributed to the balanced arrangement of figures, the graceful correspondence of colour and line, and the subordination of every detail to the unity of the composition.

The beauty of a Mozart piano concerto is attributed to the symmetry of its phrases, the clarity of its melodic lines, and the way in which each musical element supports the coherence of the formal structure.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) the parts of O stand in proportional and harmonious relations to one another and to the whole, (ii) the perception of O produces a disinterested pleasure in a suitably attentive observer, and (iii) the pleasure is grounded in the apprehension of the formal relations among the parts.
conditions: The parts of O stand in proportional and harmonious relations
Perception of O produces disinterested pleasure
The pleasure is grounded in apprehension of formal relations
counterexample: Consider a night sky in which the stars are scattered with no discernible pattern — no symmetry, no proportion, no formal structure that the eye can organise into a harmonious whole. The distribution is, so far as the observer can tell, entirely random. Yet the observer, gazing upward in a state of disinterested attention, experiences a deep and contemplative pleasure. She would call the sky beautiful, and her judgement commands wide assent. The first and third conditions are not satisfied: there are no proportional relations among the parts, and the pleasure is not grounded in the apprehension of any formal order.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Greek architecture, Renaissance painting, Mozartean music — all derive their beauty from an internal formal order that the observer can apprehend and take pleasure in. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement of proportional harmony as the ground of aesthetic pleasure. But the starry sky presents beauty without order — its power lies in the vastness and irregularity that overwhelm any attempt to apprehend formal relations, suggesting that beauty may sometimes arise from the felt absence of the very structure the definition demands.
passage:
The cases most commonly cited as paradigms of beauty share a structural feature that invites generalisation. The Greek temple is admired for its proportional harmony: every column, every moulding, stands in a precise ratio to the whole. The Raphael Madonna is praised for the balanced arrangement of its figures, the measured correspondence of colour and line. The Mozart concerto delights through the symmetry of its phrases and the transparency of its formal architecture. In each case, beauty appears to consist in the apprehension of a proportional order among the parts of an object, and the pleasure that the observer derives from this apprehension.

One might therefore define beauty by requiring that the parts stand in proportional and harmonious relations, that the perception produce disinterested pleasure, and that the pleasure be grounded in the apprehension of these formal relations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the contemplation of the night sky. On a clear evening, far from the illumination of cities, the stars are scattered across the heavens in no discernible pattern. There is no symmetry, no proportion, no formal structure that the eye can organise into a coherent whole. The distribution is, so far as the observer can tell, entirely random. And yet the observer, gazing upward in a state of disinterested attention, may experience a pleasure as deep and contemplative as any produced by a Greek temple or a Mozart concerto. She would call the sky beautiful, and few would dispute her judgement. The first condition is not met — there are no proportional relations among the stars — and the third condition fails as well, for the pleasure is not grounded in the apprehension of formal order. It is grounded, rather, in the felt immensity and inexhaustibility of the spectacle. What the background cases all possessed was an internal formal order that served as the ground of pleasure; the definition mistakes this recurrent feature for a necessary condition, when beauty may sometimes arise from the overwhelming absence of such order.

background_cases: Michelangelo's David is regarded as a work of art because it was intentionally created by a skilled sculptor to embody an aesthetic vision, and was presented to the public as an object for contemplation and admiration.

Beethoven's Fifth Symphony is regarded as a work of art because it was deliberately composed by a master of musical form to express a coherent artistic conception, and was offered to audiences in the context of concert performance.

Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring is regarded as a work of art because it was painted with extraordinary technical skill and artistic purpose, and has been displayed and admired in the context of gallery and museum exhibition.
definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is intentionally produced by a human creator, (ii) the creator possesses and exercises a significant degree of skill in the production of O, and (iii) O is made with the purpose of being an object of aesthetic contemplation.
conditions: O is intentionally produced by a human creator
The creator exercises significant skill
O is made for the purpose of aesthetic contemplation
counterexample: Consider Marcel Duchamp's submission of a mass-produced porcelain urinal, signed with a pseudonym and titled 'Fountain,' to an exhibition in 1917. Duchamp did not produce the urinal; it was manufactured in a factory. He exercised no traditional artistic skill in its selection. And the purpose of the submission was not to furnish an object for serene aesthetic contemplation but to provoke a re-examination of the very concept of art. Yet 'Fountain' is now regarded by critics, historians, and institutions as one of the most significant works of art of the twentieth century. None of the three conditions is met in any straightforward sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve objects created through the intentional exercise of skill for the purpose of aesthetic contemplation — the sculptor carving marble, the composer organising tones, the painter layering pigments. The definition abstracts from these cases a triad of necessary conditions. But Duchamp's readymade reveals that the institutional and conceptual context in which an object is presented may be sufficient to confer the status of art, even when intentional creation, skill, and the purpose of contemplation are all absent in their traditional forms.
passage:
When we survey the objects most securely regarded as works of art, a common structure presents itself. Michelangelo's David was intentionally created by a sculptor of supreme skill for the purpose of aesthetic contemplation. Beethoven's Fifth Symphony was deliberately composed by a master of musical form for performance before an attentive audience. Vermeer's Girl with a Pearl Earring was painted with extraordinary technique and displayed for the admiration of viewers. In each case, we find intentional human production, the exercise of significant skill, and the purpose of aesthetic contemplation.

One might therefore define a work of art as an object intentionally produced by a human creator who exercises significant skill and whose purpose is aesthetic contemplation. This definition has the merit of excluding natural objects, accidental products, and utilitarian artefacts. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by a celebrated episode in the history of twentieth-century art. In 1917, Marcel Duchamp acquired a mass-produced porcelain urinal from a plumbing supply company, signed it with a pseudonym, titled it 'Fountain,' and submitted it to an exhibition. Duchamp did not produce the urinal — it was factory-made. He exercised no traditional artistic skill in selecting it — any person could have purchased the same object. And the purpose of the submission was not to furnish a thing of beauty for serene contemplation but to challenge and subvert the prevailing assumptions about what art is. None of the three conditions is met in any straightforward manner. Yet 'Fountain' has come to be regarded, by critics and art historians of virtually every persuasion, as one of the most consequential works of the twentieth century. What the background cases share — intentional creation, skill, contemplative purpose — turns out to be a feature of a particular tradition of art-making rather than a necessary condition of art as such. The readymade reveals that institutional context and conceptual framing may suffice to confer the status of art upon an object that satisfies none of the conditions abstracted from the paradigm cases.

background_cases: The sight of a violent thunderstorm over open sea occasions the sublime because the observer is confronted with a natural force that exceeds her physical powers, and yet she is safely removed from danger, so that the experience of her own vulnerability gives way to an exhilarating sense of the mind's capacity to comprehend what the body cannot withstand.

The contemplation of a vast mountain range occasions the sublime because the sheer scale of the landscape overwhelms the senses, and the observer's initial feeling of insignificance is succeeded by an awareness of the mind's power to grasp the infinite.

Standing at the edge of a great waterfall occasions the sublime because the ceaseless torrent of water exceeds what the imagination can fully take in, and the observer's feeling of being dwarfed gives way to an elevating consciousness of her own rational nature.
definition: An experience E is sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E involves an initial phase of felt inadequacy or diminishment in the observer, and (iii) E is followed by a compensating phase of elevation in which the observer becomes aware of capacities that transcend the merely sensory.
conditions: E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude or power
E involves an initial phase of felt inadequacy
E is followed by a compensating elevation of mind
counterexample: Consider a person reading Milton's Paradise Lost who encounters the passage describing Satan's flight through Chaos. There is no natural phenomenon — only words on a page. The reader is not confronted with any physical magnitude or power. Yet the passage occasions an experience that many readers would unhesitatingly call sublime: the imagination is stretched to its limits by the vastness of the scene described, and there is a felt elevation of mind in grasping the scope of Milton's conception. The first condition is entirely unmet — the occasion is an artistic artefact, not a natural phenomenon.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve natural phenomena — storms, mountains, waterfalls — whose physical magnitude overwhelms the senses and triggers the characteristic sublime sequence of diminishment followed by elevation. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement that the occasion be a natural phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude. But the Miltonic sublime reveals that art and literature can occasion the same sequence of felt inadequacy and compensating elevation through the power of imaginative representation alone, without any natural phenomenon being present.
passage:
The paradigm experiences of the sublime appear to share a common structure rooted in the encounter with natural phenomena of overpowering magnitude. The thunderstorm over open sea, the vast mountain range, the great waterfall — each confronts the observer with a force or scale that exceeds the capacity of the senses, producing an initial feeling of inadequacy or diminishment that gives way to an elevating awareness of the mind's power to transcend mere sensation. In each case, it is a natural phenomenon — something physical, vast, and overwhelming — that triggers the experience.

One might therefore define the sublime as an experience occasioned by a natural phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude, involving an initial phase of felt inadequacy and a subsequent phase of compensating elevation. This definition captures the structure of the paradigm cases with considerable fidelity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the experience of reading certain works of literature. Consider a reader who encounters Milton's description of Satan's flight through Chaos in Paradise Lost. There is no natural phenomenon here — only ink on paper. The reader is not physically confronted with any vast or powerful object. Yet the passage may occasion an experience that the reader would unhesitatingly call sublime. The imagination is stretched to its limits by the immensity of the scene depicted — the boundless void, the warring elements, the solitary figure traversing an abyss of inconceivable extent. There is a felt inadequacy in the attempt to compass such a vision, and a compensating elevation in the very act of grasping it. The definition's requirement that the sublime be occasioned by a natural phenomenon excludes the literary, musical, and artistic sublime — a tradition recognised since antiquity. What the background cases share is not the natural origin of the stimulus but the cognitive structure of the response: the felt inadequacy before something that exceeds ordinary comprehension, followed by the elevating awareness of a capacity equal to the challenge.

background_cases: Listening to a Bach cello suite in a quiet room, with no purpose beyond the hearing itself, produces an aesthetic experience characterised by sustained attention to the tonal qualities, phrasing, and architecture of the music — an experience valued for its own sake.

Contemplating a Cezanne still life in a gallery, attending to the interplay of colour, form, and brushwork without any ulterior motive, produces an aesthetic experience in which the viewer's attention is absorbed by the sensory and formal properties of the painting.

Reading a sonnet by Shakespeare with full attention to its rhythm, imagery, and verbal texture, and valuing the reading as an end in itself, produces an aesthetic experience that is focused upon the literary qualities of the text.
definition: An experience E is aesthetic if and only if (i) E is characterised by sustained and focused attention to the sensory or formal properties of an object, (ii) E is valued as an end in itself rather than as a means to any further purpose, and (iii) E is affectively engaged — that is, the object arouses a felt response in the observer.
conditions: E involves sustained attention to sensory or formal properties
E is valued as an end in itself
E is affectively engaged
counterexample: Consider an architect who examines a building with sustained attention to its formal properties — the proportions, the rhythm of its fenestration, the relationship between structure and ornament. She is affectively engaged: the building arouses in her a felt response of admiration and delight. But she does not value the experience as an end in itself; she is examining the building in order to learn from it for a commission of her own. Her attention is aesthetic in character, yet the experience serves an instrumental purpose. The second condition is not met, even though the phenomenological character of her experience is indistinguishable from that of a visitor who examines the same building purely for pleasure.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve experiences that are purely non-instrumental — the listener, the gallery visitor, and the reader attend to their respective objects with no purpose beyond the experience itself. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement that the experience be valued as an end in itself. But the architect's case reveals that the phenomenological core of aesthetic experience — sustained attention, sensitivity to formal properties, affective engagement — may be present even when the experience also serves a practical end, suggesting that the non-instrumental condition is too restrictive.
passage:
The experiences most readily identified as aesthetic share a constellation of features that invites codification. Listening to a Bach cello suite with no purpose beyond the hearing, one attends to the tonal qualities and formal architecture of the music and values the experience for its own sake. Contemplating a Cezanne still life, one is absorbed by the interplay of colour and form and finds the absorption intrinsically rewarding. Reading a Shakespeare sonnet, one attends to rhythm and imagery and values the reading as an end in itself. In each case, the experience involves sustained attention to sensory or formal properties, is valued for its own sake, and is affectively engaged.

One might therefore define aesthetic experience by requiring sustained attention to sensory or formal properties, intrinsic valuation, and affective engagement. This definition captures the spirit of the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises from the professional practice of the arts. Consider an architect who visits a recently completed building in order to study its design for a commission of her own. She examines the building with sustained and focused attention to its formal properties — the proportions of its facade, the rhythm of its windows, the relationship between structure and ornament. She is affectively engaged: the building arouses in her a genuine response of admiration. Yet she does not value the experience as an end in itself. She is there to learn, to extract principles she can apply in her own work. The experience is instrumental. Nevertheless, its phenomenological character is indistinguishable from that of a tourist who examines the same building purely for pleasure. The sustained attention, the sensitivity to form, the affective response — all are present. The definition's insistence that the experience be valued as an end in itself excludes the architect's experience, but the exclusion seems arbitrary, for the aesthetic character of the attending is the same in both cases. The non-instrumental condition, abstracted from the paradigm cases, turns out to be a common accompaniment rather than a necessary feature of aesthetic experience.

background_cases: The beauty of a Greek temple is widely attributed to the proportional relations among its columns, entablature, and pediment — the ratios are determinate, the symmetry is exact, and the beauty is felt to reside in the mathematical order that governs the arrangement of parts.

The beauty of a Mozart piano sonata is attributed to the balance and proportion of its phrases, the symmetry of its formal structure, and the clarity with which its melodic ideas are developed — each part stands in a satisfying relation to every other.

The beauty of a well-tended formal garden — its geometric beds, its clipped hedges, its gravel paths radiating from a central fountain — is attributed to the orderly arrangement of its elements, the symmetry of the design, and the sense that every feature occupies its appointed place.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits proportion and symmetry in the arrangement of its parts, (ii) the arrangement produces a feeling of satisfaction or repose in a competent observer, and (iii) the satisfaction is attributable to the perception of the proportional relations themselves.
conditions: O exhibits proportion and symmetry
The arrangement produces satisfaction or repose
The satisfaction is attributable to perceived proportional relations
counterexample: Consider a Japanese rock garden of the Ryoan-ji type, consisting of fifteen stones arranged on a bed of raked gravel. The arrangement is deliberately asymmetrical: no two stones are the same size, the groupings are irregular, and the spacing follows no discernible mathematical ratio. A competent observer experiences a profound satisfaction upon contemplating the garden, but this satisfaction cannot be attributed to the perception of proportional relations, since there are none of the kind the definition requires. The beauty of the garden arises from the tension between its elements, from the suggestion of naturalness within an enclosed space, and from a quality that resists analysis in terms of proportion.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Greek temple, the Mozart sonata, the formal garden — are all instances of beauty achieved through mathematical order and symmetry. The definition has been constructed from these cases and captures their common structure faithfully. But the Ryoan-ji garden achieves beauty through asymmetry, irregularity, and the deliberate avoidance of proportional relations, revealing that the definition has mistaken one aesthetic strategy for a necessary condition of beauty itself.
passage:
When one examines the objects most frequently cited as beautiful in the Western tradition, a striking commonality presents itself. The Greek temple achieves its beauty through the proportional relations among columns, entablature, and pediment — ratios that were studied and codified by ancient architects. The Mozart piano sonata achieves its beauty through the symmetrical balance of its phrases and the clarity of its formal architecture. The formal garden achieves its beauty through geometric precision: clipped hedges, radiating paths, a central fountain that anchors the composition. In each case, proportion and symmetry are the governing principles, and the beauty is felt to reside in the mathematical order that unifies the parts.

One might codify these observations in the following definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits proportion and symmetry in the arrangement of its parts, the arrangement produces satisfaction in a competent observer, and the satisfaction is attributable to the perception of proportional relations. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases with considerable fidelity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the Japanese rock garden at Ryoan-ji. The garden consists of fifteen stones arranged on a rectangular bed of raked white gravel. The arrangement is deliberately asymmetrical — the stones are grouped in clusters of five, two, three, two, and three, with no discernible mathematical ratio governing the spacing. No two stones are the same size or shape. The competent observer who contemplates this garden experiences a profound and enduring satisfaction, but the satisfaction cannot be traced to the perception of proportional relations, for there are none of the kind that the definition specifies. What the background cases share — mathematical proportion — is precisely what the Ryoan-ji garden lacks, and yet its beauty is not in dispute. The definition has been constructed from a tradition in which proportion is the privileged aesthetic principle, and it mistakes this particular tradition for a universal condition of beauty.

background_cases: The sonnet achieves its formal perfection through the constraint of fourteen lines, a fixed rhyme scheme, and the volta that divides the poem into an octave and a sestet — the form disciplines the content and gives it shape.

The sonata-allegro form achieves its formal perfection through the tripartite structure of exposition, development, and recapitulation — the thematic material is stated, transformed, and returned, and the listener's satisfaction arises from the sense of completeness that this arc provides.

The five-act tragedy achieves its formal perfection through the structured progression from exposition through rising action, climax, falling action, and denouement — each act serves a determinate function in the unfolding of the dramatic action.
definition: A work of art W has formal excellence if and only if (i) W conforms to a recognised formal pattern appropriate to its genre, (ii) the formal pattern imposes constraints that discipline and organise the content, and (iii) the constraints are satisfied without any appearance of strain or artificiality.
conditions: W conforms to a recognised formal pattern
The pattern disciplines and organises the content
Constraints are satisfied without strain or artificiality
counterexample: Consider the late novels of James Joyce, particularly Finnegans Wake. The work conforms to no recognised formal pattern — it is not a novel in any conventional sense, nor a poem, nor a drama. Its organising principles, to the extent that they exist, were invented by Joyce for this particular work. No prior genre supplies the formal constraints. Condition (i) is violated. Yet many critics regard the work as possessing a formal excellence of the highest order — an excellence that arises precisely from the invention of a new form rather than the perfection of an existing one.
abductive_insight: The background cases all achieve formal excellence within inherited forms — the sonnet, the sonata, the five-act tragedy. The definition captures the logic of formal perfection within tradition but cannot accommodate the artist who invents a new form, since there is no prior recognised pattern for the work to conform to. Formal excellence, it appears, may consist not only in the masterful inhabitation of an existing form but in the creation of a form that did not previously exist.
passage:
The works most frequently praised for their formal excellence tend to operate within inherited structures of considerable rigour. The sonnet's fourteen lines and prescribed rhyme scheme impose a discipline that the finest sonneteers inhabit with such ease that the constraint becomes invisible — one reads Shakespeare's sonnets without any sense that the form has forced the thought into an unnatural shape. The sonata-allegro form offers the composer a tripartite architecture — exposition, development, recapitulation — within which thematic material can be stated, transformed, and restored with a satisfying sense of completeness. The five-act tragedy provides the dramatist with a structured progression from opening situation through crisis to resolution.

One might codify these observations by defining formal excellence as the property of a work that conforms to a recognised formal pattern, is disciplined and organised by the constraints of that pattern, and satisfies the constraints without strain or artificiality. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by Finnegans Wake. Joyce's final work conforms to no recognised formal pattern — it is not a novel in any conventional sense, nor a poem, nor a cycle of stories, nor a drama. Its organising principles — the cyclical structure derived from Vico, the elaborate systems of correspondence, the portmanteau language — were invented by Joyce for this work alone. No inherited genre supplies the constraints. Condition (i) is not met. Yet the work's formal ambition and internal coherence have been the subject of sustained critical admiration. What the background cases share — the masterful inhabitation of an existing form — is absent, and yet formal excellence is present. The definition has mistaken the perfection of inherited form for the whole of formal excellence, neglecting the possibility that an artist may achieve formal distinction by inventing a form that did not previously exist.

background_cases: A Dutch still-life painting of the seventeenth century represents a table laden with fruit, cheese, and glassware by reproducing the visual appearance of these objects with such fidelity that the viewer can identify each item — the bloom on the grapes, the translucency of the wine glass, the texture of the bread crust.

A portrait by Ingres represents its sitter by capturing the precise contours of the face, the fall of the clothing, and the posture of the body with such accuracy that a person who knew the sitter could identify her from the painting alone.

A topographical landscape by Canaletto represents a Venetian scene by reproducing the architectural details of the buildings, the reflections in the canal, and the quality of the light with sufficient accuracy to serve as a visual record of the city.
definition: A picture P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P visually resembles S — that is, P and S share a significant number of visual properties, (ii) P was produced with the intention of creating a visual likeness of S, and (iii) a competent viewer who is familiar with S can identify S by inspecting P.
conditions: P visually resembles S
P was produced with intention of creating a likeness
A competent viewer can identify S from P
counterexample: Consider a cubist portrait by Picasso — say, the 1910 portrait of Daniel-Henry Kahnweiler. The painting does not visually resemble Kahnweiler in any straightforward sense: the face is fragmented into intersecting planes, the body is dissolved into geometric facets, and the spatial relations among the parts are disrupted. A viewer unfamiliar with the title would not identify the sitter. Conditions (i) and (iii) are violated. Yet the painting is universally described as a portrait of Kahnweiler — as a representation of him — and Picasso's intention to depict Kahnweiler is not in doubt. The painting represents its subject without visually resembling him.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Dutch still life, the Ingres portrait, the Canaletto landscape — all achieve representation through visual resemblance. The definition captures the logic of resemblance-based representation but fails when applied to pictorial traditions that represent their subjects through other means — fragmentation, abstraction, schematisation — revealing that resemblance is one strategy of pictorial representation rather than a necessary condition of it.
passage:
The pictures most commonly adduced as paradigms of representation share an obvious feature: they look like what they depict. The Dutch still-life painter reproduces the visual appearance of fruit and glassware with such fidelity that one might almost reach into the canvas. The portrait by Ingres captures the sitter's likeness with an accuracy that permits identification. The Canaletto reproduces a Venetian scene with the precision of a topographical survey. In each case, the picture and its subject share a significant number of visual properties, and the representation succeeds by virtue of this resemblance.

One might codify these observations by defining pictorial representation as a relation in which the picture visually resembles its subject, was produced with the intention of creating a likeness, and enables a competent viewer to identify the subject from the picture. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases faithfully. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by Picasso's cubist portrait of Daniel-Henry Kahnweiler, painted in 1910. The painting fragments the sitter's face and body into intersecting geometric planes. The nose, the eyes, the hands are suggested rather than depicted; spatial relations are disrupted; the figure merges with the background in a way that abolishes the distinction between solid form and surrounding space. The painting does not visually resemble Kahnweiler in any straightforward sense, and a viewer ignorant of the title would not identify the sitter. Conditions (i) and (iii) are violated. Yet the painting is universally recognised as a representation of Kahnweiler — critics describe it as a portrait, Picasso intended it as a portrait, and it functions within the history of art as a portrait. What the background cases share is resemblance-based representation, and the definition captures this strategy accurately. But Picasso's portrait represents its subject through means other than resemblance — through schematisation, through the analytic decomposition of form — revealing that resemblance is a sufficient but not a necessary condition of pictorial representation.

background_cases: Milton's Paradise Lost engages the reader's imagination by describing scenes that no human eye has witnessed — the war in Heaven, the creation of the world, the landscape of Hell — in language so vivid that the reader forms detailed mental images of events that lie entirely beyond experience.

Turner's late seascapes engage the viewer's imagination by presenting storms, sunsets, and atmospheric effects in a manner that goes beyond the recording of visual fact to evoke the subjective experience of being immersed in light and weather.

Beethoven's Pastoral Symphony engages the listener's imagination by employing musical figures that suggest birdsong, the murmuring of a brook, and the violence of a thunderstorm, so that the listener forms quasi-perceptual experiences of a rural scene.
definition: A work of art W engages the imagination if and only if (i) W presents content that goes beyond what is immediately perceived — that is, W invites the audience to entertain ideas, images, or scenarios not directly given, (ii) the audience forms mental representations in response to W, and (iii) the mental representations are vivid and detailed.
conditions: W presents content beyond what is immediately perceived
The audience forms mental representations
The representations are vivid and detailed
counterexample: Consider a Rothko colour-field painting — a large canvas divided into two or three horizontal bands of luminous colour, with no representational content, no narrative, and no suggestion of any scene or scenario. The painting does not invite the viewer to entertain ideas or scenarios not directly given; it presents only itself — colour, scale, and edge. Yet many viewers report that the painting profoundly engages their imagination, producing not vivid mental images but rather states of absorption, contemplation, and emotional resonance that they describe as imaginative experiences of the first order. Conditions (i) and (iii) are not met in any straightforward sense, yet the engagement of imagination is undeniable.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Milton, Turner, Beethoven — all engage the imagination through representational or quasi-representational content that prompts the formation of vivid mental images. The definition captures this image-forming mode of imaginative engagement but cannot accommodate the non-representational mode exemplified by Rothko, in which the imagination is engaged not through the production of mental images but through a form of contemplative absorption that is imaginative without being imagistic.
passage:
When one surveys the works most often praised for engaging the imagination, a pattern of representational vividness emerges. Milton's Paradise Lost describes scenes no eye has witnessed — the fall of the rebel angels, the garden of Eden before the Fall — in language so precise and sensuous that the reader forms mental images of extraordinary detail. Turner's late seascapes present atmospheric effects with such intensity that the viewer feels immersed in the depicted weather. Beethoven's Pastoral Symphony employs musical figures that suggest birdsong and running water with sufficient specificity that the listener forms quasi-perceptual images of a rural landscape.

One might codify these observations by defining the engagement of imagination as a process in which the work presents content beyond what is immediately perceived, the audience forms mental representations in response, and these representations are vivid and detailed. This definition captures the imaginative experience of the paradigm cases with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by Rothko's colour-field paintings. A large Rothko canvas — say, a composition of deep red hovering above a band of dark maroon — contains no representational content. It depicts no scene, tells no story, and suggests no scenario. The painting presents only itself: colour, luminosity, scale, and the soft indeterminacy of the edges where one colour-region meets another. It does not invite the viewer to entertain images or ideas not directly given, and the viewer does not form vivid mental pictures. Yet the imaginative engagement that such paintings produce is widely attested and, for many viewers, is more profound than any representational experience. The viewer is drawn into a state of contemplative absorption — not forming images but dwelling in a space of colour and feeling that is experienced as imaginative in the deepest sense. The background cases all engaged the imagination through image-forming; the definition captures this mode faithfully. But Rothko's paintings reveal an alternative mode — imaginative absorption without imagery — that the definition cannot accommodate.

background_cases: A sunset over the Mediterranean, with its graduated bands of gold, crimson, and violet reflected upon the water, is regarded as beautiful by virtually all observers; the scene possesses a harmonious arrangement of colours, elicits a disinterested pleasure, and invites prolonged contemplation without any admixture of practical interest.

A sonata by Mozart, in which every phrase is perfectly proportioned and the themes develop with an inevitability that seems both surprising and right, is regarded as beautiful; the work exhibits internal harmony, elicits serene pleasure, and rewards repeated listening with an ever-deepening sense of formal perfection.

A Greek vase of the fifth century, with its balanced figures and restrained decoration, is regarded as beautiful; the proportions of the vessel and the arrangement of painted figures achieve an equilibrium that satisfies the eye without effort, and the pleasure it produces is entirely disinterested.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses internal harmony — its parts stand in relations of proportion and balance, (ii) O elicits a disinterested pleasure in a normally constituted observer, and (iii) the pleasure is occasioned by the perception of O's sensory qualities.
conditions: O possesses internal harmony — its parts stand in relations of proportion and balance
O elicits a disinterested pleasure in a normally constituted observer
The pleasure is occasioned by the perception of O's sensory qualities
counterexample: Consider a mathematical proof — say, Euclid's proof that the prime numbers are infinite. Mathematicians describe this proof as beautiful. It possesses a kind of internal harmony: each step follows from the preceding with an economy and inevitability that strikes the trained mind as deeply satisfying. The pleasure it occasions is disinterested — one does not admire it because it serves one's personal advantage. Yet the pleasure is not occasioned by the perception of sensory qualities. The proof has no colour, no shape, no sound. Its beauty is apprehended by the intellect, not the senses.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve beauty apprehended through the senses — the colours of the sunset, the sounds of the sonata, the visible proportions of the vase. The definition codifies this sensory dimension as a necessary condition. Yet the mathematical proof shares the harmony and the disinterested pleasure of the background cases whilst lacking their sensory character entirely, revealing that beauty may be a property of intellectual structures as well as of sensory objects, and that the definition's restriction to sensory pleasure is too narrow.
passage:
When one surveys the objects most readily acknowledged as beautiful, a pattern emerges. The sunset over the sea possesses a harmony of graduated colours that elicits a serene pleasure in the observer. The Mozart sonata exhibits a proportioned development of themes that rewards the ear with a sense of formal perfection. The Greek vase achieves an equilibrium of painted figures and vessel proportions that satisfies the eye without effort. In each case, the object possesses internal harmony, the pleasure it occasions is disinterested, and the pleasure arises from the perception of sensory qualities — colours, sounds, visible forms.

One might attempt to codify this pattern by the following definition: an object is beautiful if and only if it possesses internal harmony, elicits a disinterested pleasure in a normally constituted observer, and the pleasure is occasioned by the perception of the object's sensory qualities. This definition captures the shared structure of the background cases with admirable economy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the beauty attributed to mathematical proofs. Euclid's proof that the prime numbers are infinite is described as beautiful by mathematicians of every generation. It possesses internal harmony: each step follows from the preceding with an economy and inevitability that is deeply satisfying to the trained mind. The pleasure it occasions is disinterested — one does not admire it because it serves a practical end. Yet the pleasure is not occasioned by the perception of sensory qualities. The proof has no colour, no visible form, no audible character. Its beauty is apprehended by the intellect alone. What the background cases share — sensory apprehension — is precisely what the mathematical proof lacks, and yet the proof possesses the harmony and the disinterested pleasure that the definition correctly identifies as central to beauty. It appears that the definition, by restricting beauty to the domain of the senses, fails to accommodate the beauty of purely intellectual objects.

background_cases: A Chopin nocturne that conveys a quality of tender melancholy was composed during a period when Chopin was homesick and suffering from ill health; those who know the biographical circumstances recognise the melancholy in the music as an outpouring of the composer's own feeling, and the expressive character of the work is enriched by this recognition.

A self-portrait by Rembrandt painted late in life, with its unflinching depiction of age and weariness, is experienced as an expression of the painter's own confrontation with mortality; the expressive power of the painting is inseparable from the knowledge that Rembrandt was painting himself, recording his own experience of ageing.

The war poems of Wilfred Owen, written in the trenches during the final year of the Great War, express horror and compassion with an intensity that derives in part from the knowledge that Owen was recording his own experience; the poems are taken as expressions of the poet's genuine emotional response to the suffering he witnessed.
definition: A work of art W is expressive of an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E, (ii) W was created as a vehicle for communicating E, and (iii) the expressive character of W is recognisable to a competent audience familiar with the relevant artistic conventions.
conditions: The artist who created W experienced E
W was created as a vehicle for communicating E
The expressive character of W is recognisable to a competent audience
counterexample: Consider an actor who, whilst in perfect emotional equilibrium, gives a performance of King Lear's grief over the dead Cordelia that moves the audience to tears. The performance is expressive of overwhelming grief — every competent observer recognises this. Yet the actor does not experience overwhelming grief; he employs technique, vocal control, and bodily discipline to produce the appearance of an emotion he does not feel. The work — the performance — was created as a vehicle for communicating grief, and its expressive character is recognisable. But condition (i) is not satisfied: the artist does not experience the emotion expressed.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve artists who experienced the emotions their works express — Chopin's homesickness, Rembrandt's confrontation with age, Owen's horror. The definition extracts this biographical connection as a necessary condition. Yet the actor's performance reveals that expressive power may be achieved through craft and technique alone, without the artist undergoing the relevant emotion, suggesting that what matters for expression is not the artist's inner state but the work's capacity to embody the emotion in its formal and conventional properties.
passage:
The works most naturally described as expressive tend to be those in which the artist's own emotional experience is legible in the formal properties of the creation. Chopin's nocturnes, composed during periods of homesickness and illness, convey a tender melancholy that seems inseparable from the composer's biography. Rembrandt's late self-portraits, painted as the artist confronted his own ageing and decline, express a quality of weariness and dignity that is enriched by the knowledge that the painter was recording his own face. Owen's war poems, written amid the squalor of the trenches, express horror and compassion with an intensity that derives in part from their autobiographical origin.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: a work of art is expressive of an emotion if and only if the artist experienced the emotion, the work was created to communicate it, and a competent audience recognises the expressive character. This definition captures the biographical dimension that enriches the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the performing arts. An actor who, whilst in a state of perfect emotional equilibrium, gives a performance of Lear's grief over the dead Cordelia may move the audience to tears. The performance is expressive of overwhelming grief — every competent observer recognises this without hesitation. The work was created to communicate grief. Yet the actor does not experience the emotion he expresses. He relies upon technique, vocal control, and bodily discipline — what Diderot called the paradox of the actor — to produce the appearance of an emotion he does not feel. What the background cases share, and what the definition correctly notes, is a connection between the artist's emotional state and the work's expressive character. But the actor's case reveals that this connection is not necessary: expressive power may be achieved through mastery of convention and technique, without the artist undergoing the relevant emotion.

background_cases: A connoisseur of wine who can distinguish between vintages of neighbouring vineyards on the basis of subtle differences in aroma, body, and finish demonstrates a discriminative capacity that is the product of long training and attentive experience; her judgements are respected because they are more finely discriminating than those of the untrained palate.

A literary critic who can identify the qualities that distinguish a great novel from a competent one — attending to nuances of prose rhythm, structural economy, and depth of characterisation — demonstrates a discriminative capacity developed through wide reading and sustained reflection; her judgements command respect because they perceive distinctions invisible to the casual reader.

A seasoned listener of chamber music who can distinguish a merely correct performance from a great one, attending to phrasing, intonation, and the subtle dynamics of ensemble balance, demonstrates the fruits of long and attentive listening; her judgements are valued because they are more perceptive than those of the inexperienced listener.
definition: A person P possesses good aesthetic taste if and only if (i) P can reliably discriminate between works of greater and lesser aesthetic merit, (ii) P's discriminative capacity has been developed through extensive experience with works in the relevant domain, and (iii) P's judgements are consistent — P does not judge a work favourably on one occasion and unfavourably on another without relevant change in the work or the circumstances of its presentation.
conditions: P can reliably discriminate between works of greater and lesser aesthetic merit
P's discriminative capacity has been developed through extensive experience
P's judgements are consistent
counterexample: Consider a young person, untrained and without extensive experience, who attends her first orchestral concert and is profoundly moved by the slow movement of a Beethoven symphony whilst remaining unmoved by the competent but uninspired overture that preceded it. Her response accurately discriminates between the greater and the lesser work. Her judgement, tested against the consensus of experienced listeners, proves to be correct. Yet her discriminative capacity has not been developed through extensive experience — it is, as it were, a natural gift, an unschooled sensitivity to musical quality. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, but her taste in this instance is no less good for that.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve persons whose discriminative capacity is the product of training and experience — the wine connoisseur's palate, the literary critic's wide reading, the chamber-music listener's years of attentive listening. The definition codifies this experiential development as a necessary condition. Yet the young listener's case reveals that some persons possess a natural sensitivity to aesthetic quality that enables them to discriminate accurately without prior training, suggesting that the ground of good taste lies in the capacity for fine discrimination itself rather than in the particular history by which that capacity was acquired.
passage:
When one considers the persons most readily acknowledged as possessing good taste, a pattern emerges. The wine connoisseur's judgements are respected because they rest upon years of attentive tasting. The literary critic's evaluations command assent because they are informed by decades of wide reading. The seasoned concert-goer's verdicts are valued because they reflect a lifetime of attentive listening. In each case, the person's discriminative capacity — the ability to perceive distinctions of quality that escape the untrained — has been developed through extensive experience with works in the relevant domain.

One might attempt to codify this pattern by the following definition: a person possesses good aesthetic taste if and only if she can reliably discriminate between works of greater and lesser merit, her discriminative capacity has been developed through extensive experience, and her judgements are consistent. This definition captures the cultivated character of taste as it appears in the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the phenomenon of natural sensitivity. A young person, untrained and without extensive experience, attends her first orchestral concert and is profoundly moved by the slow movement of a Beethoven symphony whilst remaining entirely unmoved by the competent but uninspired overture that preceded it. Her response accurately discriminates between the greater and the lesser work. Tested against the consensus of experienced listeners, her judgement proves correct. Her taste, in this instance, is impeccable. Yet her discriminative capacity has not been developed through extensive experience — she possesses, as it were, a natural ear for musical quality. It appears that what matters for good taste is the capacity for fine discrimination itself, however it may have been acquired. The definition, by requiring extensive experience, mistakes one common route to good taste for a necessary condition of its possession.

background_cases: Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon is regarded as original because it introduced formal elements — the fragmentation of the picture plane, the incorporation of African mask forms into the human figure — that were unprecedented in European painting; the work's originality lies in its radical departure from existing conventions.

Stravinsky's Rite of Spring is regarded as original because its rhythmic complexity, percussive orchestration, and dissonant harmonies were without precedent in the concert repertoire; the work's originality lies in its introduction of sounds and structures that had not previously been heard.

James Joyce's Ulysses is regarded as original because it employed stream-of-consciousness narration, radical shifts of style, and a mythological structural framework in ways that no prior novel had attempted; the work's originality lies in its invention of new narrative techniques.
definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W introduces formal or stylistic elements that are genuinely new — not found in any prior work in the tradition, (ii) these new elements are the result of the artist's deliberate creative choices, and (iii) the new elements make a significant contribution to the aesthetic value of W.
conditions: W introduces formal or stylistic elements not found in any prior work
The new elements result from the artist's deliberate creative choices
The new elements make a significant contribution to W's aesthetic value
counterexample: Consider the Well-Tempered Clavier of Johann Sebastian Bach. Every formal element it employs — fugue, prelude, modulation through the circle of keys — was well established before Bach composed the work. One cannot point to a single formal or stylistic device in the collection that is genuinely new. Yet the work is universally regarded as one of the most original compositions in the history of music. Its originality lies in the extraordinary depth, ingenuity, and expressiveness with which familiar materials are handled — a mastery so complete that the conventional forms are transformed into something that sounds unprecedented.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve works whose originality is manifest in the introduction of genuinely new formal elements — Picasso's fragmented plane, Stravinsky's rhythmic innovations, Joyce's narrative techniques. The definition codifies this novelty of elements as a necessary condition. Yet the Bach case reveals that originality may consist not in the invention of new elements but in the transformative mastery of existing ones, suggesting that the essential feature of originality is the creation of aesthetic effects not previously achieved rather than the introduction of formal devices not previously employed.
passage:
The works most readily cited as original tend to introduce formal or stylistic elements that break decisively with the existing tradition. Picasso's Les Demoiselles d'Avignon fragmented the picture plane in ways no European painting had attempted. Stravinsky's Rite of Spring deployed rhythmic and harmonic resources that were genuinely without precedent. Joyce's Ulysses invented narrative techniques — the interior monologue carried to its logical extreme, the radical stylistic variation from chapter to chapter — that no prior novelist had employed. In each case, the work's originality is identifiable in the novelty of its formal means.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: a work of art is original if and only if it introduces formal or stylistic elements not found in any prior work, these elements are the result of the artist's deliberate choices, and they contribute significantly to the work's aesthetic value. This definition captures the revolutionary character of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the Well-Tempered Clavier of Johann Sebastian Bach. The fugue was an established form. The prelude was a conventional genre. Modulation through the circle of keys was a recognised technique. One cannot point to a single formal device in the Well-Tempered Clavier that was not already available to Bach's contemporaries. Yet the work is universally regarded as one of the most original compositions in the history of Western music. Its originality lies in the depth, ingenuity, and expressive range with which these familiar materials are handled — a mastery so complete that the conventional forms sound unprecedented. What the background cases share, and what the definition captures, is the creation of something not previously encountered. But the Bach case reveals that the novelty may lie in the aesthetic effect rather than in the formal means. The definition, by requiring new elements, mistakes the most conspicuous manifestation of originality for its essential nature.

background_cases: The beauty of a Greek temple is typically attributed to the precise mathematical ratios governing its proportions — the relation of column height to diameter, the spacing of the triglyphs, the curvature of the stylobate — all of which produce a sense of harmonious order apprehended immediately by the eye.

The beauty of a Bach fugue is attributed to the intricate formal relations among its voices — the inversions, augmentations, and strettos — which produce a sense of intellectual and sensory satisfaction that persists across repeated hearings.

The beauty of a proof in Euclidean geometry — say, the proof that the base angles of an isosceles triangle are equal — is attributed to its economy of means, the inevitability of each step, and the clarity with which the conclusion follows from the premises.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a high degree of formal order — proportion, symmetry, or structural unity, (ii) the formal order of O is perceptible to an attentive observer without specialised training, and (iii) the perception of O's formal order produces pleasure that is disinterested and non-instrumental.
conditions: O exhibits a high degree of formal order — proportion, symmetry, or structural unity
The formal order of O is perceptible to an attentive observer without specialised training
The perception of O's formal order produces pleasure that is disinterested and non-instrumental
counterexample: Consider a passage of late Beethoven — say, the Grosse Fuge, Op. 133 — whose formal order is extraordinarily dense but largely imperceptible to an attentive listener without specialised musical training. The untrained listener may find the passage harsh, bewildering, and devoid of beauty. Yet a trained listener perceives a rigorous formal architecture and experiences intense aesthetic pleasure. The work exhibits formal order and produces disinterested pleasure, but only for the trained listener. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, yet many would wish to call the Grosse Fuge beautiful.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the Beethoven case lacks: in each, the formal order is accessible to perception without a period of specialised study. The temple's proportions are visible to the untutored eye; the fugue's interplay of voices is audible to the attentive ear; the geometric proof's economy is graspable by any reasoning mind. The Grosse Fuge, by contrast, conceals its formal order behind a surface of apparent chaos. What the definition captures is beauty that is, so to speak, on the surface; what it misses is beauty that must be excavated by training and repeated engagement, where the formal order is real but perceptually occluded.
passage:
When one surveys the objects most frequently cited as paradigms of beauty, a common feature emerges: their formal order is available to perception without arduous preparation. The proportions of the Parthenon are apprehended by the eye; the interweaving of voices in a Bach fugue is heard by the attentive ear; the elegance of a Euclidean proof is grasped by any mind that follows the reasoning. In each case, the formal order is, as it were, on the surface — perceptible to anyone who attends with care.

One might accordingly define beauty as the exhibition of formal order perceptible without specialised training, producing disinterested pleasure. This definition captures the beauty of temples, fugues, and proofs with admirable precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with music of extreme formal density. Consider Beethoven's Grosse Fuge, a work whose contrapuntal architecture is among the most rigorous in the repertoire. An attentive listener without specialised training may find the piece harsh and chaotic; its formal order is concealed beneath a surface of apparent disorder. A trained listener, by contrast, perceives the intricate motivic development and experiences profound aesthetic pleasure. The formal order is present, and the pleasure it produces is disinterested. But condition (ii) is violated: the order is not perceptible without training. The background cases suggest that paradigmatic beauty is characterised by accessibility of formal order, yet the Grosse Fuge appears to be beautiful in a way that requires apprenticeship. It appears that the definition captures one species of beauty — the immediately accessible — but cannot accommodate a second species in which the formal order is real but perceptually occluded, requiring cultivation of the faculties before it can be apprehended.

background_cases: When Wordsworth's poetry expresses a sense of awe before nature, the expression is achieved through carefully chosen imagery, rhythmic patterns, and diction that together evoke the emotional state in the reader; the poet's personal feeling is communicated by means of the poem's sensible properties.

When a Romantic piano sonata expresses melancholy, the expression is achieved through minor tonality, slow tempo, and descending melodic contour — musical properties that listeners recognise as expressive of sadness without being told what the composer felt.

When a painting by Munch expresses anguish, the expression is achieved through distorted forms, lurid colours, and agitated brushwork — visual properties that convey the emotional state to any attentive viewer.
