definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W possesses sensible properties — such as tempo, colour, rhythm, or diction — that are conventionally associated with E, (ii) an attentive and appropriately informed audience recognises E in W on the basis of those properties, and (iii) the expressive properties of W are the result of deliberate artistic choices rather than accident.
conditions: W possesses sensible properties — such as tempo, colour, rhythm, or diction — that are conventionally associated with E
An attentive and appropriately informed audience recognises E in W on the basis of those properties
The expressive properties of W are the result of deliberate artistic choices rather than accident
counterexample: Consider a work of art from an unfamiliar cultural tradition — say, a piece of Javanese gamelan music — that expresses serenity to its native audience through conventions of tuning, rhythmic layering, and melodic contour that are wholly unfamiliar to a Western listener. The Western listener, however attentive and informed about Western music, does not recognise serenity in the music; the conventional associations are different. All three conditions are satisfied relative to the Javanese audience, but condition (ii) fails for the Western listener. The definition either relativises expression to a single cultural community or must deny that the music expresses serenity to the Western listener — yet the music's expressive character seems to be a property of the music itself, not of the audience.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Wordsworth, the Romantic sonata, the Munch painting — all operate within a single cultural tradition in which the conventions linking sensible properties to emotions are widely shared. What makes expression seem objective in these cases is precisely the breadth of the shared convention. The gamelan case reveals that the definition's reliance on convention ties expression to a particular cultural community, and that when conventions diverge, the same work may express different emotions to different audiences, or none at all. The definition captures expression-within-a-tradition but cannot ground the intuition that expression is a property of the work rather than of the audience's conventions.
passage:
The most compelling instances of artistic expression share a notable feature: the sensible properties through which emotion is conveyed are linked to the expressed emotion by conventions that are widely shared within a cultural tradition. Wordsworth's imagery evokes awe through associations familiar to readers of English poetry. The minor tonality of a Romantic sonata conveys melancholy through conventions shared across the Western musical tradition. Munch's distorted forms communicate anguish through visual conventions recognisable to any viewer acquainted with European painting.

One might accordingly define artistic expression as the possession of sensible properties conventionally associated with an emotion, recognised by an informed audience, and resulting from deliberate artistic choice. This definition captures the mechanism of expression in the paradigmatic cases with considerable precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we cross cultural boundaries. Consider a piece of Javanese gamelan music that, within its own tradition, expresses serenity through specific conventions of tuning and rhythmic layering. A Western listener, however attentive and musically cultivated, may recognise no serenity whatever — the conventions that link those sonic properties to serenity are not shared. The definition, by requiring that the audience recognise the emotion on the basis of conventionally associated properties, either relativises expression to a community of convention or must deny that the gamelan music expresses serenity to the uninitiated listener. Yet there is a strong intuition that the serenity is in the music — that it is a property of the work, not a projection of the audience. The background cases, by drawing on broadly shared conventions, obscure this difficulty. It appears that the definition captures expression within a tradition but cannot secure the objectivity of expression across traditions.

background_cases: The experience of standing before Niagara Falls is frequently described as sublime: the sheer volume and force of the water overwhelm the senses, producing an initial feeling of insignificance that gives way to a heightened awareness of one's capacity for rational comprehension.

The experience of contemplating the night sky in a remote location, where the Milky Way is visible in its full extent, is similarly described as sublime: the vastness of the cosmos produces a sense of awe that is at once humbling and exhilarating.

The experience of witnessing a violent thunderstorm from a position of safety — the crack of lightning, the rolling thunder, the curtain of rain — is described as sublime: the power of the storm is terrifying, but the observer's safety transforms terror into admiration.
definition: An experience E is an instance of the natural sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great magnitude or power, (ii) E produces in the subject a feeling of being overwhelmed that is initially unpleasant, and (iii) E culminates in a feeling of exhilaration or elevation that arises from the subject's awareness of being able to comprehend what overwhelms the senses.
conditions: E is occasioned by a natural phenomenon of great magnitude or power
E produces in the subject a feeling of being overwhelmed that is initially unpleasant
E culminates in a feeling of exhilaration or elevation that arises from the subject's awareness of being able to comprehend what overwhelms the senses
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who, caught in a severe blizzard at high altitude, is overwhelmed by the magnitude and power of the storm. The experience is initially unpleasant — intensely so. But it does not culminate in exhilaration or elevation; it culminates in sustained terror, for the mountaineer is in genuine danger and cannot achieve the detachment necessary for rational comprehension. The natural phenomenon is of great magnitude and power; the feeling of being overwhelmed is present. But condition (iii) is not satisfied, because the subject cannot attain the position of safety or detachment that would permit the transformation of terror into elevation.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the mountaineer's experience: in each, the subject occupies a position of safety from which the overwhelming phenomenon can be contemplated without personal peril. The observer at Niagara stands behind a railing; the stargazer is in no danger from the cosmos; the storm-watcher is sheltered. It is this safety that permits the transformation of overwhelm into elevation. The definition omits any reference to the subject's security, and thereby cannot explain why genuine danger extinguishes the sublime rather than intensifying it.
passage:
The paradigmatic experiences of the natural sublime share a structural feature that is easily overlooked. The visitor to Niagara Falls stands behind a railing, safely distant from the torrent. The stargazer contemplating the Milky Way is in no peril from the immensity of space. The observer of a thunderstorm watches from the shelter of a window. In each case, the subject is overwhelmed by a natural phenomenon of great power, experiences an initial moment of unpleasant awe, and then enjoys a feeling of exhilaration arising from the capacity to comprehend what exceeds the senses. The structure is consistent: overwhelm, followed by elevation.

One might codify this pattern as follows: the natural sublime occurs when a phenomenon of great magnitude overwhelms the subject, the initial response is unpleasant, and the experience culminates in exhilaration through rational comprehension. This definition captures the three background cases precisely. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we remove the condition of safety. Consider a mountaineer trapped in a blizzard at high altitude. The storm is of immense magnitude and power. The feeling of being overwhelmed is intense and unpleasant. But the experience does not culminate in exhilaration — it culminates in sustained, unrelieved terror. The mountaineer is in genuine danger, and no detachment is possible. What the background cases possess and the mountaineer's experience lacks is the security that permits contemplation. The railing, the remoteness of the stars, the sheltering window — these create a space in which the mind can turn from terror to admiration. It appears that the definition, by omitting any requirement of the subject's safety, cannot explain why genuine peril destroys the sublime rather than heightening it.

background_cases: A Renaissance portrait represents its sitter by employing conventions of shading, perspective, and anatomical proportion that enable a viewer to recognise the depicted individual; the representation depends upon a system of pictorial conventions shared between artist and audience.

A Japanese woodblock print represents a landscape by employing conventions of flattened perspective, bold outline, and selective colour that differ markedly from Western conventions but are equally effective within the tradition; representation succeeds because artist and audience share the relevant pictorial system.

An ancient Egyptian tomb painting represents a pharaoh by employing the convention of composite perspective — head in profile, shoulders frontal, legs in profile — a system that would be puzzling to an observer unfamiliar with it but that is immediately legible to anyone within the tradition.
definition: A picture P represents a subject S if and only if (i) P belongs to a system of pictorial conventions that assigns pictorial content to configurations of marks on a surface, (ii) within that system, the configuration of marks in P is assigned the content S, and (iii) a viewer competent in the relevant pictorial system would recognise S in P.
conditions: P belongs to a system of pictorial conventions that assigns pictorial content to configurations of marks on a surface
Within that system, the configuration of marks in P is assigned the content S
A viewer competent in the relevant pictorial system would recognise S in P
counterexample: Consider a child's first drawing — a circle with two dots for eyes and a curved line for a mouth — intended to represent the child's mother. The drawing does not belong to any established system of pictorial conventions. It is not produced within a tradition that assigns determinate content to configurations of marks. No pictorial system exists relative to which a competent viewer would recognise the child's mother. Yet the child intends to represent the mother and, shown to the mother, the drawing is recognised and cherished as a depiction. All three conditions fail, yet representation appears to occur.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Renaissance portrait, the woodblock print, the tomb painting — all operate within mature pictorial systems with established conventions. What makes representation seem convention-dependent in these cases is precisely the existence of a codified tradition. The child's drawing reveals that representation can occur prior to and independently of any system of conventions, suggesting that some more basic capacity — perhaps the intention to depict combined with a minimal structural correspondence — grounds representation at a level beneath convention. The definition captures conventional representation but cannot account for the pre-conventional or proto-representational act.
passage:
The most familiar instances of pictorial representation operate within established systems of convention. The Renaissance portrait employs perspective, shading, and anatomical proportion; the Japanese woodblock print employs flattened space and bold outline; the Egyptian tomb painting employs composite perspective. In each case, representation succeeds because artist and audience share a system that assigns pictorial content to configurations of marks. The conventions differ — sometimes radically — but the mechanism is the same: a shared system mediates between marks on a surface and depicted content.

One might accordingly define pictorial representation as the assignment of content to marks within a shared conventional system, recognisable by a competent viewer within that system. This definition accommodates the diversity of pictorial traditions with admirable economy. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises at the very origin of picture-making. Consider a child who, prior to any instruction in drawing, produces a circle with two dots and a curved line, and declares that it is a picture of her mother. The child has not been inducted into any pictorial system. No convention assigns content to these particular marks. No viewer competent in any established system would, on the basis of that system, recognise the child's mother. Yet the drawing represents the mother — the child intends it so, and the mother, shown the drawing, recognises it as a depiction of herself. The background cases, by drawing upon mature traditions, obscure the fact that representation can occur prior to convention. It appears that some more primitive capacity — the intention to depict, combined with a minimal structural correspondence between marks and subject — grounds representation at a level that the convention-based definition cannot reach.

background_cases: The beauty of a Greek temple is apprehended through the proportions of its columns, the symmetry of its facade, and the harmony of its parts; a competent observer takes pleasure in the visual form of the building, and this pleasure does not depend upon any desire to own or inhabit the temple.

The beauty of a Mozart piano sonata is apprehended through the balance of its phrases, the elegance of its modulations, and the inevitability of its resolutions; the listener takes pleasure in the auditory form of the composition, and this pleasure is not contingent upon any practical advantage the music might confer.

The beauty of a Persian miniature is apprehended through its intricate line-work, its jewel-like colour, and its delicate composition; the observer takes pleasure in the visual form, and this pleasure arises from attending to the painting itself rather than from any external association.

The beauty of a Shakespeare sonnet is apprehended through its metre, its imagery, and the turn of its argument; the reader takes pleasure in the verbal form, and this pleasure is not reducible to the pleasure of agreeing with the sentiment expressed.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O exhibits a formal organisation that is perceptible to a suitably attentive observer, (ii) the perception of this organisation produces in the observer a disinterested pleasure — that is, a pleasure independent of any desire for or practical interest in the object, and (iii) the pleasure is communicable and can be expected of any similarly attentive observer.
conditions: O exhibits a formal organisation that is perceptible to a suitably attentive observer
The perception of this organisation produces in the observer a disinterested pleasure
The pleasure is communicable and can be expected of any similarly attentive observer
counterexample: Consider a sunset of extraordinary colour. An observer takes disinterested pleasure in it; the pleasure is communicable; any attentive person would share it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the sunset has no formal organisation in any non-trivial sense — its colours are the product of atmospheric scattering and are arranged by no principle of composition. If we insist that beauty requires formal organisation, we exclude the beauty of natural phenomena that lack intentional structure. If we relax the requirement, we lose the capacity to explain why some arrangements are beautiful and others are not.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the sunset case lacks: in each, the beauty is apprehended through a structure that has been intentionally composed — columns proportioned by an architect, phrases balanced by a composer, lines drawn by a painter. The definition requires 'formal organisation,' but the paradigmatic cases suggest that what produces aesthetic pleasure is not mere form but designed or intelligible form. The sunset possesses visual splendour but no structure that can be analysed as the product of compositional intention. It appears that the definition, drawn from cases of artistic beauty, does not extend to natural beauty without modification, and that the concept of beauty may harbour an unresolved tension between its application to art and its application to nature.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of beauty in the Western tradition share a recognisable structure. A Greek temple is beautiful in virtue of the proportions of its columns and the symmetry of its facade — features that are the products of architectural deliberation. A Mozart sonata is beautiful in virtue of the balance of its phrases and the elegance of its modulations — features that are the products of compositional craft. A Persian miniature is beautiful in virtue of its intricate line-work and jewel-like colour — features achieved by painstaking draughtsmanship. In each case, the observer takes a disinterested pleasure in a form that has been deliberately organised; the pleasure is communicable and can be expected of any suitably attentive person.

One might accordingly propose that an object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits a perceptible formal organisation that produces disinterested and communicable pleasure. This proposal captures the shared structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we turn from art to nature. Consider a sunset of extraordinary colour — bands of crimson and gold shading into violet above a darkening horizon. An attentive observer takes profound disinterested pleasure in the spectacle. The pleasure is communicable: anyone who witnessed the same sunset would be expected to share it. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are plainly satisfied. But it is far from clear that the sunset exhibits formal organisation in any non-trivial sense. Its colours are the product of atmospheric scattering, particle density, and the angle of the sun — physical processes that are not governed by any principle of composition or design. One might observe that the background cases all involve beauty that arises from intentional structure: the architect proportioned the columns, the composer balanced the phrases, the painter drew the lines. The sunset has no such provenance. It appears that the definition, abstracted from cases of artistic beauty in which formal organisation is the work of a deliberate intelligence, cannot accommodate the beauty of nature without either stretching the concept of formal organisation beyond recognition or acknowledging that beauty in nature may rest upon a different foundation altogether.

background_cases: Beethoven's late string quartets are experienced as expressing profound spiritual struggle; the angular melodic lines, the extreme contrasts of dynamics, and the unexpected harmonic shifts produce in the listener a sense of anguished striving that could not be conveyed by a verbal description of the composer's state of mind.

El Greco's paintings of saints are experienced as expressing ecstatic religious fervour; the elongated figures, the lurid colours, and the turbulent skies produce in the viewer a sense of spiritual intensity that goes beyond what any theological proposition could capture.

Keats's odes are experienced as expressing a poignant awareness of transience; the lush imagery, the slow vowel sounds, and the carefully modulated rhythms produce in the reader an ache of beauty passing that could not be conveyed by a mere assertion of the theme.
definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) W possesses perceptible qualities that are experienced by a competent observer as corresponding to E, (ii) the observer's experience of those qualities produces a felt response that is recognisably related to E, and (iii) the correspondence between the work's qualities and the emotion is not merely accidental but arises from features of the work that are integral to its artistic character.
conditions: W possesses perceptible qualities experienced by a competent observer as corresponding to E
The observer's experience of those qualities produces a felt response recognisably related to E
The correspondence is not merely accidental but arises from features integral to the work's artistic character
counterexample: Suppose a piece of music is played at the funeral of someone the listener loved. The music, a cheerful waltz, possesses no qualities that correspond to grief. Yet the listener, associating the waltz with the deceased, experiences profound sadness whenever she hears it. Her felt response is recognisably related to grief. And the association is not accidental — it arises reliably and persistently from her personal history with the piece. One might argue that conditions (i) and (ii) are met from her perspective, and condition (iii) is met in the sense that the features of the work are integral to her experience. Yet the music does not express grief; it merely occasions grief through a private association.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the funeral waltz lacks: in each, the emotion is perceived as residing in the work's formal and sensory qualities themselves — the angular lines of Beethoven, the elongated figures of El Greco, the lush imagery of Keats. The listener's or viewer's emotional response is a response to what is heard or seen, not to a private association superimposed upon it. The waltz case reveals that the definition, by attending to felt response and non-accidentality, does not adequately distinguish between emotions that a work expresses through its intrinsic qualities and emotions that a listener projects upon it through biographical contingency. What the paradigmatic cases suggest is that artistic expression requires the emotion to be, in some sense, publicly available in the work — discernible to any competent observer, not only to one with a particular personal history.
passage:
The works we most confidently describe as expressive share a striking feature: the emotion they express appears to reside in their perceptible qualities rather than in any information we possess about the circumstances of their creation. Beethoven's late quartets express spiritual struggle through angular melodic lines and extreme dynamic contrasts. El Greco's paintings express ecstatic fervour through elongated figures and turbulent skies. Keats's odes express an awareness of transience through lush imagery and slow vowel sounds. In each case, the emotion is apprehended in the work itself, not inferred from external knowledge.

One might accordingly propose that a work expresses an emotion when it possesses qualities experienced as corresponding to that emotion, when those qualities produce a felt response in the observer, and when the correspondence is integral to the work's artistic character rather than merely accidental. This proposal appears to capture the shared structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the role of private association. Suppose a listener hears a cheerful waltz — a piece whose qualities correspond, if to any emotion, to gaiety and light-heartedness. But the waltz was played at the funeral of someone the listener loved, and now she cannot hear it without experiencing profound sadness. Her response is not accidental; it arises reliably from her personal history with the piece. The features of the waltz that occasion her sadness — its particular melody, its tempo, its timbre — are integral to her experience. One might observe that the conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied from her perspective. Yet no one would say that the waltz expresses grief. The background cases suggest why: in those cases, the emotion is publicly available in the work's formal and sensory qualities, discernible to any competent observer regardless of personal history. The waltz's sadness, by contrast, is available only to this particular listener. It appears that the definition requires supplementation by a condition of public availability that the paradigmatic cases exemplify but that the definition does not explicitly state.

background_cases: Dr. Johnson's literary judgements, admired for their penetration, were characterised not only by their correctness but by their groundedness in wide reading, close attention to the qualities of particular texts, and a willingness to revise verdicts upon renewed acquaintance with the works in question.

Ruskin's assessments of painting, though sometimes eccentric, were grounded in an extraordinary capacity to perceive and articulate the specific visual qualities of the works he examined; his taste was inseparable from his ability to describe what he saw.

Tovey's musical criticism was distinguished by its combination of technical understanding and direct responsiveness to the auditory character of the compositions he analysed; his verdicts carried authority because they were demonstrably rooted in a close engagement with the musical texture.
definition: A person P has good taste if and only if (i) P is disposed to take pleasure in objects of genuine aesthetic merit, (ii) P is disposed to withhold pleasure from objects that lack such merit, and (iii) P's aesthetic dispositions are stable and resistant to fluctuations of fashion or social influence.
conditions: P is disposed to take pleasure in objects of genuine aesthetic merit
P is disposed to withhold pleasure from objects that lack such merit
P's aesthetic dispositions are stable and resistant to fluctuations of fashion or social influence
counterexample: Consider a wealthy collector who, having been advised by expert consultants, acquires only paintings of the highest quality. He takes pleasure in his collection — genuine pleasure, not merely the pleasure of ownership. He withholds pleasure from paintings his consultants have identified as mediocre. His dispositions are stable: he has followed the same consultants for thirty years. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the collector has never examined a painting with his own eyes in the way that Johnson examined a text or Ruskin examined a canvas. His pleasure is triggered by the consultants' verdicts, not by his own perception of the paintings' qualities. We should hesitate to attribute good taste to him.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the collector's case: Johnson, Ruskin, and Tovey each grounded their aesthetic responses in a direct, perceptive engagement with the qualities of the objects they assessed. Their taste was not merely extensionally correct — reliably tracking merit — but was constituted by a capacity to perceive and respond to the specific features that make a work excellent. The collector's dispositions track merit, but they do so through the mediation of expert opinion rather than through his own perceptual and critical faculties. The definition, by specifying only the correctness and stability of the person's dispositions, cannot distinguish taste grounded in perception from taste parasitic upon the judgements of others.
passage:
The critics we most readily credit with good taste share a distinctive quality: their aesthetic responses are grounded in a close and perceptive engagement with the objects they assess. Johnson's literary judgements were rooted in wide reading and minute attention to the qualities of particular passages. Ruskin's verdicts on painting were inseparable from his extraordinary capacity to perceive and articulate specific visual qualities — the fall of light, the handling of pigment, the structure of composition. Tovey's musical criticism combined technical understanding with a direct responsiveness to the auditory character of the compositions he analysed. In each case, the critic's taste was not merely a disposition to reach correct verdicts but was constituted by a capacity to perceive and respond to the specific features that make a work excellent.

One might accordingly propose that good taste consists in a stable disposition to take pleasure in objects of genuine merit and to withhold pleasure from those that lack it, where this disposition is resistant to the fluctuations of fashion. This proposal captures the extensional correctness and the stability that are, plausibly, necessary conditions of good taste. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the figure of the wealthy collector. Suppose a man acquires, on the advice of expert consultants, only paintings of the highest quality. He takes genuine pleasure in his collection and genuine indifference toward mediocre works. His dispositions have been stable for decades. All three conditions are met. Yet his pleasure is triggered not by what he sees in the paintings but by what his consultants have told him about them. He has never examined the brushwork, the composition, or the colour relationships of any painting in his collection with the kind of attention that Ruskin brought to a Turner landscape. One might observe that the background cases suggest a requirement the definition does not state: that the pleasure constitutive of good taste must arise from the person's own direct perceptual engagement with the aesthetic qualities of the object, not from deference to the judgements of others.

background_cases: Cézanne's treatment of Mont Sainte-Victoire introduced a mode of pictorial construction — the rendering of volume through planes of colour rather than through linear perspective — that had no precedent in the landscape tradition and that opened new possibilities for subsequent painters.

Stravinsky's Rite of Spring introduced rhythmic and harmonic innovations — irregular metres, polytonal superpositions, percussive orchestration — that had no precedent in the orchestral repertoire and that transformed the vocabulary of subsequent composers.

Joyce's Ulysses introduced narrative techniques — interior monologue, stream of consciousness, radical shifts of style within a single work — that had no precedent in the novel and that expanded the possibilities of prose fiction for subsequent writers.
definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W introduces an element of form, technique, or subject that has no precedent in the tradition to which W belongs, (ii) the novel element is the product of the artist's deliberate inventive activity, and (iii) the novel element is integrated into the work in such a way that it contributes to the work's overall artistic achievement.
conditions: W introduces an element with no precedent in the relevant tradition
The novel element is the product of the artist's deliberate inventive activity
The novel element is integrated into the work so as to contribute to its overall artistic achievement
counterexample: Suppose a painter, experimenting with materials, accidentally spills a new chemical compound on a canvas, producing a visual texture that has no precedent in the history of painting. Recognising its interest, she incorporates the texture into a finished painting, where it contributes materially to the work's visual power. The texture has no precedent — condition (i) is satisfied. It is integrated into the work and contributes to its achievement — condition (iii) is satisfied. But it was not the product of deliberate inventive activity; it was an accident that was subsequently exploited. Condition (ii) fails. Yet the finished painting, in which the accidental discovery is purposefully deployed, seems genuinely original.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the accidental-discovery case possesses in its final form but not in its genesis: Cézanne, Stravinsky, and Joyce each arrived at their innovations through sustained deliberate effort. But the painter's case reveals that the origin of the novel element — whether deliberate or accidental — may be less important than the manner of its incorporation. The painter recognised the value of the accident, made it her own through purposeful deployment, and integrated it into a work of genuine achievement. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the novel element originate in deliberate invention, mislocates the source of originality: what matters is not whether the discovery was intentional but whether the artist exercised creative judgement in recognising its value and integrating it into the work.
passage:
The works we most confidently call original share a common structure: each introduces an unprecedented element that transforms the tradition to which it belongs. Cézanne's landscapes introduced a mode of pictorial construction through planes of colour that had no precedent in the landscape tradition. Stravinsky's Rite of Spring introduced rhythmic and harmonic innovations that had no precedent in the orchestral repertoire. Joyce's Ulysses introduced narrative techniques that had no precedent in the novel. In each case, the novel element was the product of sustained deliberate effort, and it was integrated into the work in such a way as to contribute decisively to its artistic achievement.

One might accordingly propose that originality consists in the deliberate introduction of an unprecedented element that is integrated into the work so as to contribute to its overall achievement. This proposal captures the shared structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the role of accident in artistic creation. Suppose a painter, experimenting with unfamiliar chemical compounds, accidentally spills a substance on her canvas, producing a visual texture of extraordinary interest — a mottled, iridescent surface that has no precedent in the history of painting. She does not discard the canvas; instead, she recognises the value of the accident and builds a painting around it, deploying the accidental texture purposefully alongside her usual techniques. The finished work is powerful, and the unprecedented texture contributes materially to its achievement. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. But the texture was not the product of deliberate inventive activity — it was an accident. Condition (ii) fails. One might observe, however, that the background cases suggest a subtler account of what makes a work original. What Cézanne, Stravinsky, and Joyce share is not merely the deliberate invention of a novel element but the exercise of creative judgement in recognising the significance of what they had discovered and integrating it into a work of art. The painter exercises precisely this judgement when she recognises the value of her accidental texture and deploys it purposefully. It appears that the definition, by requiring deliberate invention at the point of origin, cannot accommodate the creative judgement that consists in recognising and exploiting an unintended discovery.

background_cases: When Beethoven composed the 'Eroica' Symphony, the expressive power of the work appeared to derive from the intensity of the composer's own emotional engagement — his identification with heroic ideals and his subsequent disillusionment with Napoleon; the emotional content of the music seemed inseparable from the emotional life of the artist.

When Wordsworth composed 'Tintern Abbey,' the poem's expression of tranquil contemplation appeared to flow directly from the poet's own meditative experience at the scene; the sincerity of the emotion seemed essential to the poem's expressive success.

When Rembrandt painted his late self-portraits, the expression of sorrow and self-awareness appeared to emanate from the painter's own experience of ageing and loss; the depth of expression seemed to require the depth of personal experience.

When Schubert composed 'Winterreise,' the cycle's expression of desolation appeared to mirror the composer's own isolation and illness; the link between lived suffering and artistic expression seemed direct and necessary.
definition: A work of art W expresses an emotion E if and only if (i) the artist who created W experienced E during or in connection with the creation of W, (ii) the formal properties of W are such as to make E perceptible to a suitably attentive audience, and (iii) the audience's perception of E in W is caused by the formal properties specified in (ii).
conditions: The artist who created W experienced E during or in connection with the creation of W
The formal properties of W are such as to make E perceptible to a suitably attentive audience
The audience's perception of E in W is caused by the formal properties specified in (ii)
counterexample: Consider a studio musician who records a film soundtrack passage of intense pathos — swelling strings, a solo violin in a minor key, a gradual diminuendo into silence. The musician performs the passage with technical precision and aesthetic sensitivity but without experiencing any pathos herself; she is thinking of the rhythmic demands and the intonation. Every listener perceives deep sorrow in the passage. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not: the performer experienced no sorrow. Yet the music expresses sorrow as powerfully as any passage composed in genuine grief.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is not that expression requires the artist's actual emotional experience but that successful expression requires formal properties capable of making an emotion perceptible to a receptive audience; the artist's personal emotion, where present, is a frequent accompaniment but not a constitutive condition of expressive success.
passage:
The history of artistic practice offers a rich array of cases in which emotional expression appears to flow from the artist's own emotional life. When Beethoven composed the 'Eroica,' his passionate engagement with heroic ideals seemed to infuse every bar. When Wordsworth revisited the Wye Valley, his meditative tranquillity seemed to pass directly into the verse. When Rembrandt took up the brush before his own ageing face, the sorrow and self-awareness in the resulting portrait seemed inseparable from the painter's lived experience. These cases suggest a natural generalisation: that artistic expression requires the artist to have undergone the expressed emotion.

Yet one might observe that this generalisation, compelling as it appears, does not survive scrutiny. Consider a studio musician engaged to record a film soundtrack. The score calls for a passage of intense pathos — swelling strings, a solo violin tracing a melody in a minor key, a gradual diminuendo into silence. The musician performs the passage with consummate skill, attending to intonation, bowing pressure, and dynamic shading. She is concentrating, engaged, and perhaps mildly satisfied with the quality of her performance, but she is not experiencing pathos. She is, as she might put it, doing her job. Yet every listener who hears the recording perceives deep sorrow. The formal properties of the passage — the melodic contour, the harmonic language, the rhythmic pacing, the timbral qualities of the solo instrument — are sufficient to make sorrow perceptible. The audience's emotional response is genuine and is caused by these formal properties. What the background cases have in common, it now appears, is not the artist's emotional experience but the presence of formal properties adequate to communicate emotion. Where the artist's own feeling is present, it may guide the selection and shaping of formal properties, but it is the properties themselves — not the feeling behind them — that constitute the expressive content. The definition, by requiring the artist's actual experience of the expressed emotion, confuses a frequent causal antecedent with a necessary constitutive condition.

background_cases: When the connoisseurs of eighteenth-century London judged the poetry of Alexander Pope to be of the highest order, their verdicts were confirmed by subsequent generations; the agreement among cultivated judges appeared to be a reliable indicator of genuine excellence.

When the Parisian salons of the mid-nineteenth century declared Delacroix a master colourist, their judgement was borne out by the later development of painting; the shared discernment of the educated audience seemed to track real merit.

When the Viennese musical public of the 1790s recognised the genius of Mozart's late operas, their acclaim was vindicated by the permanent place these works assumed in the repertoire; agreement among persons of taste appeared to be the surest mark of artistic worth.
definition: A judgement of taste is correct if and only if (i) it is the product of disinterested contemplation rather than personal advantage, (ii) it accords with the judgements of the majority of cultivated and experienced observers, and (iii) it concerns an object that possesses features independently capable of producing the relevant aesthetic response.
conditions: It is the product of disinterested contemplation rather than personal advantage
It accords with the judgements of the majority of cultivated and experienced observers
It concerns an object that possesses features independently capable of producing the relevant aesthetic response
counterexample: Consider a listener in 1830 who, after disinterested and sustained attention, judges the late string quartets of Beethoven to be works of extraordinary depth and originality. The listener's contemplation is free of personal interest. The works possess formal features — contrapuntal complexity, radical harmonic exploration, unprecedented structural freedom — independently capable of producing the aesthetic response in question. But the majority of cultivated observers in 1830 regard these quartets as incoherent and even unlistenable. Condition (ii) fails, yet the listener's judgement is, as posterity confirms, correct.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is not that correct taste requires present agreement among cultivated judges but that it requires responsiveness to features of the object that are independently capable of sustaining the aesthetic judgement — features whose merit may be recognised by the majority only after a period of assimilation.
passage:
The history of aesthetic judgement offers numerous cases in which the agreement of cultivated persons appeared to track genuine artistic excellence. The Augustan admiration for Pope, the Romantic enthusiasm for Delacroix, the Classical-era acclaim for Mozart — in each case, the shared judgement of discerning audiences was vindicated by the enduring reputation of the works in question. One might extract from these cases a generalisation: that a judgement of taste is correct when it is disinterested, when it accords with the preponderance of cultivated opinion, and when it concerns an object whose features are independently capable of producing the relevant response.

Yet this generalisation encounters a difficulty when applied to cases of advanced artistic achievement. Consider the late string quartets of Beethoven, first performed in the mid-1820s. A listener of exceptional sensitivity and musical education attends a performance of the Grosse Fuge and finds in it a work of staggering originality — a contrapuntal argument of tremendous energy, harmonic clashes that resolve into unexpected consonances, a formal structure that defies the conventions of the quartet genre. The listener's response is disinterested; she has no personal connection to the composer and no investment in the work's reception. The features she identifies — the contrapuntal density, the harmonic daring, the structural ambition — are objectively present in the score and independently capable of producing the aesthetic response she describes. But the majority of cultivated listeners in 1830 find the Grosse Fuge incomprehensible. They consider it the product of a deaf composer's loss of judgement. The consensus of the cultivated is against her. On the proposed definition, her judgement is incorrect. Yet posterity will recognise the Grosse Fuge as one of the supreme achievements of Western music. One might observe that what the background cases and this disconfirming case share is not agreement among judges but responsiveness to the objective features of the work. Where the majority's response lags behind, the fault lies not in the dissenting judge but in the majority's failure to assimilate a formal language that exceeds their present capacities.

background_cases: When Giotto introduced spatial depth and naturalistic emotion into the flat, hieratic conventions of Byzantine painting, his originality was recognised as consisting in the introduction of formal elements that had no precedent in the tradition he inherited.

When Monteverdi composed the 'Lamento d'Arianna,' his originality lay in the invention of a new musical rhetoric — the use of dissonance, chromaticism, and rhythmic freedom to depict emotional states — that had no counterpart in the polyphonic tradition.

When Turner dissolved the solid forms of landscape into fields of light and colour, his originality was identified with the introduction of pictorial techniques that departed radically from the conventions of topographical painting.
definition: A work of art W is original if and only if (i) W introduces formal, structural, or thematic elements that have no precedent in the tradition to which W belongs, (ii) the unprecedented elements in W are the product of the artist's creative activity rather than of accident, and (iii) the unprecedented elements significantly alter or extend the possibilities of the art form.
conditions: W introduces formal, structural, or thematic elements that have no precedent in the tradition to which W belongs
The unprecedented elements in W are the product of the artist's creative activity rather than of accident
The unprecedented elements significantly alter or extend the possibilities of the art form
counterexample: Consider a poet who, working within the strict formal constraints of the Petrarchan sonnet in the early seventeenth century, introduces no formal, structural, or thematic element without precedent. The fourteen-line structure, the octave-sestet division, the iambic pentameter, the thematic preoccupation with desire and mortality — all are inherited. Yet within these inherited forms the poet achieves a voice of such distinctive intensity that every subsequent reader recognises the poems as unmistakably original. The originality resides not in unprecedented elements but in a singular manner of inhabiting familiar forms. Condition (i) fails, yet the poems are original.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is not necessarily the introduction of unprecedented elements but the achievement of a distinctive artistic identity that transforms the audience's perception of what the art form can accomplish — an achievement that may proceed through innovation in materials, as in Giotto and Turner, or through the singular deployment of inherited ones.
passage:
The history of art abounds with cases in which originality appears to consist in the introduction of unprecedented formal elements. Giotto's depth, Monteverdi's expressive dissonance, Turner's dissolution of form into light — in each case the artist broke with inherited conventions and introduced something genuinely new. These cases suggest a natural definition: that a work is original when it introduces unprecedented elements through creative activity, significantly extending the possibilities of its art form.

Yet one might observe that this definition, however well it fits the revolutionary cases, cannot accommodate a species of originality that is equally real and perhaps more common. Consider the sonnets of John Donne. Donne wrote within the Petrarchan tradition: fourteen lines, an octave-sestet structure, the themes of desire, absence, and mortality that had been explored by Petrarch, Sidney, and Shakespeare before him. He introduced no new verse form, no new structural device, no new thematic territory. By the criteria of the definition, his work is unoriginal. But no attentive reader of Donne can fail to recognise the extraordinary originality of his poetry — the colloquial urgency of the opening lines, the metaphysical conceits that yoke together disparate domains of experience, the dramatic immediacy that transforms the conventional address to a beloved into something startlingly alive. The originality lies not in unprecedented elements but in the manner of deployment: in the way Donne inhabits the inherited form with a voice so distinctive that the form itself seems transformed. One might observe that the background cases and the Donne case share something deeper than the mere introduction of novelty. What Giotto, Monteverdi, and Turner accomplished was not merely the addition of new elements but the transformation of the audience's perception of what painting or music could be. Donne accomplished precisely the same thing within the sonnet, without adding any element that lacked precedent. The definition, by attending exclusively to unprecedented materials, overlooks the originality that consists in the unprecedented use of familiar ones.

background_cases: When the builders of the Parthenon proportioned the columns, entablature, and pediment according to precise mathematical ratios, the resulting harmony was attributed to the mutual support of elements, each contributing to an effect that would be diminished by the removal or alteration of any part.

When Palestrina composed his masses, the harmony of the polyphonic texture was understood to arise from the way each vocal line supported and complemented the others, so that no part could be extracted without impoverishing the whole.

When the designers of a Persian garden arranged water channels, plantings, and pavilions in a symmetrical pattern, the harmony was perceived as the product of elements in perfect equipoise, each indispensable to the total design.
definition: A composition C is harmonious if and only if (i) the elements of C are so arranged that each supports the effect of the others, (ii) no element could be removed or altered without diminishing the whole, and (iii) the arrangement of elements produces in the observer a sense of completeness, balance, and repose.
conditions: The elements of C are so arranged that each supports the effect of the others
No element could be removed or altered without diminishing the whole
The arrangement of elements produces in the observer a sense of completeness, balance, and repose
counterexample: Consider a sumi-e ink painting consisting of a single bamboo stalk rendered in three swift brushstrokes against a vast field of blank paper. The painting is universally regarded as harmonious. But the harmony resides as much in the empty space as in the brushstrokes. The emptiness is not an element in the sense intended by the definition — it is the deliberate absence of elements. One cannot say that removing the empty space would diminish the whole, for the empty space is precisely the non-presence of further marks. The harmony is constituted by restraint — by what has been withheld — as much as by what has been provided.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition captures only partially, is the principle that harmony arises from a perfect relationship between what is present and what the composition demands; in the limiting case, what the composition demands is the absence of further elements, and the harmony is constituted by restraint as much as by plenitude.
passage:
The great exemplars of harmony in art and architecture appear to confirm a natural generalisation: that harmony consists in the mutual support of elements, each indispensable to the whole, producing a sense of completeness and repose. The Parthenon, the masses of Palestrina, the symmetrical gardens of Isfahan — in each case the observer perceives a composition in which every element contributes and none could be spared. The harmony seems to be a function of plenitude: of having just the right elements, in just the right relations, with nothing superfluous and nothing lacking.

Yet one might observe that this account, persuasive as it is for compositions of many elements, encounters difficulty when applied to works of radical economy. Consider a sumi-e ink painting of a single bamboo stalk — three swift brushstrokes against a field of blank paper that occupies perhaps nine-tenths of the composition. The painting is, by the unanimous testimony of those versed in the East Asian tradition, profoundly harmonious. But the harmony does not reside in the mutual support of numerous elements, for there are very few elements present. Nor can one say that the empty space is an element in the relevant sense: it is the deliberate absence of further marks, the artist's decision to withhold. The harmony is constituted by this withholding — by the relationship between the few strokes that are present and the vast silence that surrounds them. What the background cases and this case share, one might suggest, is not the mutual support of a plurality of elements but rather the achievement of a perfect relationship between what is present and what the composition requires. In the Parthenon, what is required is many elements in precise proportion. In the sumi-e painting, what is required is very few elements in a field of deliberate emptiness. The definition, by attending exclusively to the mutual support of present elements, cannot accommodate the harmony of absence, and thereby excludes some of the most refined achievements of the visual arts.

background_cases: The Parthenon has long been regarded as beautiful, and its beauty is commonly attributed to the mathematical proportions governing its columns, entablature, and overall structure — a harmony of parts that produces a serene and unified visual impression.

A well-composed landscape painting by Claude Lorrain — with its balanced arrangement of trees, classical ruins, and luminous sky — is regarded as beautiful in virtue of the pleasing harmony among its visual elements, each contributing to a unified whole.

A Mozart piano sonata is regarded as beautiful for the clarity of its melodic lines, the balance of its phrases, and the proportioned elegance of its formal structure — qualities that together produce a sense of completeness and order.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) the parts of O stand in harmonious proportion to one another, (ii) O produces a pleasurable response in an attentive and disinterested observer, and (iii) O exhibits clarity of form — its structure is readily apprehended by the senses.
conditions: The parts of O stand in harmonious proportion to one another
O produces a pleasurable response in an attentive and disinterested observer
O exhibits clarity of form — its structure is readily apprehended by the senses
counterexample: Consider a vast Gothic cathedral — Chartres, perhaps — whose interior is a bewildering profusion of ribbed vaults, flying buttresses, stained glass, sculptural programmes, and carved capitals, all ascending into a dimness that the eye cannot fully penetrate. The structure does not exhibit clarity of form in the sense that its totality is readily apprehended; it overwhelms rather than clarifies. Yet it is widely regarded as beautiful. The proportions of its elements may be harmonious, and it produces pleasure, but the third condition — clarity — is violated by the very complexity and obscurity that contribute to its beauty.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that their beauty is of a particular kind — the beauty of perspicuous order — but the definition generalises from this one species to the whole genus, thereby excluding the beauty of the complex, the obscure, and the overwhelming, which depends upon the impossibility of complete apprehension.
passage:
The Parthenon, the landscapes of Claude Lorrain, and the piano sonatas of Mozart each exhibit a beauty that has been attributed to the harmonious proportion of parts, the pleasure they afford a disinterested observer, and the clarity with which their formal structure presents itself to the senses. These cases invite a general account: beauty consists in proportioned harmony, disinterested pleasure, and formal clarity.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from Gothic architecture. Consider the interior of Chartres Cathedral — a vast space articulated by ribbed vaults rising into shadow, walls dissolved into expanses of stained glass, sculptural programmes of bewildering iconographic density, and carved capitals whose details can scarcely be discerned from the nave floor. The cathedral does not exhibit clarity of form in any straightforward sense. Its structure is not readily apprehended; it overwhelms the visual faculty rather than satisfying it with lucid order. Yet Chartres is widely regarded as among the most beautiful buildings in existence. Its beauty derives in part from precisely this complexity — from the way it exceeds the capacity of the senses to take it in at a single view. One might observe that the background cases, whilst genuinely beautiful, represent a particular species of beauty — the beauty of perspicuous order — and that the definition, by generalising from these cases alone, excludes the beauty of the complex, the sublime, and the mysterious, whose power depends upon the impossibility of complete apprehension.

background_cases: Michelangelo's ceiling of the Sistine Chapel is a work of art whose status is secured by the extraordinary skill of its execution — the anatomical mastery, the compositional daring, the technical virtuosity of fresco painting on a curved surface — combined with the artist's evident creative intention.

A finely crafted Persian miniature painting, with its meticulous detail, harmonious colours, and skilful rendering of figures within a traditional iconographic framework, is a work of art whose status rests upon the evident craft and intentional artistry of its maker.

A hand-thrown ceramic vase by a master potter, whose glaze, form, and balance result from decades of refined skill and deliberate aesthetic choices, is regarded as a work of art by virtue of the craft and intention embodied in its making.
definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is an artefact produced by human skill, (ii) the maker of O intended O to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities, and (iii) O displays a level of craftsmanship that exceeds what is required for purely functional purposes.
conditions: O is an artefact produced by human skill
The maker of O intended O to be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities
O displays a level of craftsmanship that exceeds what is required for purely functional purposes
counterexample: Consider a piece of driftwood collected from a beach by an artist and displayed upon a pedestal in a gallery with a title and an artist's statement. The driftwood is not an artefact produced by human skill — it was shaped by the sea. The level of craftsmanship displayed is nil, for no human hand has fashioned it. Yet the art world regards it as a work of art: it is exhibited, discussed, and critiqued. The artist's act of selection, framing, and presentation has conferred upon it a status that the definition's conditions cannot account for.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the artistic status of the object is inseparable from the process of skilled making — but the definition mistakes this feature of the background cases for a necessary condition of art as such, overlooking works whose artistic status derives from conceptual acts of selection, designation, or recontextualisation.
passage:
Michelangelo's Sistine ceiling, the Persian miniature, and the master potter's vase share a common feature: in each case, the object's status as a work of art appears inseparable from the extraordinary skill exercised in its making and from the maker's intention that the object be appreciated for its aesthetic qualities. These paradigmatic cases suggest a definition: a work of art is a human-made artefact, produced with aesthetic intention, displaying craftsmanship that exceeds functional necessity.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the practice of found-object art. Suppose an artist collects a piece of driftwood from a beach — a weathered branch shaped by decades of tidal action into a form of striking elegance. The artist places it upon a pedestal in a gallery, assigns it a title, and accompanies it with a statement describing the selection process. No human skill has fashioned the driftwood; the sea and the wind are its only makers. No craftsmanship exceeding functional necessity is displayed, for no craftsmanship of any kind has been applied. Yet the art world treats the result as a work of art. Critics discuss it, curators exhibit it, and audiences contemplate it. It appears that what the background cases shared — the centrality of skilled making — was a feature of those particular cases rather than a necessary condition of art as such. The definition, by generalising from cases in which making is central, cannot accommodate works whose artistic status derives from acts of selection, framing, or conceptual designation.

background_cases: The experience of listening to a Beethoven string quartet in a concert hall — absorbed, attentive, freed from practical concerns — is valued not for any information conveyed or practical advantage gained but for the intrinsic quality of the listening experience itself; the pleasure is inseparable from the act of attending to the music's formal development.

The experience of standing before a Turner seascape, absorbed in the luminous interplay of light and water, is aesthetic precisely because the observer's attention is directed towards the perceptual qualities of the painting and the experience is valued as an end in itself.

The experience of reading a finely wrought passage of prose by Virginia Woolf, in which the rhythm of the sentences and the precision of the imagery produce a felt quality of literary pleasure, is aesthetic because the reader attends to the formal and sensory qualities of the language for their own sake.
definition: An experience E is aesthetic if and only if (i) E is characterised by absorbed attention to the formal or sensory properties of an object, (ii) E is valued as an end in itself rather than as a means to some further purpose, and (iii) E is pleasurable.
conditions: E is characterised by absorbed attention to the formal or sensory properties of an object
E is valued as an end in itself rather than as a means to some further purpose
E is pleasurable
counterexample: Consider the experience of viewing Picasso's Guernica — the shattered figures, the screaming horse, the dismembered bodies rendered in stark monochrome. The viewer is absorbed; the experience is valued for its own sake; the attention is directed towards the formal properties of the painting. Yet the experience is not pleasurable. It is harrowing, disturbing, and painful. Nevertheless, it is an aesthetic experience of the first importance — one that many regard as among the most powerful the visual arts can afford.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the aesthetic experience they afford happens to be pleasurable, but the definition mistakes this contingent feature for a necessary condition — overlooking the fact that some of the most profound aesthetic experiences are characterised by disturbance, anguish, or moral discomfort rather than pleasure.
passage:
The absorbed listening to a Beethoven quartet, the contemplation of a Turner seascape, and the reading of a passage by Woolf share a common structure: in each case, attention is directed towards the formal or sensory qualities of an object, the experience is valued as an end in itself, and the result is pleasurable. These cases suggest a general account: an aesthetic experience is one of absorbed, disinterested, pleasurable attention to form or sensation.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the experience of viewing Picasso's Guernica. The viewer stands before the vast canvas, absorbed in its fractured forms — the screaming horse, the dismembered soldier, the weeping mother holding her dead child. Attention is directed towards the formal properties of the painting: the stark monochrome palette, the angular distortion of figures, the compressed and claustrophobic pictorial space. The experience is valued as an end in itself; the viewer does not contemplate Guernica for any practical advantage. Yet the experience is not pleasurable. It is harrowing, disturbing, and morally painful. Nevertheless, it is an aesthetic experience of the first importance — one that many regard as among the most powerful the visual arts can offer. It appears that the background cases, whilst all pleasurable, represent only one variety of aesthetic experience. The definition, by making pleasure a necessary condition, excludes the aesthetic of the terrible, the tragic, and the morally challenging — experiences that are aesthetic precisely in their power to disturb.

background_cases: A Dutch still-life painting of the seventeenth century represents a table laden with fruit, flowers, and silverware by means of a detailed visual resemblance between the painted surface and the objects depicted; the viewer recognises the objects because the painting looks like what it represents.

A portrait by Rembrandt represents a specific individual by reproducing the sitter's physiognomy with such fidelity that a viewer familiar with the subject would recognise him in the painting — resemblance secures the representational relation.

A Victorian botanical illustration represents a particular species of orchid by meticulously reproducing the plant's colour, form, and structure in a manner that enables identification — the illustration looks like the orchid it depicts.
definition: A picture P represents an object O if and only if (i) P bears a perceptible visual resemblance to O, (ii) the resemblance is intended by the maker of P, and (iii) a viewer acquainted with O would recognise O in P.
conditions: P bears a perceptible visual resemblance to O
The resemblance is intended by the maker of P
A viewer acquainted with O would recognise O in P
counterexample: Consider a cartographic map that represents a coastline by means of a coloured line bearing no perceptual resemblance to the cliffs, beaches, and harbours it depicts. The line is blue; the coast is not. The line is thin; the coast extends for miles. No viewer would recognise the coast by looking at the line alone. Yet the map represents the coastline — its representational function is undeniable. The representation operates through convention and structural correspondence rather than through visual resemblance.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are all instances of depictive representation, in which resemblance happens to be the vehicle of reference — but the definition mistakes this particular vehicle for a necessary condition of all pictorial representation, thereby excluding conventional, diagrammatic, and structural modes of representation.
passage:
The Dutch still life, the Rembrandt portrait, and the Victorian botanical illustration each represent their subjects by means of visual resemblance: the painting looks like the fruit, the portrait looks like the sitter, and the illustration looks like the orchid. These cases suggest a general theory of pictorial representation: a picture represents an object when it visually resembles it, the resemblance is intended, and a viewer acquainted with the object would recognise it.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this resemblance-based account may be drawn from cartography. Consider a map that represents a coastline by means of a thin blue line drawn on white paper. The line bears no perceptual resemblance to the actual coast — to the cliffs, the sandy beaches, the harbours, the seabirds wheeling above the surf. The coast is immense; the line is a few inches long. The coast is three-dimensional; the line is flat. No viewer would look at the blue line alone and recognise the coastline of Devon. Yet the map undeniably represents that coastline. Its representational function is secured not by resemblance but by convention and structural correspondence — the spatial relations among points on the map preserve the spatial relations among points on the coast. One might observe that what the background cases shared — the vehicle of resemblance — was a feature of depictive representation in particular, not a necessary condition of pictorial representation as such. The definition, by equating representation with resemblance, cannot accommodate the vast domain of conventional, diagrammatic, and structural representation.

background_cases: When one contemplates a Greek temple, the beauty of the structure does not reside merely in the proportions of its columns or the regularity of its entablature taken in isolation, but in the way these elements are animated by the play of light and shadow, the texture of weathered marble, and the relationship of the building to its landscape — the formal properties become beautiful only insofar as they are suffused with a sensory vitality that engages the imagination.

When one is moved by a passage of Mozart, the beauty does not consist merely in the correct observance of harmonic rules or the symmetry of phrase-lengths, but in the way the melodic line breathes, the way tension builds and resolves with a quality of inevitability that is at once surprising and satisfying — the formal structure becomes beautiful only when it is experienced as alive.

When one admires a Persian miniature, the beauty lies not merely in the intricacy of the geometric patterns or the fineness of the brushwork, but in the luminosity of the colours, the interplay between the minuteness of the detail and the grandeur of the compositional scheme, and the sense that every element is animated by a governing imaginative vision.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) O possesses formal order — proportion, symmetry, or structural regularity, (ii) O is perceived by the observer without any interfering practical concern, and (iii) the perception of O produces pleasure in the observer.
conditions: O possesses formal order such as proportion, symmetry, or structural regularity
O is perceived without any interfering practical concern
The perception of O produces pleasure in the observer
counterexample: Consider a perfectly regular grid of identical white tiles on a bathroom floor. The grid possesses formal order — exact proportion, symmetry, and structural regularity. An idle visitor, having no practical concern with the bathroom, perceives the grid and experiences a mild pleasure in its neatness. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call the tile grid beautiful; it is merely neat, orderly, and unremarkable. It lacks the sensory vitality, expressive richness, and imaginative engagement that characterise genuine beauty.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that the formal order be animated by a sensory and imaginative vitality — that the proportions and regularities be experienced as alive, expressive, or engaging to the imagination, rather than as a merely mechanical or inert pattern.
passage:
The history of aesthetic response reveals a pattern that repays careful attention. When one contemplates a Greek temple, the beauty of the structure does not reside merely in the proportions of its columns or the regularity of its entablature, but in the way these formal elements are animated by the play of light and shadow, the texture of weathered marble, and the relationship of the building to its surrounding landscape. When one is moved by a passage of Mozart, the beauty lies not in the mere observance of harmonic rules or the symmetry of phrase-lengths, but in the way the melodic line breathes, the way tension builds and resolves with a quality of inevitability that is at once surprising and satisfying. When one admires a Persian miniature, the beauty consists not merely in the intricacy of geometric patterns but in the luminosity of colour and the sense that every element is animated by a governing imaginative vision.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an object is beautiful when it possesses formal order, is perceived without practical interference, and produces pleasure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the most mundane of settings. Consider a perfectly regular grid of identical white tiles on a bathroom floor. The grid possesses exact proportion, symmetry, and structural regularity. An idle visitor, having no practical concern with the room, perceives the grid and experiences a mild pleasure in its neatness. Every condition of the proposed definition is satisfied. Yet the tile grid is not beautiful — it is merely neat and orderly. It lacks the quality that animates the temple, the sonata, and the miniature: a sensory and imaginative vitality that makes formal order come alive. One might observe that what the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the formal order be experienced as expressive, as alive — that the proportions and regularities engage the imagination and possess a richness that transcends mere mechanical regularity.

background_cases: When Rembrandt painted a self-portrait, the work counted as art not merely because it was a skilled representation of a human face, but because the visible marks of the brush, the handling of chiaroscuro, and the psychological depth achieved through painterly technique transformed the representation into something that demanded sustained contemplation and rewarded it with ever-deepening insight into the human condition.

When a Japanese potter fashioned a raku tea bowl, the object was regarded as art not merely because it served a ceremonial function or displayed technical competence, but because the deliberate irregularities of the glaze, the controlled accidents of the firing process, and the aesthetic philosophy informing the potter's choices invested the bowl with a significance that exceeded its practical use.

When Bach composed a fugue, the work was recognised as art not merely because it followed the rules of counterpoint with technical mastery, but because the systematic exploration of a musical subject through inversion, augmentation, and stretto produced a structure whose intellectual and emotional depth sustained repeated encounters and yielded new discoveries upon each hearing.
definition: An artefact A is a work of art if and only if (i) A is the product of skilled human labour, (ii) A is made with the intention that it be appreciated by an audience, and (iii) A belongs to a recognised category of art such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature.
conditions: A is the product of skilled human labour
A is made with the intention that it be appreciated by an audience
A belongs to a recognised category of art such as painting, sculpture, music, or literature
counterexample: Consider a highly skilled calligrapher who, with the intention that the result be appreciated by an audience, produces an ornate and technically flawless menu for a fashionable restaurant. The menu belongs to the recognised category of calligraphy, which is widely treated as a branch of visual art. All three conditions are met. Yet the menu, however skilfully produced, is not a work of art; it is a functional document whose purpose is to list dishes and prices, and the calligraphic skill, however impressive, serves an entirely practical communicative end without investing the object with the kind of significance that demands sustained contemplative engagement.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that the skilled labour and the maker's intention be directed not merely at producing something to be appreciated but at creating an object whose significance exceeds its immediate practical or communicative function — an object that rewards sustained contemplation and yields meaning beyond what is necessary for any utilitarian purpose.
passage:
Reflection upon the objects that have been recognised as works of art across diverse traditions reveals a recurring structural feature. When Rembrandt painted a self-portrait, the work transcended mere skilled representation: the visible brushwork, the handling of light and shadow, and the psychological penetration transformed the image into an object demanding sustained contemplation. When a Japanese potter fashioned a raku tea bowl, the deliberate irregularities of glaze and the aesthetic philosophy governing the work invested the object with a significance exceeding its ceremonial function. When Bach composed a fugue, the systematic exploration of a musical subject through devices of counterpoint produced a structure of intellectual and emotional depth that rewarded repeated listening.

One might attempt to capture this by defining a work of art as a product of skilled human labour, made with the intention of audience appreciation, belonging to a recognised artistic category. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the practice of calligraphy. Suppose a highly skilled calligrapher, intending the result to be appreciated, produces an ornate and technically flawless menu for a fashionable restaurant. The menu belongs to calligraphy, widely regarded as a branch of visual art. All three conditions are met. Yet the menu is not a work of art. It is a functional document whose purpose is to communicate a list of dishes and prices. The calligraphic skill serves an entirely practical end, and the object does not demand or reward the kind of sustained contemplative engagement that characterises the self-portrait, the tea bowl, and the fugue. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the maker's skill and intention be directed at creating something whose significance exceeds its practical or communicative function, yielding meaning that transcends any utilitarian purpose.

background_cases: When one listens to a late Beethoven string quartet, the experience is aesthetic not merely because attention is sustained and pleasure is felt, but because the listener is drawn into a mode of perception in which the temporal unfolding of the music becomes the sole object of awareness, and the ordinary categories by which one organises experience — past and future, self and world — temporarily recede, leaving the listener absorbed in a present that is charged with significance.
