When one stands before a large canvas by Turner and is enveloped by the luminous atmospherics of sea and sky, the experience is aesthetic not merely because it is pleasurable and attentive, but because the perceptual field is reorganised: the boundaries between the depicted elements dissolve, and one enters a mode of seeing in which colour and light become the primary realities, displacing the usual framework of object-recognition.

When one reads a passage of Proust and is transported by the evocation of involuntary memory, the experience is aesthetic not merely because of the pleasure of the prose, but because the reading induces a distinctive alteration in one's relation to one's own past — a heightened, almost hallucinatory vividness in which forgotten sensations are recovered with an intensity that transforms one's sense of temporal existence.
definition: An experience E is an aesthetic experience if and only if (i) E is pleasurable, (ii) E involves sustained attention to an object, and (iii) E is occasioned by a work of art or a natural scene.
conditions: E is pleasurable
E involves sustained attention to an object
E is occasioned by a work of art or a natural scene
counterexample: Suppose a birdwatcher sits in a hide for several hours, sustaining attention upon a rare bird perched in a distant tree. The birdwatcher finds the observation pleasurable. The experience is occasioned by a natural scene. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the birdwatcher's attention is directed entirely at identifying the species — noting the precise shape of the bill, the pattern of the plumage in relation to a field guide, the length of the tail relative to the body. The birdwatcher's mode of perception is classificatory and taxonomic throughout, and the pleasure derives from the satisfaction of a successful identification rather than from any transformation of perceptual awareness. We should hesitate to call this an aesthetic experience.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that the experience involve a distinctive transformation of the subject's perceptual or imaginative awareness — a reorganisation of attention in which ordinary practical and classificatory frameworks recede, yielding a mode of experience in which the object's sensory qualities become intrinsically significant.
passage:
The experiences that we most readily recognise as aesthetic share a structural feature that simple characterisations tend to overlook. When one listens to a late Beethoven string quartet, the experience is not merely pleasurable and attentive; the listener is drawn into a mode of perception in which the temporal unfolding of the music becomes the sole object of awareness, and the ordinary categories of past and future, self and world, temporarily recede. When one stands before a Turner canvas, the perceptual field is reorganised: the boundaries between depicted elements dissolve, and colour and light become the primary realities. When one reads Proust, the prose induces a distinctive alteration in one's relation to one's own past, recovering forgotten sensations with an intensity that transforms one's sense of temporal existence.

One might attempt to capture this by defining aesthetic experience as pleasurable, involving sustained attention, and occasioned by a work of art or a natural scene. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the practice of birdwatching. Suppose a birdwatcher sits in a hide for several hours, sustaining attention upon a rare species perched in a distant tree. The observation is pleasurable, and it is occasioned by a natural scene. Every condition is met. Yet the birdwatcher's mode of perception is entirely classificatory: the attention is directed at identifying the species by noting bill shape, plumage pattern, and tail length in relation to a field guide. The pleasure derives from the satisfaction of successful identification. No transformation of perceptual awareness occurs; the birdwatcher remains firmly within the practical and taxonomic framework throughout. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is a distinctive reorganisation of the subject's awareness, in which ordinary classificatory and practical frameworks recede and the object's sensory qualities become intrinsically significant.

background_cases: When Burke described the experience of contemplating a vast and stormy ocean, the sublimity consisted not merely in the observer's feeling of terror or insignificance, but in the fact that the observer was at a sufficient remove from actual danger to contemplate the scene rather than merely flee from it — the distance between the observer and the threatening phenomenon permitted an elevated reflection that mere fear would have precluded.

When Kant described the experience of contemplating the starry sky, the sublimity arose not merely from the feeling of being overwhelmed by vastness, but from the subsequent recognition that the mind possesses a faculty — reason — capable of conceiving what sense cannot encompass, a recognition that could occur only because the observer was not practically threatened by the heavens.

When travellers of the eighteenth century described the experience of viewing Alpine precipices, the sublimity depended upon the observer's safety: the precipice was terrifying, but the observer stood upon solid ground, and this security enabled a reflective engagement with the spectacle that would have been impossible had the observer been falling.
definition: An experience E is an experience of the sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by a phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude or power, (ii) E produces in the observer a feeling of insignificance or terror, and (iii) E is accompanied by a feeling of exaltation or elevation.
conditions: E is occasioned by a phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude or power
E produces a feeling of insignificance or terror
E is accompanied by a feeling of exaltation or elevation
counterexample: Suppose a soldier in the midst of battle witnesses an enormous explosion at close range. The explosion is a phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude and power. The soldier feels profound terror and a sense of insignificance in the face of destructive forces beyond human control. Immediately afterwards, the soldier experiences a surge of exaltation — the elation of having survived. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to describe this as an experience of the sublime. The soldier is in immediate practical danger; the terror is not contemplative but visceral and action-directing, and the exaltation is the physiological relief of survival rather than an elevation of the reflective faculties.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that the observer be situated at a sufficient distance from the overwhelming phenomenon — removed from practical danger — so that the experience of terror and subsequent exaltation can take a contemplative rather than a merely visceral form, enabling reflective engagement rather than the instinctive responses demanded by immediate threat.
passage:
The accounts of the sublime that have been most influential in the history of aesthetics share a structural feature that merits attention. When Burke described the experience of contemplating a vast and stormy ocean, the sublimity depended upon the observer's sufficient remove from actual danger — the distance permitted contemplation rather than flight. When Kant described the experience of the starry sky, the sublimity arose from the recognition that reason can conceive what sense cannot encompass, a recognition possible only because the observer was not practically threatened. When eighteenth-century travellers described the sublimity of Alpine precipices, the experience depended upon the observer's standing upon solid ground — the security that enabled reflective engagement with the terrifying spectacle.

One might attempt to capture this by defining the sublime as an experience occasioned by overwhelming magnitude or power, producing feelings of insignificance or terror, and accompanied by exaltation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the experience of combat. Suppose a soldier in battle witnesses an enormous explosion at close range. The explosion is of overwhelming magnitude and power. The soldier feels profound terror and insignificance. Immediately afterwards, the soldier experiences a surge of exaltation — the elation of survival. Every condition is satisfied. Yet this is not an experience of the sublime. The soldier is in immediate danger; the terror is not contemplative but visceral, directing the body towards flight and self-preservation; and the exaltation is physiological relief rather than elevation of the reflective faculties. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the observer be situated at a safe distance from the overwhelming phenomenon, so that terror and exaltation can assume a contemplative form rather than remaining the instinctive reactions demanded by immediate peril.

background_cases: When one contemplates the rose garden at Sissinghurst, the beauty of the scene appears to depend upon the harmonious arrangement of colours, the proportion of the beds to the surrounding walls, and the overall unity of design. The garden pleases because its diverse elements are brought into a coherent visual whole, and one's pleasure is inseparable from the perception of that coherence.

When one regards a Greek temple of the classical period, its beauty is commonly attributed to the mathematical proportions of its columns, the symmetry of its facade, and the balanced relation between its parts. The pleasure it occasions seems to arise from the apprehension of a rational order made visible in stone.

When one listens to a Mozart string quartet, the beauty of the music is associated with the clarity of its melodic lines, the balance between the voices, and the formal perfection of its structure. The listener's pleasure appears grounded in the perception of an intelligible order governing the succession of sounds.
definition: An object is beautiful if and only if (i) it exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts, (ii) the arrangement is governed by intelligible proportions or structural principles, and (iii) the perception of this arrangement occasions a disinterested pleasure in the observer.
conditions: The object exhibits a harmonious arrangement of its parts
The arrangement is governed by intelligible proportions or structural principles
The perception of this arrangement occasions a disinterested pleasure in the observer
counterexample: Suppose one contemplates a weathered cliff face on a remote coastline. The rock has been eroded into irregular, asymmetrical forms by centuries of wind and sea. There is no harmonious arrangement of parts in any intelligible sense: the fissures, overhangs, and jagged outcrops follow no discernible proportion or structural principle. Yet the cliff face occasions a profound disinterested pleasure in the contemplative observer — a pleasure that arises not from the perception of order but from the apprehension of the raw, ungoverned force of natural processes made visible in stone. The observer would unhesitatingly call the cliff beautiful.
abductive_insight: The background cases, drawn from gardens, architecture, and music, naturally suggest that beauty consists in harmonious, proportioned arrangement perceived with disinterested pleasure. Yet the cliff face reveals that beauty may arise from the apprehension of qualities that are antithetical to harmony and proportion — from irregularity, rawness, and the visible trace of ungoverned natural forces. The inductive generalisation from ordered artefacts to a universal definition of beauty fails because it overlooks the aesthetic power of the formless and the wild.
passage:
Reflection upon the most familiar instances of beauty in the arts and in designed nature reveals a pattern that lends itself readily to generalisation. The rose garden at Sissinghurst pleases by the harmonious arrangement of its colours and the proportion of its beds to the surrounding walls. The Greek temple of the classical period derives its beauty from the mathematical proportions of its columns and the balanced relation among its parts. The Mozart string quartet delights by the clarity of its melodic lines and the formal perfection of its structure. In each case, the beauty of the object appears to consist in a harmonious arrangement governed by intelligible proportions, the perception of which occasions a disinterested pleasure.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an object is beautiful when it exhibits harmony among its parts, when that harmony is governed by intelligible structural principles, and when the perception of this arrangement gives rise to a disinterested pleasure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the contemplation of wild nature. Suppose one stands before a weathered cliff face on a remote and desolate coastline. The rock has been shaped by centuries of wind and sea into irregular, asymmetrical forms: jagged outcrops, deep fissures, and precarious overhangs that follow no discernible proportion or structural principle. There is nothing harmonious in the arrangement of these elements. Yet the contemplative observer experiences a profound disinterested pleasure before the cliff — a pleasure arising not from the perception of order but from the apprehension of the raw, ungoverned force of natural processes made visible in the scarred and broken stone. The observer would without hesitation call the cliff beautiful. What the background cases share — and what leads the definition astray — is their provenance in designed or cultivated settings where harmony and proportion are deliberately achieved. The generalisation from these cases to a universal account of beauty overlooks the aesthetic power of the formless, the irregular, and the wild, where beauty arises from qualities antithetical to the very harmony that the definition demands.

background_cases: When a listener attends closely to a Beethoven symphony in a concert hall, the aesthetic experience seems to require sustained attention: the listener follows the development of themes, registers the interplay of instruments, and apprehends the formal architecture of the work over time. The experience is one of active, concentrated engagement.

When a visitor stands before a large-scale painting by Turner, the aesthetic experience involves a deliberate act of looking: the visitor scans the composition, notices the handling of light and colour, and allows the painting's atmosphere to unfold through attentive contemplation. The experience is not passive but requires the visitor's sustained perceptual effort.

When a reader engages with a sonnet by Keats, the aesthetic experience demands careful reading: the reader attends to the rhythm, the imagery, the turns of thought, and the relation of the sestet to the octave. The richness of the experience is proportional to the concentration the reader brings to the text.
definition: An experience is aesthetic if and only if (i) it is directed at an object with perceptible formal properties, (ii) it involves sustained, deliberate attention to those properties, and (iii) it yields a felt quality of absorption or engagement that is valued for its own sake.
conditions: The experience is directed at an object with perceptible formal properties
It involves sustained, deliberate attention to those properties
It yields a felt quality of absorption or engagement that is valued for its own sake
counterexample: Suppose a person, walking through a busy city street, is struck by a fleeting glimpse of late afternoon sunlight falling upon the wet pavement in such a way that the reflected light, the dark silhouettes of passers-by, and the colours of the shop fronts compose, for an instant, a scene of arresting visual beauty. The experience lasts no more than two or three seconds. There is no sustained, deliberate attention: the person did not choose to look, did not scan the scene, and could not prolong the moment. Yet the experience yields an intense felt quality of aesthetic absorption, valued entirely for its own sake. The person would describe the moment as one of genuine aesthetic experience despite the absence of sustained deliberate attention.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the symphony, the painting, the sonnet — are all cases of extended engagement with complex works of art, and they naturally suggest that aesthetic experience requires sustained deliberate attention. But the fleeting glimpse on the city street reveals that aesthetic experience can occur in a momentary, involuntary flash of perceptual receptivity, without any deliberate act of attention. The inductive generalisation from extended artistic engagement to a universal account of aesthetic experience fails to accommodate the instantaneous and unbidden character of some of the most vivid aesthetic moments.
passage:
The most frequently discussed instances of aesthetic experience involve sustained engagement with complex works of art, and these cases naturally encourage a particular generalisation. When one attends to a Beethoven symphony, the experience requires the listener to follow the development of themes and apprehend the formal architecture of the work over an extended duration. When one contemplates a Turner painting, the experience involves a deliberate act of looking that unfolds through attentive perceptual effort. When one reads a Keats sonnet, the richness of the experience is proportional to the care and concentration the reader brings to the text. In each case, the aesthetic experience is sustained, deliberate, and directed at an object with perceptible formal properties, yielding a felt quality of absorption valued for its own sake.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an experience is aesthetic when it is directed at an object with formal properties, involves sustained deliberate attention, and yields a valued quality of absorption. Yet a case which tells against this formulation may be drawn from ordinary perceptual life. Suppose a person, walking through a busy city street with no thought of art or beauty, is struck by a fleeting glimpse of late afternoon sunlight falling upon wet pavement. For no more than two or three seconds, the reflected light, the dark silhouettes of passers-by, and the colours of the shop fronts compose a scene of arresting visual beauty. The person did not choose to look, did not deliberately scan the scene, and could not prolong the moment. There was no sustained attention of any kind. Yet the experience yields an intense felt quality of aesthetic absorption, valued entirely for its own sake, and the person would without hesitation describe it as a moment of genuine aesthetic significance. What the background cases share — and what leads the generalisation astray — is their provenance in deliberate encounters with artworks, where sustained attention is a natural accompaniment. The definition, built upon these cases, fails to accommodate the aesthetic experience that arrives unbidden and lasts only an instant.

background_cases: When a Dutch still-life painter of the seventeenth century paints a bowl of fruit, the painting represents the fruit by presenting a visual likeness: the colours, shapes, and spatial relations on the canvas correspond closely to those of the actual objects. The viewer recognises the fruit because the painting looks like fruit.

When a portrait painter renders the face of a sitter, the painting represents the sitter by reproducing the distinctive visual features of the face — the shape of the nose, the set of the eyes, the line of the jaw. A viewer who knows the sitter can identify him because the portrait resembles him.

When a landscape painter depicts a particular valley, the painting represents the valley by reproducing its visual appearance: the contour of the hills, the course of the river, the quality of the light. One recognises the place because the painting looks like the place.
definition: A painting represents a subject if and only if (i) the painting bears a visual resemblance to the subject, (ii) the resemblance is sufficient for a viewer acquainted with the subject to identify it in the painting, and (iii) the artist intended the painting to depict the subject.
conditions: The painting bears a visual resemblance to the subject
The resemblance is sufficient for a viewer acquainted with the subject to identify it in the painting
The artist intended the painting to depict the subject
counterexample: Suppose a cubist painter intends to depict a guitar. The finished painting fragments the guitar into geometric planes viewed from multiple simultaneous angles, distorts its proportions, and renders its form in colours that bear no relation to the appearance of an actual guitar. The painting does not bear a visual resemblance to a guitar in any ordinary sense: no viewer, confronted with the painting in isolation, would recognise a guitar by visual likeness alone. Condition (i) fails. Yet the painting represents a guitar: it is so titled, the artist intended it as a depiction of a guitar, and the conventions of cubist painting make the representational content intelligible to viewers familiar with the style. Representation is achieved without visual resemblance.
abductive_insight: The background cases are all drawn from traditions of naturalistic painting in which resemblance is the vehicle of representation, and they naturally suggest that resemblance is a necessary condition. Yet the cubist painting reveals that representation can be achieved through conventions, formal strategies, and contextual cues that do not depend upon visual likeness at all. The inductive generalisation from naturalistic painting to a universal account of pictorial representation fails because it mistakes a feature of one tradition for a necessary condition of representation as such.
passage:
The most familiar instances of pictorial representation involve paintings that look like their subjects, and these cases naturally encourage the view that resemblance is essential to representation. The Dutch still-life painter represents a bowl of fruit by reproducing the colours and shapes of the actual objects on the canvas. The portrait painter represents the sitter by capturing the distinctive features of the face. The landscape painter represents the valley by reproducing its visual appearance. In each case, the viewer recognises the subject because the painting resembles it, and the artist intended the painting to depict the subject in question.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a painting represents a subject when it bears a visual resemblance to the subject sufficient for identification, and the artist intended the depiction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the practice of cubist painting. Suppose a painter intends to depict a guitar. The finished canvas fragments the instrument into geometric planes viewed from multiple simultaneous angles, distorts its proportions beyond recognition, and renders its form in colours that have no correspondence to the appearance of any actual guitar. No viewer, confronted with the painting in isolation and without knowledge of the title or the conventions of cubism, would recognise a guitar by visual likeness alone. The painting does not bear a visual resemblance to a guitar in any ordinary sense. Yet it represents a guitar: it is so titled, the artist intended it as such, and the conventions of cubist painting make the representational content intelligible to viewers who understand those conventions. One might observe that representation has been achieved without resemblance, through formal and conventional means that the definition cannot accommodate. What the background cases share — and what the generalisation mistakes for a universal truth — is their provenance in naturalistic traditions where resemblance happens to be the vehicle of representation. The definition, built upon these cases, fails to recognise that resemblance is a feature of one mode of representation rather than a necessary condition of representation itself.

background_cases: When Chopin composed his Preludes, his letters reveal that he experienced intense emotional states during the period of composition — longing, melancholy, agitation — and these states appear to be directly communicated through the music. The listener hears the longing in the slow preludes and the agitation in the fast ones, and the correspondence between the composer's emotional life and the character of the music seems to ground the work's expressiveness.

When Van Gogh painted the Starry Night, his letters to his brother describe the anguish and exaltation he felt while working. The swirling, turbulent brushwork appears to transmit those emotions directly to the viewer, as though the paint bore the impress of the painter's inner state.

When Wordsworth composed the 'Intimations' ode, his autobiographical writings indicate that he was experiencing a profound sense of loss and spiritual questioning. The poem's emotional power seems to derive from the poet's own feelings, communicated through the medium of verse.
definition: A work of art is expressive of an emotion E if and only if (i) the creator of the work experienced E during the process of creation, (ii) the formal properties of the work bear the impress of E, and (iii) the work communicates E to a competent audience.
conditions: The creator of the work experienced E during the process of creation
The formal properties of the work bear the impress of E
The work communicates E to a competent audience
counterexample: Suppose a film composer, working under a tight deadline and feeling only boredom and professional fatigue, is asked to compose a score for a scene depicting profound grief. Drawing upon her technical mastery, she produces a passage of extraordinary emotional power: the orchestration, harmonic progression, and melodic contour communicate grief to every listener who hears it. Critics praise the score's devastating expressiveness. Yet the composer experienced no grief whatsoever during the composition; she felt only the dull irritation of a deadline. The formal properties of the score bear the impress of grief, and the audience receives grief, but the creator's emotional state was entirely unrelated to the emotion expressed.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Chopin, Van Gogh, Wordsworth — are all cases in which the artist's biographical circumstances happened to align with the emotional character of the work, and this alignment naturally suggests that the creator's experience of the emotion is a necessary condition of artistic expression. Yet the film composer reveals that technical mastery alone can produce all the formal properties required for expressive communication, without the creator experiencing the relevant emotion at all. The inductive generalisation from biographically authenticated cases to a universal requirement of felt emotion fails because it mistakes a contingent correlation for a necessary condition.
passage:
The most celebrated instances of artistic expressiveness often come accompanied by biographical evidence of the creator's emotional engagement, and these cases naturally encourage a particular theory of expression. When Chopin composed his Preludes, his letters attest to intense emotional states that appear directly communicated through the music. When Van Gogh painted the Starry Night, his correspondence describes the anguish and exaltation that seem to animate the turbulent brushwork. When Wordsworth composed the 'Intimations' ode, his autobiographical writings confirm the sense of loss that pervades the poem. In each case, the creator experienced the emotion, the formal properties of the work appear to bear its impress, and a competent audience receives the emotion.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a work is expressive of an emotion when the creator felt it, the work's formal properties bear its impress, and the audience receives it. Yet a case which tells against this formulation may be drawn from the practice of commercial film scoring. Suppose a composer, working under a tight deadline and feeling nothing beyond boredom and professional fatigue, is asked to write music for a scene of profound grief. Drawing upon her technical mastery — her command of orchestration, harmonic colour, and melodic contour — she produces a passage of extraordinary emotional power. Critics praise the score's devastating expressiveness, and every listener reports receiving an unmistakable communication of grief. The formal properties of the music bear the impress of grief in every respect. Yet the composer experienced no grief at all during the composition. What the background cases share — and what the generalisation mistakes for a necessary condition — is the contingent alignment of the creator's biographical circumstances with the emotional character of the work. The film composer demonstrates that technical mastery can produce all the formal properties needed for expressive communication without the creator experiencing the relevant emotion, revealing that felt emotion is a frequent accompaniment but not a necessary condition of artistic expression.

background_cases: When we admire a Greek temple, the beauty appears to reside in the proportional relations among its columns, entablature, and pediment. The pleasing effect upon the observer is bound up with the mathematical ratios governing the spacing of the columns and the inclination of the roof. Alter these proportions, and the beauty diminishes.

When we judge a sonata to be beautiful, we attend to the balance of its themes, the resolution of its harmonic tensions, and the symmetry of its formal structure. A sonata whose recapitulation mirrors its exposition in a satisfying way strikes us as more beautiful than one whose parts bear no discernible relation to one another.

When we regard a human face as beautiful, research suggests that symmetry, averageness of features, and the proportional relations among eyes, nose, and mouth are reliably associated with judgements of beauty. Faces that deviate markedly from these proportional norms are less frequently judged beautiful.
definition: An object O is beautiful if and only if (i) the parts of O stand in proportional relations that are mathematically specifiable, (ii) the proportional relations among the parts of O produce a perceptible harmony or balance, and (iii) the perceptible harmony or balance elicits a pleasurable response in a normally constituted observer.
conditions: The parts of O stand in proportional relations that are mathematically specifiable
The proportional relations produce a perceptible harmony or balance
The perceptible harmony or balance elicits a pleasurable response in a normally constituted observer
counterexample: Suppose a painter creates an abstract canvas consisting of a single unbroken field of deep blue, without any discernible parts standing in proportional relations. There are no columns, no themes, no features whose ratios might be measured. Yet a normally constituted observer, contemplating the canvas, experiences a profound pleasurable response, a sense of depth, tranquillity, and beauty that she cannot attribute to any proportional relation, for the painting has no parts to be proportioned. The canvas is beautiful, but its beauty does not arise from proportional harmony among parts.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is the presence of multiple distinguishable parts whose relations produce the pleasing effect. The inductive generalisation to proportional harmony as the basis of beauty is defeated by objects whose beauty arises from qualities such as colour, depth, or luminosity that inhere in the whole rather than in relations among parts. Beauty may reside in the qualitative character of a unified field as readily as in the proportional structure of a composite object.
passage:
The most persuasive examples of beauty in the philosophical tradition appear to share a common structure. When we admire a Greek temple, the beauty seems bound up with the mathematical ratios governing its columns and entablature. When we judge a sonata beautiful, we attend to the balance and symmetry of its formal structure. When we regard a human face as beautiful, proportional relations among the features play a decisive role. These cases invite the generalisation that beauty consists in proportional harmony among the parts of an object, a harmony that produces a pleasurable response in a normally constituted observer.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the contemplation of certain abstract paintings. Suppose a painter creates a canvas consisting of a single unbroken field of deep blue, without any discernible parts standing in proportional relations to one another. There are no columns to be spaced, no themes to be balanced, no features whose ratios might be computed. Yet a normally constituted observer, contemplating the canvas, experiences a profound pleasurable response: a sense of depth, tranquillity, and beauty that she cannot attribute to any proportional relation, for the painting presents no parts to be proportioned. It appears that the background cases, by featuring objects with multiple distinguishable components, encouraged an inductive generalisation that is too narrow. Beauty may reside in the qualitative character of a unified field, in the luminosity of a single colour or the depth of an unbroken surface, as readily as in the proportional structure of a composite object. The definition, abstracted from cases of structural beauty, cannot accommodate the beauty of the structureless.

background_cases: When Constable painted The Hay Wain, the painting's representational success rested upon the visual resemblance between the painted scene and the actual landscape of the Stour valley. A viewer could identify the subject matter by comparing the shapes, colours, and spatial arrangements in the painting with those of a real English countryside.

When a portrait painter captures a sitter's likeness, the portrait represents the sitter in virtue of a perceptible similarity between the painted face and the actual face. The closer the resemblance in the arrangement of features, the more successful the representation is judged to be.

When an architectural draughtsman produces a plan of a building, the drawing represents the building by reproducing its spatial proportions at a reduced scale. The representational relation consists in a systematic correspondence between the spatial relations in the drawing and those in the actual structure.
definition: A work W represents a subject S if and only if (i) W bears a perceptible visual resemblance to S, (ii) the resemblance is intended by the maker of W, and (iii) a competent viewer can identify S by attending to the visual properties of W.
conditions: W bears a perceptible visual resemblance to S
The resemblance is intended by the maker of W
A competent viewer can identify S by attending to the visual properties of W
counterexample: Suppose a composer writes a piece of programme music intended to represent a thunderstorm. The music contains no visual properties and bears no visual resemblance to a thunderstorm. Yet through the use of timpani rolls, crashing chords, and chromatic runs, the piece represents the thunderstorm in a manner that competent listeners recognise without difficulty. The maker intends the representation, and the audience identifies the subject. The second condition is satisfied, but the first and third conditions, which demand visual resemblance and visual identification, are not met. Yet the music undeniably represents a thunderstorm.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they all involve visual media, and the inductive generalisation to visual resemblance as the basis of representation is an artefact of restricting attention to painting and drawing. Representation may operate through auditory, temporal, or structural correspondence as readily as through visual likeness, and a definition abstracted from visual cases alone will inevitably fail when applied to non-visual arts.
passage:
The most familiar examples of artistic representation share a conspicuous feature: the work visually resembles its subject. When Constable painted The Hay Wain, the representational success rested upon visual similarity between the painted scene and the actual landscape. When a portrait painter captures a likeness, the portrait represents the sitter through perceptible resemblance of features. When an architectural draughtsman produces a plan, the drawing represents the building through systematic spatial correspondence. These cases invite the generalisation that representation consists in intended visual resemblance that enables a competent viewer to identify the subject.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the art of music. Suppose a composer writes a piece of programme music intended to represent a thunderstorm. The music contains no visual properties whatsoever and bears no visual resemblance to a thunderstorm. Yet through the use of timpani rolls that suggest the rumble of thunder, crashing chords that evoke lightning, and chromatic runs that mimic the rush of wind and rain, the piece represents the thunderstorm in a manner that competent listeners recognise without difficulty. The maker intends the representation, and the audience identifies the subject with confidence. But the identification proceeds through auditory rather than visual means, and the resemblance, if one may speak of resemblance at all, is a resemblance of dynamic pattern and temporal structure rather than of visual appearance. The background cases, by featuring exclusively visual media, encouraged an inductive generalisation that is too narrow. Representation may operate through auditory, temporal, or structural correspondence as readily as through visual likeness.

background_cases: When travellers in the eighteenth century described the experience of crossing the Alps, they consistently reported a mixture of terror and exaltation occasioned by the vast and threatening character of the mountains. The sublimity of the experience was bound up with the physical danger and the immensity of the natural scene.

When Burke analysed the sublime, he drew his examples from experiences of darkness, vastness, power, and obscurity, all of which produce a degree of terror in the observer. The experience of the sublime, on his account, is inseparable from the apprehension of something that threatens the self.

When observers describe the sublimity of a violent ocean storm viewed from a safe distance, the experience combines the awareness of destructive power with the security of the observer's position. The sublimity is occasioned by the magnitude and violence of the natural phenomenon.
definition: An experience E is sublime if and only if (i) E is occasioned by an object or phenomenon of overwhelming magnitude, power, or obscurity, (ii) E involves a component of terror or awe arising from the perceived threat to the self, and (iii) the terror or awe is accompanied by a sense of exaltation or elevation that arises from the subject's safe contemplation of the threatening object.
conditions: E is occasioned by an object of overwhelming magnitude, power, or obscurity
E involves a component of terror or awe from a perceived threat to the self
The terror or awe is accompanied by exaltation from safe contemplation of the threatening object
counterexample: Suppose a mathematician, working alone at her desk, arrives at the proof of a theorem that reveals an unexpected connexion between two distant branches of mathematics. She experiences a sudden sense of the infinite, an overwhelming apprehension of the vastness and depth of mathematical structure. The experience is one of awe and exaltation. Yet no object of physical magnitude or power is present; no threat to the self is perceived; and the exaltation arises not from the safe contemplation of danger but from the intellectual apprehension of a reality that exceeds the mind's ordinary compass. The experience is, by any ordinary reckoning, sublime, yet none of the three conditions is satisfied.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are all drawn from encounters with physically imposing natural phenomena. The inductive generalisation to magnitude, terror, and safe contemplation as the conditions of sublimity is an artefact of restricting attention to natural scenery. The sublime may arise in purely intellectual contexts where the mind apprehends a depth or vastness that exceeds its ordinary grasp, without any physical threat, magnitude, or sensory overwhelm.
passage:
The most celebrated accounts of the sublime draw their evidence from encounters with physically imposing natural phenomena. Eighteenth-century travellers crossing the Alps reported a mixture of terror and exaltation occasioned by the vast and threatening mountains. Burke analysed the sublime through examples of darkness, vastness, and power, all of which produce terror. Observers of violent ocean storms describe an experience combining awareness of destructive force with the security of a safe vantage. These cases invite the generalisation that the sublime is an experience occasioned by overwhelming magnitude or power, involving terror or awe from a perceived threat, accompanied by exaltation arising from safe contemplation.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the life of the intellect. Suppose a mathematician, working alone at her desk, arrives at the proof of a theorem that reveals an unexpected connexion between two remote branches of her discipline. She experiences a sudden and overwhelming apprehension of the vastness and depth of mathematical structure, a sense of the infinite that exceeds the mind's ordinary compass. The experience is one of profound awe and exaltation. Yet no object of physical magnitude or power is present; no threat to the self is perceived; and the exaltation arises not from the safe contemplation of danger but from the intellectual apprehension of a reality that surpasses comprehension. The experience is, by any ordinary reckoning, sublime. It appears that the background cases, by featuring exclusively natural and physical objects, encouraged a generalisation that is too narrow. The sublime may arise in purely intellectual contexts, where the mind encounters a depth that exceeds its ordinary grasp, without any element of physical danger, sensory overwhelm, or threatening magnitude.

background_cases: When a Renaissance painter completed an altarpiece, the work was valued for the skill with which the painter manipulated pigment to produce an illusion of depth, light, and human form. The craftsmanship was inseparable from the artistic achievement, and a painting that exhibited poor technique was judged an artistic failure regardless of its subject matter.

When a master cabinetmaker produced a piece of furniture that combined functional excellence with ornamental beauty, the craftsmanship of the joinery, the selection of wood, and the precision of the carving were regarded as essential to the object's status as a work of decorative art.

When a poet composed a sonnet, the work was judged in part by the skill with which the poet handled metre, rhyme, and the formal constraints of the sonnet form. Technical mastery of the medium was a necessary condition of poetic excellence.
definition: An object O is a work of art if and only if (i) O is the product of skilled human craftsmanship in a recognised medium, (ii) the craftsmanship exhibited in O is directed towards the achievement of aesthetic excellence, and (iii) the aesthetic value of O is inseparable from the technical skill with which it was made.
conditions: O is the product of skilled human craftsmanship in a recognised medium
The craftsmanship is directed towards the achievement of aesthetic excellence
The aesthetic value of O is inseparable from the technical skill with which it was made
counterexample: Suppose an artist selects a mass-produced porcelain urinal from a plumbing supplier, signs it with a pseudonym, and submits it to an art exhibition, where it is displayed on a pedestal. The object is not the product of the artist's skilled craftsmanship; it is a factory-made commodity. The aesthetic value, if any, does not reside in technical skill, for no skill was exercised in its manufacture by the exhibitor. Yet the object is widely discussed as a work of art, provoking debate about the nature of art itself. Its artistic significance lies in the conceptual gesture of selection and recontextualisation rather than in craftsmanship.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the artistic achievement was bound up with the maker's technical mastery of a medium. The inductive generalisation to craftsmanship as a necessary condition of art is defeated by works whose artistic significance resides in the act of selection, recontextualisation, or conceptual provocation rather than in any exercise of manual skill. The definition, abstracted from traditional craft-based arts, cannot accommodate works in which the idea is the artwork.
passage:
The most venerable examples of artistic achievement share a conspicuous feature: the work's value is bound up with the maker's technical mastery. A Renaissance altarpiece was valued for the painter's skill in producing an illusion of depth and light. A master cabinetmaker's furniture was admired for the precision of the joinery. A poet's sonnet was judged by the handling of metre and rhyme. These cases invite the generalisation that a work of art is the product of skilled craftsmanship directed towards aesthetic excellence, and that its aesthetic value is inseparable from the technical skill with which it was made.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be imagined from modern exhibition practice. Suppose an artist selects a mass-produced porcelain urinal from a plumbing supplier, signs it with a pseudonym, and submits it to an art exhibition, where it is displayed upon a pedestal. The object is not the product of the artist's skilled craftsmanship; it is a factory-made commodity. Whatever aesthetic value it possesses does not reside in technical skill, for no skill beyond the act of selection was exercised by the exhibitor. Yet the object is widely discussed as a work of art, provoking sustained debate about the boundaries of art itself. Its artistic significance lies in the conceptual gesture of choosing an ordinary object and recontextualising it within an institutional setting. The background cases, by featuring traditional craft-based arts, encouraged an inductive generalisation that is too narrow. The definition cannot accommodate works in which the artistic achievement consists in an idea, a gesture of selection, or an act of institutional recontextualisation rather than in any exercise of manual skill.

background_cases: A rose garden in full bloom strikes observers as beautiful: the colours are vivid, the forms symmetrical, and the fragrance pleasant, producing in each visitor a serene and disinterested delight.
A sunset over the sea is widely judged beautiful: the gradations of colour are harmonious, the scale is vast without being threatening, and the experience is one of quiet, selfless absorption.

A marble sculpture of the human figure is regarded as beautiful: its proportions are balanced, its surface invites contemplation, and the viewer's pleasure is free from any desire to possess or use the object.
definition: An object is beautiful if and only if it exhibits harmonious form, engages the senses pleasantly, and produces a disinterested delight in the observer.
conditions: The object exhibits harmonious form
The object engages the senses pleasantly
The object produces a disinterested delight in the observer
counterexample: Suppose one contemplates a weathered cliff face, deeply scored by centuries of erosion, its surfaces jagged and asymmetrical, its colours muted and austere. It does not exhibit harmonious form in any obvious sense; its lines are fractured and irregular. It does not engage the senses pleasantly — the rock is rough, the wind cold, the palette grey. Yet the observer experiences a disinterested delight of a peculiar kind: a feeling of awe at the sheer indifference of geological time. Many would call the cliff beautiful, yet only the third condition is satisfied. The case reveals that the inductive generalisation from paradigmatic instances of beauty — gardens, sunsets, sculptures — has produced a definition too closely tied to the sensory agreeableness of those particular cases.
abductive_insight: The background cases, being drawn from objects that are sensorily agreeable and formally harmonious, yield an inductively plausible but overly narrow definition. The cliff face reveals that beauty may arise from qualities antithetical to harmony and pleasant sensation, such as ruggedness, austerity, and the sublimity of geological indifference, and that the generalisation fails when extended beyond its evidential base.
passage:
If one surveys a range of objects commonly judged beautiful — a rose garden, a sunset, a classical sculpture — one may be led by induction to the view that beauty consists in harmonious form, pleasant sensory engagement, and the production of a disinterested delight. Each of these cases exhibits a certain formal balance, a quality that pleases the eye or the nose or the hand, and a character that invites contemplation untainted by practical desire. The inference from these cases to a general definition is natural enough: one identifies the features common to the paradigms and elevates them to the status of necessary conditions.

Yet one should hesitate before endorsing the generalisation. Suppose one stands before a weathered cliff face, deeply scored by centuries of erosion, its surfaces jagged and irregular, its lines fractured rather than flowing. The colours are austere — greys and dull browns — and the wind that sweeps across the rock is cold and uninviting. The senses are not pleasantly engaged; the form is not harmonious. Yet an observer may experience, before this cliff, a disinterested delight of a distinctive kind: a feeling of awe at the vast indifference of geological time, a recognition of something that commands attention without offering comfort. Many such observers would not hesitate to describe the cliff as beautiful. The case reveals that the definition, though plausible when tested against its evidential base, is insufficiently general. The background cases — gardens, sunsets, sculptures — share a sensory agreeableness and a formal balance that are not, as it turns out, necessary to beauty. The inductive inference has been led astray by the homogeneity of the sample from which it was drawn.

background_cases: A museum visitor stands before a large abstract painting and loses awareness of her surroundings, attending solely to the interplay of colour and form; she values the experience for its own sake and is moved by it.
A concert-goer listens to a string quartet and becomes wholly absorbed in the unfolding of melodic lines; the experience is valued intrinsically and is accompanied by a feeling of deep satisfaction.
A reader, immersed in a poem, attends closely to its rhythms and imagery, finding the experience valuable in itself and experiencing a quiet elation.
definition: An experience is aesthetic if and only if it involves absorbed attention to the sensory or formal qualities of an object, it is valued intrinsically, and it is accompanied by a positive affective response.
conditions: The experience involves absorbed attention to the sensory or formal qualities of an object
The experience is valued intrinsically
The experience is accompanied by a positive affective response
counterexample: Suppose one watches a stark and harrowing theatrical performance depicting human suffering. One attends with complete absorption to the formal qualities of the staging — the lighting, the spatial arrangement, the cadence of the dialogue — and values the experience for its own sake, not for any practical benefit. Yet one's affective response is not positive: one feels distress, discomfort, and a painful recognition of cruelty. The third condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, one would readily classify the experience as aesthetic. The inductive generalisation from pleasant paradigm cases has excluded the possibility of aesthetic experiences grounded in negative affect.
abductive_insight: The background cases — a painting, a quartet, a poem — all happen to produce positive affect, and the abductive inference treats this as a necessary condition. But the theatrical case reveals that aesthetic experience may be constituted in part by negative affect, and that the generalisation has mistakenly elevated a contingent feature of the sample to the status of a requirement.
passage:
From a range of cases — absorbed contemplation of an abstract painting, rapt attention to a string quartet, immersion in a poem — one might infer that aesthetic experience consists in absorbed attention to sensory or formal qualities, intrinsic valuation, and a positive affective response. In each case, the subject attends closely to the perceptual features of an object, values the experience for its own sake, and is moved in a manner that feels agreeable or elevating. The inference to a general definition seems warranted: these are paradigmatic instances, and the three conditions appear to capture their shared structure.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this generalisation. Suppose one attends a stark and harrowing theatrical performance. The staging is austere: harsh lighting, sparse furnishings, an arrangement of bodies in space that communicates desolation. One attends with complete absorption to these formal qualities and to the cadence of the dialogue. One values the experience for its own sake — one would not wish it otherwise, and one recognises it as important. But one's affective response is decidedly not positive. One feels distress, discomfort, even a painful recognition of the cruelty depicted. The experience is dark and unsettling, and it remains so after the performance concludes. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not. Nevertheless, one would classify the experience as aesthetic without hesitation. The case suggests that the positive character of the affect in the background cases was a contingent feature rather than a necessary one. The abductive inference, drawn from agreeable paradigms, has mistakenly treated positive affect as essential to aesthetic experience.

background_cases: A portrait of a duke executed in oils clearly represents its subject: it resembles him in features, colouring, and posture, and viewers immediately recognise the duke.
A landscape painting of the Lake District represents those hills and waters: its forms correspond to the visible features of the scene, and viewers familiar with the locale identify it at once.
A still life of fruit on a table represents those objects: the shapes, colours, and textures on the canvas match those of actual fruit, and any competent viewer sees fruit in the painting.
definition: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object in relevant respects, competent viewers recognise the object in the painting, and the painter intended to depict that object.
conditions: The painting visually resembles the object in relevant respects
Competent viewers recognise the object in the painting
The painter intended to depict that object
counterexample: Suppose a painter in the cubist tradition produces a painting of a guitar. The canvas presents fragmented geometric planes, overlapping facets, and multiple simultaneous perspectives. The painting does not visually resemble a guitar in any straightforward sense — one cannot identify the familiar shape, colour, or texture of a guitar by simple visual comparison. Yet competent viewers, acquainted with the conventions of cubism, recognise a guitar in the painting, and the painter intended to depict one. The first condition fails, but the painting nonetheless represents a guitar. The inductive inference from portraits, landscapes, and still lifes — all executed in broadly realist idioms — has yielded a definition too closely tied to visual resemblance.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve paintings in a realist tradition where visual resemblance is prominent, and the abductive generalisation treats this as necessary. The cubist guitar reveals that representation can operate through convention, schematisation, and viewer competence without visual resemblance, exposing the limits of generalising from a stylistically homogeneous sample.
passage:
If one considers a range of paintings that uncontroversially represent their subjects — a portrait of a duke, a landscape of the Lake District, a still life of fruit — one may be led to the view that pictorial representation requires visual resemblance, viewer recognition, and authorial intention. In each case, the forms on the canvas correspond to the visible features of the depicted object, viewers identify the subject without difficulty, and the painter set out to depict it. The generalisation to a definition of representation is natural: these three conditions capture what the cases share.

Yet the generalisation proves too narrow when applied beyond its evidential base. Suppose one encounters a cubist painting of a guitar. The canvas presents an array of fragmented geometric planes, overlapping facets, and simultaneous perspectives that bear no straightforward visual resemblance to a guitar as it appears to ordinary perception. One cannot identify the familiar silhouette, the warm colour of wood, or the regular pattern of strings by simple visual comparison. The first condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet competent viewers, acquainted with the conventions of cubist painting, recognise a guitar in the image. They understand the fragmentation as a mode of representation rather than as an obstacle to it. The painter, moreover, intended to depict a guitar. The second and third conditions are met. The painting represents a guitar by any reasonable standard. We are thus compelled to recognise that the definition, inferred from cases of realist painting, has mistakenly treated visual resemblance as a necessary condition. Representation may proceed by convention, schematisation, and acquired competence, without resemblance in the sense the definition requires.
